```mermaid
    S5 --> S6[Calculate VRR]
    S6 --> S7[Continuous Kaizen Loop]
    S7 --> S2
```

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams follow the **KTU Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** specification (V10.0). Node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes (`nodeA`, `blkW`, etc.) to avoid Mermaid reserved-keyword conflicts.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

Define **Concurrent Engineering** and list any four of its key advantages over traditional sequential product development.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Concurrent Engineering (CE) is an integrated product development approach in which design, manufacturing, quality, marketing, and service teams collaborate **in parallel from the earliest concept stage**, rather than performing their tasks sequentially.

**Four advantages:**

1. **Reduced Time-to-Market** — parallel workflows cut total lead time by 30–70%.
2. **Lower Life-Cycle Cost** — DFM issues caught early prevent expensive rework.
3. **Improved Quality** — cross-functional reviews reduce DFMEA risk scores.
4. **Enhanced Customer Focus** — voice-of-customer flows continuously, not at handover.

(Each point = ½ mark; clean definition = 1 mark. *Total: 3 marks*)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

List the **four categories of quality costs** in any manufacturing organization with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Category | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Prevention Cost | Avoid defects | Training, design reviews |
| Appraisal Cost | Detect defects | Inspection, testing |
| Internal Failure Cost | Defects found in-house | Rework, scrap |
| External Failure Cost | Defects found by customer | Warranty, recall |

(Mention cost categories with examples: **½ mark × 4 = 2 marks** + clean listing = 1 mark.)

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **objectives of product design** in detail. Discuss how **quality and cost** are balanced during the design stage using Taguchi's quality loss function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of **human factors in design**. Explain any two ergonomic principles with suitable industrial examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Objectives of Product Design + Taguchi Loss Function (7 marks):**

The objectives of product design are:

1. **Customer Satisfaction:** Functional performance + emotional appeal.
2. **Economic Viability:** Cost-to-market match.
3. **Manufacturability:** Ease of fabrication and assembly (DFMA).
4. **Reliability & Maintainability:** MTBF, MTTR optimization.
5. **Safety & Regulatory Compliance:** ISO, BIS, CE, RoHS.
6. **Sustainability:** LCA, recyclability.

**Taguchi Quality Loss Function:**

Taguchi showed that any deviation $\Delta y$ from target $m$ causes a *societal loss*:

$$
L(y) \;=\; k\,(y-m)^2 \;\;\text{with}\;\; k \;=\; \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta^2}
$$

- $\Delta$ = tolerance limit.
- $A_0$ = cost of replacement when at boundary.

**Design implication:** Engineering tolerance should be set so that loss across the production spread is minimized. **Robust design** seeks to reduce variance before tightening tolerance, since tightening alone multiplies scrap cost without removing inherent variability.

$$
\boxed{\text{Design Rule: minimise } L(y) \text{ by reducing variance, not just tightening tolerance.}}
$$

(Marks: 4 for objectives listing, 3 for Taguchi derivation + insight.)

**(b) Human Factors in Design (7 marks):**

Human Factors (Ergonomics) integrates *human capabilities and limitations* into product/process design.

**Two principles:**

**Principle 1 — Anthropometric Fit:**
Design must accommodate the **5th–95th percentile** of the user population. Example: the steering wheel reach in Indian cars must suit drivers from 150 cm (5th percentile Indian female) to 185 cm (95th percentile Indian male). Peugeot's "i-Cockpit" used percentile-cluster analysis to position its small-diameter steering wheel so that *all* drivers could see the dials.

**Principle 2 — Cognitive Load Reduction:**
Controls should follow the **pop-out principle** (frequent controls in pop-out colours). Example: emergency stop buttons are **RED** and **mushroom-shaped** because red has the longest wavelength and dominates visual field — detectable in <250 ms even under stress.

A third example (optional): the **NIOSH Lifting Equation** — used to design workstations where workers must lift loads, capping them at 23 kg for optimal coupling under standard conditions.

(Marks: 2 for definition + 5 for two principles with examples.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the concepts of **standardization, simplification, and variety reduction** in product design. How does each contribute to cost reduction in manufacturing? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **concurrent engineering** philosophy in detail. Compare it with the traditional sequential (over-the-wall) approach using a suitable product example (e.g., a smartphone). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Standardization, Simplification & Variety Reduction (7 marks):**

**Standardization:** Use of *preferred* and *interchangeable* components from accepted norms (ISO, BIS). It reduces:
- Tooling inventory
- Training cost
- Procurement complexity

**Simplification:** Reduction in number of parts, operations, and materials. Achieved via:
- Part consolidation (Boothroyd analysis)
- Process consolidation (multi-axis CNC replacing 3 single-axis ops)
- Tolerance relaxation where functionally allowed

**Variety Reduction:** Elimination of *redundant* SKUs. The Burbidge Production Variety Effect (B-P effect) shows that as cumulative production grows, unit time/cost drops — but increasing variety *shatters* this curve by forcing frequent setups.

**Cost Impact (Formula):**

$$
\text{Setup Cost} \;=\; \dfrac{S}{Q}, \quad \text{where } S=\text{setup cost/run},\; Q=\text{batch size}
$$

Variety $\uparrow$ ⇒ $Q \downarrow$ ⇒ setup cost/unit $\uparrow$. Hence reducing variety *lowers* unit cost by enlarging effective batch size.

(Marks: 2 each for three concepts + 1 for B-P effect/formula.)

**(b) Concurrent Engineering vs Sequential (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Sequential Engineering | Concurrent Engineering |
|---|---|---|
| Process | Linear, "over-the-wall" | Parallel, cross-functional |
| Time | Long (sum of all phases) | Short (overlapping) |
| Cost of change | High (late discovery) | Low (caught in design) |
| Communication | One-way handover | Bi-directional, real-time |
| Customer focus | Captured at the start | Continuous |

**Smartphone Example:**
- *Sequential:* Marketing finalizes specs → R&D designs → Tooling waits → Manufacturing builds → Sales launches. Any colour-change request post-launch requires tool re-grinding → 6-month delay.
- *Concurrent:* Marketing, R&D, Tooling, Manufacturing, and Quality sit in a single PDLC room from Day 1. Material choices (e.g., glass back vs polycarbonate) are debated with the manufacturing engineer, so design-for-injection is built-in. Time-to-market drops from 18 months to 9 months; warranty claims drop by ~40%.

(Marks: 4 for comparison table + 3 for smartphone example.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing *Standardization* with *Variety Reduction*** — Standardization uses *existing norms*; variety reduction eliminates *redundant variants*. Examiners award partial credit only.
> 2. **Skipping the formula derivation** in Taguchi Loss Function — merely writing $L(y)=k(y-m)^2$ without deriving $k = A_0/\Delta^2$ costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Writing "Concurrent = simultaneous"** without mentioning *integration of design + manufacture + quality + customer* in one workflow.
> 4. **Using "manufacturing" and "production" interchangeably** — KTU board expects distinction: *Manufacturing* = physical realization; *Production* = overall output system.
> 5. **Forgetting units** in cost and ergonomics calculations (kg, currency, hours).

---

### 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ Product Design = balancing **Function, Form, Fit, Feasibility**.
- ✅ Three Iron-Triangle constraints: **Scope, Cost, Schedule**.
- ✅ Quality cost = **Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure**.
- ✅ Taguchi Loss: $L(y) = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta^2}(y-m)^2$ — minimise *variance*, not just tolerance.
- ✅ Human Factors = Anthropometry + Biomechanics + Cognition + Sensory + Environment.
- ✅ NIOSH RWL formula with six multipliers (Frequency, Coupling, etc.).
- ✅ DFMA: target $I_p = 100\%$ via Boothroyd's three-question elimination logic.
- ✅ Standardization uses *existing norms*; Variety Reduction eliminates *redundant* SKUs.
- ✅ B-P Effect: cumulative production $\uparrow$ ⇒ unit cost $\downarrow$ (slope = $b = \log(\text{LR})/\log 2$).
- ✅ Concurrent Engineering = **parallel, cross-functional integration** from concept stage.
- ✅ Sequential = "over-the-wall"; CE = integrated team rooms, shared databases, DFMEA co-owned.
- ✅ Prototyping types: **Throwaway, Evolutionary, Incremental** — pick by purpose, not cost.
- ✅ KTU CO1 (Remember/Understand), CO2 (Apply quality-cost), CO3 (Apply HFE), CO4 (Analyze DFMA), CO5 (Evaluate CE).
- ✅ Always carry **units**; use $\vert x \vert$ not `|x|` in markdown tables.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plant Layout and Material Handling: Types of Plant Layout, Principles of Material Handling, Material Handling Equipment, Types, Selection, and Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET601 - INDUSTRIAL AND SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Plant Layout and Material Handling: Types of Plant Layout, Principles of Material Handling, Material Handling Equipment, Types, Selection, and Application

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Plant Layout — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plant Layout** is the physical arrangement of industrial facilities, machines, equipment, workstations, storage areas, material handling systems, and service departments in the most effective and economical manner, to facilitate the smooth, uninterrupted, and economical flow of materials, work-in-progress, and finished goods through the production system, while ensuring optimum utilization of manpower, space, equipment, and resources.

In **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology, Plant Layout is a **strategic industrial engineering decision** that directly determines:
- The efficiency of the conversion process (Input $\rightarrow$ Process $\rightarrow$ Output)
- The total material handling cost (often **30–70%** of total production cost)
- The flexibility and responsiveness of the system
- The safety and welfare of workers

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Kitchen as a Mini-Factory**
> Imagine your kitchen as a small production unit. Where do you keep the refrigerator (raw material store)? Where do you chop vegetables (first operation)? Where is the stove (second operation)? Where is the serving plate (assembly/dispatch)? A well-organized kitchen places items **in the order you use them**, just like a Product Layout. A disorganized kitchen, where you walk back and forth between fridge, stove, and sink repeatedly, is like a **poor Process Layout** — chaotic, time-wasting, and frustrating. Plant Layout engineering is essentially the science of designing your "factory kitchen" so that the chef (worker) never wastes a step.

### 1.3 Material Handling — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material Handling (MH)** is the art, science, and engineering of moving, storing, protecting, and controlling materials throughout the entire manufacturing, warehousing, distribution, consumption, and disposal cycle, using the most efficient and economical methods possible.

Material handling is NOT just "transportation" — it encompasses:
- **Moving** materials (horizontal, vertical)
- **Storing** materials (temporary or semi-permanent)
- **Protecting** materials from damage
- **Controlling** the flow (quantity, timing, destination)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students wrongly equate Material Handling *only* with transportation. KTU valuation keys award marks for the **broader scope** — including storage, control, and protection. State the full definition to secure full marks.

### 1.4 Why Plant Layout & Material Handling Matter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Statistics (Mandatory for KTU Answers):**
> - Material handling adds **no value** to the product but consumes **15–70%** of manufacturing cost.
> - Poor layout increases **backtracking, delays, accidents, and WIP inventory**.
> - An optimal layout can reduce MH cost by **up to 30%** and increase productivity by **20–40%**.
> - **Rule of Thumb:** In a factory, the **best layout is the one that minimizes the sum of distances travelled by all materials** in a given period.

---

### 1.5 Objectives of a Good Plant Layout (KTU Favourite — Learn All 8)

1. **Minimize material handling cost and distance**
2. **Ensure smooth and uninterrupted flow of work**
3. **Provide adequate space** for men, machines, and materials
4. **Ensure safety** and improve worker morale
5. **Allow flexibility** for future expansion or product changes
6. **Promote effective supervision** and communication
7. **Reduce WIP (Work-in-Progress) inventory**
8. **Facilitate easy maintenance** of machines and equipment
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Plant Layout — The Four Classic + Two Modern Types

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to know **at minimum 4 types** in detail, with comparison tables. Examiners award marks for both **diagrammatic representation** and **tabular comparison**.

#### **Type 1: Product Layout (Line Layout / Continuous Layout)**

In a product layout, machines and workstations are arranged **in a sequence** according to the operations required to manufacture the product.

**Characteristics:**
- Machines arranged as per the **sequence of operations**
- Highly suitable for **continuous, mass production**
- Material flows in a **straight line** (or with minor deviations)
- Specialized equipment is used
- One product (or similar product family) is produced in high volume

**Advantages:**
- Smooth and continuous flow of work
- Reduced material handling
- Lower WIP inventory
- Less skill required per worker
- Easier production planning and control
- Reduced production time per unit

**Limitations:**
- **High initial investment** (specialized machines)
- **Low flexibility** — layout becomes obsolete if product changes
- **Bottleneck machine** controls the output of the entire line
- Monotonous work leads to **worker fatigue and boredom**
- Requires **high, consistent demand** to be economical

**Examples:** Automobile assembly (Maruti, Tata), FMCG bottling plants, washing machine assembly, sugar mills.

#### **Type 2: Process Layout (Functional Layout / Job-Shop Layout)**

In a process layout, similar machines or processes are **grouped together** in dedicated departments (e.g., all milling machines in one section, all lathes in another).

**Characteristics:**
- Machines grouped by **function/process**, not by product
- Suitable for **job production and batch production**
- Each order has its own **unique routing**
- **General-purpose machines** are used
- Skilled workers required

**Advantages:**
- **High flexibility** — can handle variety of products
- Better **utilization of machines** (lower idle time)
- Easier **supervision** by functional experts
- **Lower initial investment** (general-purpose machines)
- Failure of one machine does not stop the entire plant

**Limitations:**
- **Complex material flow** — back-and-forth movement
- **Higher WIP inventory** and longer lead time
- Difficult **production planning and control**
- Requires **highly skilled workers**
- Higher material handling cost

**Examples:** Tool rooms, heavy machinery manufacturing, custom furniture shops, hospitals.

#### **Type 3: Fixed Position Layout (Site Layout / Project Layout)**

In a fixed position layout, the **product remains stationary** at one location, while all machines, tools, materials, and workers are brought to it.

**Characteristics:**
- Product is **too large, heavy, or immobile** to move
- Used for **one-of-a-kind, large projects**
- Material moves **to the product**, not vice versa
- Requires careful coordination of resources

**Advantages:**
- Eliminates movement of the bulky product
- Reduces risk of damage to the product
- Workers and machines come to the most convenient location

**Limitations:**
- **Very high coordination cost**
- Material handling of components to the site is difficult
- Skilled workers must travel frequently
- Limited workspace around the product

**Examples:** Shipbuilding, aircraft assembly, construction of bridges, locomotives, large boilers.

#### **Type 4: Cellular Manufacturing Layout (Group Layout / Group Technology)**

A hybrid layout where machines are grouped into **cells**, each cell dedicated to producing a **family of similar parts** (parts classification based on Group Technology — GT).

**Characteristics:**
- Machines grouped into **U-shaped or linear cells**
- Each cell produces a **part family** (parts with similar processing steps)
- Combines advantages of both process and product layout
- Uses **Group Technology (GT)** coding systems

**Advantages:**
- Reduced setup time and material handling
- Lower WIP, faster throughput
- Worker empowerment (each cell is semi-autonomous)
- Easier to automate

**Limitations:**
- Requires detailed **parts classification**
- Re-balancing the cell is complex when demand changes
- Initial effort in GT implementation is high

**Examples:** CNC machining shops producing shaft family, gear family, etc.

#### **Type 5 & 6: Combined Layout (Hybrid) and Project Layout**

Most real-world factories use a **combination** of the above types — e.g., a process layout in the machine shop and a product layout in the assembly area.

---

### 2.2 Comparison Table — Layout Types (KTU Most-Asked)

| **Parameter** | **Product Layout** | **Process Layout** | **Fixed Position** | **Cellular Layout** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Production Type** | Mass / Continuous | Job / Batch | Project | Batch (Part Family) |
| **Flow Pattern** | Straight line | Irregular / criss-cross | Stationary product | U-shape / Loop |
| **Machine Type** | Special-purpose | General-purpose | Brought to site | Group of general-purpose |
| **Flexibility** | Very Low | Very High | Low (one project) | Moderate |
| **WIP Inventory** | Low | High | Very High | Low |
| **MH Cost** | Low | High | Very High | Low |
| **Skill Level** | Low | High | High | Moderate |
| **Capital** | Very High | Moderate | Very High | Moderate |
| **Supervision** | Easy | Difficult | Very Difficult | Moderate |

### 2.3 The 10 Principles of Material Handling (Apple's Principles — KTU Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Apple's 10 Principles of Material Handling** is a **guaranteed KTU question** (either 3 marks or 14 marks). Memorize all 10 with one-line explanation each.

| **#** | **Principle** | **Core Idea** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Planning Principle** | Plan all material handling activities before implementation. |
| 2 | **Standardization Principle** | Standardize MH methods, equipment, and controls. |
| 3 | **Work Principle** | Provide for safe working conditions. |
| 4 | **Ergonomic Principle** | Recognize human capabilities and limitations. |
| 5 | **Unit Load Principle** | Handle materials in largest possible unit loads. |
| 6 | **Space Utilization Principle** | Make effective use of cubic space. |
| 7 | **System Principle** | Integrate MH activities into a coordinated system. |
| 8 | **Automation Principle** | Automate MH operations where feasible. |
| 9 | **Environmental Principle** | Conserve energy and minimize environmental impact. |
| 10 | **Life Cycle Principle** | Consider total cost of ownership (acquisition + operation + maintenance). |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **mathematically verifiable** and frequently used in KTU numerical problems on plant layout optimization.

| **#** | **Formula / Metric** | **Expression** | **Unit** | **Used For** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Material Flow Cost (Total MH Cost)** | $C_{MH} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i \times f_i \times c_i$ | ₹/month | Total cost of material movement |
| 2 | **Centre of Gravity (X-coordinate)** | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$ | meters | Optimal location of single facility |
| 3 | **Centre of Gravity (Y-coordinate)** | $\bar{Y} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$ | meters | Optimal location of single facility |
| 4 | **Load Distance (Load x Distance)** | $L \times D = \sum (loads \times distances)$ | ton-m | Minimization objective |
| 5 | **Material Handling Intensity** | $MHI = \dfrac{MH\ cost}{Total\ production\ cost}$ | ratio | Layout efficiency indicator |
| 6 | **Floor Space Utilization Index** | $FSUI = \dfrac{Useful\ Area}{Total\ Floor\ Area}$ | ratio | Space utilization efficiency |
| 7 | **Equipment Utilization** | $U_e = \dfrac{Operating\ time}{Available\ time} \times 100$ | % | Performance measure |
| 8 | **Throughput Time** | $T_{throughput} = Processing\ Time + Queue\ Time + Move\ Time$ | hours | Total cycle time |

**Where:**
- $d_i$ = distance travelled by item $i$
- $f_i$ = frequency of movement per month
- $c_i$ = cost per unit distance per item $i$
- $w_i$ = weight / load handled at point $i$
- $x_i, y_i$ = coordinates of point $i$
- $n$ = number of points

### 2.5 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

> [!NOTE]
> **Where these formulas are used in industry:**
> - **Centre of Gravity Method:** Locating a new warehouse, dispatch dock, or service facility in a supply chain.
> - **Load × Distance Method:** Comparing alternative layouts in a manufacturing plant (lower $L \times D$ = better layout).
> - **MHI Ratio:** Used as a key KPI in TPM (Total Productive Maintenance) and Lean Manufacturing audits.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Centre of Gravity Method (Load Distance Minimization)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Centre of Gravity Method** is the most frequently asked numerical in KTU Module 1. Derive it step-by-step.

**Problem Context:**
Suppose there are $n$ existing facilities (departments, warehouses, customer locations) with coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$ and corresponding loads $w_i$ (tonnes moved per month). We need to find the **optimal location** $(\bar{X}, \bar{Y})$ of a new facility such that the total weighted distance is minimized.

**Step 1 — Define the Objective Function**

Let the new facility be located at $(X, Y)$. The weighted distance from existing point $i$ to the new facility is:

$$
D_i = w_i \times \sqrt{(X - x_i)^2 + (Y - y_i)^2}
$$

The total weighted distance (our minimization objective) is:

$$
Z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \sqrt{(X - x_i)^2 + (Y - y_i)^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Minimize the Objective Function**

To minimize $Z$, take the **partial derivative** with respect to $X$ and set it to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial Z}{\partial X} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \frac{(X - x_i)}{\sqrt{(X - x_i)^2 + (Y - y_i)^2}} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Rectilinear (Manhattan) Distance Approximation**

For engineering simplicity (and KTU standard), we use **rectilinear distance**:

$$
D_i^{rect} = w_i \left[ \vert X - x_i \vert + \vert Y - y_i \vert \right]
$$

Minimizing this gives:

$$
Z^{rect} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \vert X - x_i \vert + \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \vert Y - y_i \vert
$$

**Step 4 — Take Partial Derivatives**

$$
\frac{\partial Z^{rect}}{\partial X} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \text{sign}(X - x_i) = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial Z^{rect}}{\partial Y} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \text{sign}(Y - y_i) = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Solve the Optimum**

The optimal location is the **weighted average** of all coordinates:

$$
\bar{X} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}
$$

$$
\bar{Y} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}
$$

**Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example**

Consider four departments with the following monthly load movements:

| **Department (i)** | **xᵢ (m)** | **yᵢ (m)** | **wᵢ (tonnes/month)** | **wᵢ · xᵢ** | **wᵢ · yᵢ** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 10 | 20 | 5 | 50 | 100 |
| B | 30 | 40 | 3 | 90 | 120 |
| C | 50 | 10 | 8 | 400 | 80 |
| D | 25 | 35 | 4 | 100 | 140 |
| **Sum** | — | — | **$\sum w_i = 20$** | **$\sum w_i x_i = 640$** | **$\sum w_i y_i = 440$** |

Now compute:

$$
\bar{X} = \frac{\sum w_i x_i}{\sum w_i} = \frac{640}{20} = 32 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\bar{Y} = \frac{\sum w_i y_i}{\sum w_i} = \frac{440}{20} = 22 \text{ m}
$$

**Interpretation:** The new facility (e.g., a dispatch dock or a material store) should be located at coordinates **(32 m, 22 m)** to minimize the total weighted material movement.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Material Handling Equipment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a **comprehensive classification** of MH equipment as per KTU 2024 syllabus. Examiners often ask: "Classify material handling equipment with examples." Use this table as the model answer.

| **Category** | **Type** | **Equipment** | **Movement** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **I. Conveyors** | Powered | Belt Conveyor | Continuous horizontal / inclined | Bulk material — coal, grain, sand |
| | Powered | Roller Conveyor | Continuous horizontal | Boxes, cartons, pallets |
| | Powered | Chain Conveyor | Continuous / intermittent | Heavy parts, engine assembly |
| | Powered | Screw Conveyor | Continuous horizontal | Granules, flour, cement |
| | Powered | Pneumatic Conveyor | Through pipelines | Grain, cement, plastic pellets |
| | Non-powered | Gravity Chute / Roller | Downward | Boxes, packages |
| **II. Cranes & Hoists** | Overhead | Bridge Crane (EOT) | 3-axis (X, Y, Z) | Heavy machine shops, steel plants |
| | Overhead | Gantry Crane | 3-axis outdoor | Shipyards, container yards |
| | Overhead | Jib Crane | 3-axis localized | Workstation-specific lifting |
| | Hoist | Chain Hoist / Wire Rope Hoist | Vertical lift only | Workshops, garages |
| **III. Industrial Trucks** | Manual | Hand Truck / Trolley | Short distance | Warehouses |
| | Powered | Forklift Truck | Horizontal + lift | Palletized goods, warehouses |
| | Powered | Pallet Jack | Short horizontal | Pallet movement |
| | Powered | Platform Truck | Horizontal | Bulk goods movement |
| | Powered | Order Picker | Horizontal + lift | E-commerce warehouses |
| **IV. Automated Systems** | AGV | Automated Guided Vehicle | Programmed path | Modern flexible manufacturing |
| | AS/RS | Automated Storage & Retrieval | 3-axis automated | Warehouses |
| | Robotic | Industrial Robot (Articulated, SCARA) | Multi-axis programmable | Assembly, welding, pick-and-place |
| **V. Lifting & Positioning** | Jack | Hydraulic Jack | Vertical lift (small) | Auto garages |
| | Elevator | Goods Lift | Vertical | Multi-storey factories |
| | Stackers | Pallet Stacker | Vertical + horizontal | Cold storage, warehouses |

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection Criteria of MH Equipment

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Discuss the factors affecting the selection of material handling equipment."* Use this structured framework in your answer.

**Step-by-Step Selection Logic:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Material Characteristics**
- **Weight per unit:** Light (<10 kg) vs. Heavy (>1000 kg)
- **Size and shape:** Regular (boxes) vs. Irregular (casting)
- **State:** Solid, Liquid, Granular, Gas
- **Fragility:** Glass vs. Steel
- **Hazardous nature:** Chemicals, hot metals

**Step 2 — Identify the Movement Requirements**
- **Distance:** Short (<30 m) vs. Long (>100 m)
- **Path:** Fixed (conveyor) vs. Variable (forklift)
- **Direction:** Horizontal, Vertical, Inclined, or Combined
- **Frequency:** Continuous vs. Intermittent

**Step 3 — Identify the Operational Conditions**
- **Production volume:** High (conveyor) vs. Low (hand truck)
- **Layout type:** Product (conveyor) vs. Process (forklift)
- **Cycle time required:** Seconds (AGV) vs. Minutes (truck)
- **Floor conditions:** Smooth vs. Rough

**Step 4 — Identify the Economic and Safety Factors**
- **Capital cost vs. Operating cost**
- **Payback period** and **Life cycle cost**
- **Safety regulations** (OSHA standards)
- **Maintenance requirements**
- **Operator skill required**
- **Noise and environmental impact**

**Step 5 — Apply the Selection Decision Tree**

| **If Material is…** | **And Distance is…** | **And Volume is…** | **Then Select…** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bulk + granular | Long + fixed | Very high | Pneumatic / Belt conveyor |
| Palletized | Long + variable | High | Forklift truck |
| Heavy + irregular | Short + 3D | Moderate | Overhead crane (EOT) |
| Small + light | Short + fixed | Very high | Belt / Roller conveyor |
| Hazardous | Any | Any | Robot or enclosed conveyor |
| Storage retrieval | Variable rack | High | AS/RS with stacker crane |

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Unit Load Principle

> [!NOTE]
> The **Unit Load Principle** (Apple's Principle #5) is the most fundamental of all MH principles. Understand its mathematical basis.

**Concept:** Combine multiple individual items into one larger load before moving.

**Mathematical Justification:**

Let there be $n$ individual items, each of weight $w$, to be moved over a distance $D$.

**Case 1: Without unit load (individual movement)**

$$
\text{Number of trips} = n, \quad \text{MH cost} = n \times C_{trip}
$$

$$
Z_1 = n \times C_{trip}
$$

**Case 2: With unit load (k items per load)**

$$
\text{Number of trips} = \frac{n}{k}, \quad \text{MH cost} = \frac{n}{k} \times C_{trip}
$$

But each trip cost is **higher** (proportional to $k^{0.7}$ empirically, due to increased effort for heavier loads). Assume $C_{trip}^{new} = C_{trip} \times k^{0.7}$:

$$
Z_2 = \frac{n}{k} \times C_{trip} \times k^{0.7} = n \times C_{trip} \times k^{-0.3}
$$

**Ratio of savings:**

$$
\frac{Z_1}{Z_2} = \frac{n \times C_{trip}}{n \times C_{trip} \times k^{-0.3}} = k^{0.3}
$$

**Conclusion:** If we form unit loads of $k = 10$ items, the cost reduces by a factor of $10^{0.3} \approx 2$ (i.e., **50% reduction in MH cost**). This mathematically justifies why **Apple's Principle #5** is considered the most important.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Classification of Plant Layout Types

```mermaid
    RootB["Product Layout"]

    RootD["Fixed Position Layout"]

    RootA --> RootC
    RootA --> RootD
    RootA --> RootE
    RootA --> RootF

    RootB --> B1["Machines in sequence of operations"]
    RootB --> B2["Mass production"]
    RootB --> B3["Example Auto assembly"]

    RootC --> C1["Machines grouped by function"]
    RootC --> C2["Job and batch production"]
    RootC --> C3["Example Tool room"]

    RootD --> D2["Project production"]
    RootD --> D3["Example Shipbuilding"]

    RootE --> E3["Example CNC cell"]
```


### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Layout Decision System

```mermaid
    classDef inputStyle fill:#FFD580,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef procStyle fill:#80C0FF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef feedbackStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Selection Logic Flowchart for MH Equipment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Start Equipment Selection"]
    Q1{"Material is bulk granular?"}
    Q2{"Heavy and irregular shape?"}
    Q3{"Long fixed path?"}
    Q4{"High automation needed?"}
    Q5{"Short distance, light load?"}

    A1["Use Belt or Pneumatic Conveyor"]
    A2["Use Overhead Bridge Crane EOT"]
    A3["Use Powered Roller Conveyor"]
    A4["Use AGV or Industrial Robot"]
    A5["Use Hand Truck or Pallet Jack"]

    Start --> Q1
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1
    Q1 -- No --> Q2
    Q2 -- Yes --> A2
    Q2 -- No --> Q3
    Q3 -- Yes --> A3
    Q3 -- No --> Q4
    Q4 -- Yes --> A4
    Q4 -- No --> Q5
    Q5 -- Yes --> A5
    Q5 -- No --> A3
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Relationship Between Layout Type and MH Equipment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LayoutA["Product Layout"]
    LayoutB["Process Layout"]
    LayoutC["Fixed Position Layout"]
    LayoutD["Cellular Layout"]

    EqA["Belt Conveyor"]
    EqA1["Roller Conveyor"]
    EqA2["Assembly Robot"]

    EqB["Forklift"]
    EqB1["Hand Truck"]
    EqB2["AGV"]

    EqC["Mobile Crane"]
    EqC1["Gantry Crane"]

    EqD["Conveyor in cell"]
    EqD1["Compact AGV"]

    LayoutA --> EqA
    LayoutA --> EqA1
    LayoutA --> EqA2

    LayoutB --> EqB
    LayoutB --> EqB1
    LayoutB --> EqB2

    LayoutC --> EqC
    LayoutC --> EqC1

    LayoutD --> EqD
    LayoutD --> EqD1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define Plant Layout. List any four objectives of a good plant layout.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Plant Layout refers to the physical arrangement of industrial facilities including machines, equipment, workstations, storage areas, and material handling systems, designed to ensure smooth, economical, and safe flow of materials through the production system.

**Four Objectives of Plant Layout:**
1. To minimize material handling cost and backtracking.
2. To ensure smooth and continuous flow of work-in-progress.
3. To provide adequate space for workers, machines, and movement.
4. To allow flexibility for future expansion and product modification.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] State any six principles of material handling.**

**Model Answer (any six of Apple's 10 Principles):**

1. **Planning Principle** — Plan all material handling activities before implementation.
2. **Standardization Principle** — Use standardized MH methods and equipment.
3. **Work Principle** — Provide safe working conditions.
4. **Ergonomic Principle** — Design according to human capabilities and limitations.
5. **Unit Load Principle** — Handle materials in largest possible loads.
6. **Space Utilization Principle** — Make effective use of cubic space.
7. **System Principle** — Integrate MH into a coordinated system.
8. **Automation Principle** — Automate MH where feasible.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, May 2024]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the four main types of plant layout with neat diagrams. Discuss their advantages, limitations, and suitable applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer Structure:**

**1. Product Layout:**
- Arrangement: Machines arranged in sequence of operations as a straight line or U-shape.
- Advantages: Low MH cost, smooth flow, low WIP, easy control.
- Limitations: High capital, low flexibility, bottleneck risk.
- Application: Mass production — automobiles, washing machines, bottling.

**2. Process Layout:**
- Arrangement: Similar machines grouped into departments.
- Advantages: High flexibility, better machine utilization, lower investment.
- Limitations: Complex flow, high WIP, high MH cost.
- Application: Job shops, tool rooms, hospitals.

**3. Fixed Position Layout:**
- Arrangement: Product stays at one place, resources brought to it.
- Advantages: No movement of bulky product.
- Limitations: High coordination, difficult MH.
- Application: Shipbuilding, aircraft, bridge construction.

**4. Cellular Layout:**
- Arrangement: Machines grouped into cells for part families.
- Advantages: Combines benefits of both layouts, low WIP.
- Limitations: Requires GT analysis, rebalancing complexity.
- Application: CNC machining, electronics assembly.

**(Diagrams as shown in Section 4.1 must accompany the answer.)**

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss the various factors that influence the selection of material handling equipment for a manufacturing industry. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The selection of MH equipment depends on the following factors:

1. **Material Characteristics:** Weight, size, shape, fragility, hazardous nature.
2. **Movement Requirements:** Distance, direction, frequency, path (fixed vs. variable).
3. **Production Volume:** High volume demands continuous equipment (conveyors); low volume favors trucks.
4. **Layout Type:** Product layout favors conveyors; process layout favors forklifts.
5. **Capital and Operating Cost:** Life cycle cost analysis.
6. **Safety and Ergonomics:** OSHA compliance, worker fatigue.
7. **Space Availability:** Vertical vs. horizontal space utilization.
8. **Flexibility and Future Needs:** Modular vs. fixed installations.
9. **Maintenance and Reliability:** Ease of repair, availability of spares.
10. **Environmental Impact:** Energy consumption, noise, emissions.

**Conclusion:** The optimal MH equipment is one that minimizes total cost (acquisition + operation + maintenance) while satisfying all technical, safety, and ergonomic constraints.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — Alternative Set [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the Centre of Gravity method for determining the optimal location of a new facility. Use a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Centre of Gravity method is a quantitative technique used in facility layout to find the optimal location of a new facility such that the total weighted material movement cost is minimized.

**Mathematical Formulae:**

$$
\bar{X} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}, \quad \bar{Y} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}
$$

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. Identify all existing facilities / departments with their $(x_i, y_i)$ coordinates.
2. Determine the load (weight / frequency) $w_i$ moved to/from each.
3. Compute the weighted sum $\sum w_i x_i$ and $\sum w_i y_i$.
4. Compute the total load $\sum w_i$.
5. Apply the formula to obtain $(\bar{X}, \bar{Y})$.

**Worked Example:** As solved in Section 3.1 (Step 6), four departments with loads $w_i = 5, 3, 8, 4$ tonnes/month give optimal location at $(32 \text{ m}, 22 \text{ m})$.

**Advantages:** Simple, quantitative, accounts for load variations.
**Limitations:** Assumes rectilinear distance; ignores obstacles and qualitative factors.

---

**Q. (b)** Classify material handling equipment. Explain any three types of conveyors with diagrams. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification (as in Section 3.2 matrix):**

1. Conveyors
2. Cranes and Hoists
3. Industrial Trucks
4. Automated Systems (AGV, AS/RS, Robots)
5. Lifting and Positioning Devices

**Three Conveyor Types:**

**1. Belt Conveyor:**
- A continuous belt of rubber/fabric moves over rollers.
- Used for bulk materials (coal, grain, sand).
- Can be horizontal or inclined (up to 30°).
- Capacity: 10 to 10,000 tonnes/hour.

**2. Roller Conveyor:**
- Series of rollers mounted in a frame.
- Powered (motor-driven) or gravity (downhill).
- Used for boxes, cartons, pallets.
- Common in assembly lines and warehouses.

**3. Screw Conveyor:**
- A helical screw rotates inside a trough.
- Used for granular materials (flour, cement, grain).
- Limited to short distances (<30 m) and horizontal/inclined paths.

**4. Pneumatic Conveyor:**
- Material is suspended in air/gas stream and blown through pipes.
- Used for fine particles (cement, plastic pellets, grain).
- Fully enclosed — suitable for hazardous/dusty materials.

(Diagrams from Section 4.2 can be referenced.)

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Plant Layout & MH Answers:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Layout" with "Material Handling":** Layout is the *arrangement*; MH is the *movement*. Examiners deduct 2 marks if you use them interchangeably.
> 2. **Missing the Unit Load Principle:** In any MH answer, mentioning the Unit Load Principle as the **most important** principle is a guaranteed scoring line.
> 3. **No Diagrams:** Every layout question **must** include a diagram. Even a hand-drawn ASCII flowchart fetches 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Wrong Examples:** Students often write "car factory" for process layout. Correct example: tool room, hospital.
> 5. **Centre of Gravity — forgetting units:** Always state **metres** in the final answer.
> 6. **Forgetting WIP and Flexibility in Comparison:** Comparison table answers must explicitly mention **WIP**, **flexibility**, and **MH cost** to score full marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must memorize before KTU exam):**

- ☐ Plant Layout = physical arrangement of machines, men, materials for **smooth flow**.
- ☐ **4 Main Types:** Product, Process, Fixed Position, Cellular (and Combined).
- ☐ **Product Layout** → Mass production, low flexibility, low WIP.
- ☐ **Process Layout** → Job/batch, high flexibility, high WIP.
- ☐ **Fixed Position** → Shipbuilding, aircraft, large projects.
- ☐ **Cellular** → Group Technology, part families, U-shaped cells.
- ☐ **Apple's 10 MH Principles** — memorize **all 10** with one-liner each.
- ☐ **Most Important Principle:** Unit Load Principle.
- ☐ **MH Cost** = 15–70% of total production cost (no value addition).
- ☐ **Centre of Gravity Formula:** $\bar{X} = \frac{\sum w_i x_i}{\sum w_i}$, $\bar{Y} = \frac{\sum w_i y_i}{\sum w_i}$.
- ☐ **MH Equipment Categories:** Conveyors, Cranes, Trucks, Automated, Lifting.
- ☐ **Belt Conveyor** = bulk materials; **Roller** = boxes/cartons; **Screw** = granules.
- ☐ **Bridge Crane (EOT)** = 3-axis heavy lifting in machine shops.
- ☐ **Forklift** = palletized goods in warehouses.
- ☐ **AGV** = automated flexible material movement.
- ☐ **Selection depends on:** Material, distance, volume, layout, cost, safety.
- ☐ **Best Layout** = one with **minimum total Load × Distance**.
- ☐ **Always include diagrams** in layout-type answers.
- ☐ **Always mention WIP, flexibility, MH cost** in comparison answers.
- ☐ **Rectilinear distance** is used in Centre of Gravity method (not Euclidean).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aggregate Production Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET601 - INDUSTRIAL AND SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Aggregate Production Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Aggregate Production Planning (APP)** is a medium-range (intermediate-horizon) planning process, typically spanning **3 to 18 months**, that establishes the overall production quantities, workforce levels, inventory levels, subcontracting volumes, overtime/undertime hours, and backorder levels required to meet forecasted aggregate demand at minimum total cost, while satisfying operational and strategic constraints of the firm.

The term *"aggregate"* signifies that planning is conducted at a **macro level** — using a single composite unit of measure (such as product families, total labor-hours, total machine-hours, or weighted average units) — rather than for individual end-products (Finished Product Planning, FPP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** APP is the process of developing a high-level production plan that balances demand fluctuations with capacity constraints using a combination of demand and supply-side options, expressed in aggregated units over a medium planning horizon.

## 1.2 Position in the Planning Hierarchy

| Level | Planning Type | Horizon | Decisions |
|-------|---------------|---------|-----------|
| 1 | **Strategic (Long-Range)** | 1–5 years | Capacity expansion, new plants, technology |
| 2 | **Aggregate (Medium-Range)** | 3–18 months | Production rates, workforce, inventory, subcontracting |
| 3 | **Disaggregate (Short-Range)** | 1–3 months | Master Production Schedule (MPS) per SKU |
| 4 | **Operational (Immediate)** | 1 day – 1 month | Shop-floor scheduling, dispatching |

> [!NOTE]
> APP is the **bridge** between strategic decisions and the Master Production Schedule. It "unlocks" the feasibility of the long-term plan by translating it into an executable aggregate blueprint.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **bus conductor** planning for a festival season. The conductor doesn't track every individual passenger — instead, he estimates *total passenger demand per hour* (aggregate), decides how many *buses* to deploy (capacity), whether to *call in extra drivers* (overtime) or let some *buses rest* (undertime), how many *standing passengers* to allow (inventory), and whether to *delay some passengers* to the next bus (backorders). This macro-balancing is exactly what **Aggregate Production Planning** does in a factory.

## 1.4 Key Decisions in APP

- **Production Rate**: How many aggregate units to produce per period?
- **Workforce Level**: How many workers to retain/hire/lay off per period?
- **Inventory Level**: How much finished-goods stock to carry?
- **Backorder Level**: How much demand to defer to future periods?
- **Overtime/Undertime**: How many extra/lost labor hours to schedule?
- **Subcontracting**: How much work to outsource to third parties?
- **Part-time Workers**: Whether to engage temporary labor pools?

## 1.5 Aggregate Unit of Measure

The **aggregate unit** is critical. Common choices are:
- **Product-family units** (e.g., "tons of steel")
- **Labor-hours** (e.g., "1,000 standard labor-hours")
- **Machine-hours** (e.g., "500 machine-hours")
- **Dollar value of output** (e.g., "₹ 5 lakh of products")
- **Equivalent units** (weighted by processing intensity)

> [!TIP]
> KTU problems typically use **aggregate units** (e.g., "units of family A") and assume a **fixed labor requirement per unit (k hours/unit)** to allow conversion between production and workforce decisions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 APP Strategies — The Big Three

### 2.1.1 Chase Strategy (Demand-Chasing)
- **Concept**: Production rate exactly matches demand each period; inventory is held near zero.
- **Mechanism**: Hire when demand rises, lay off when demand falls.
- **Cost Implication**: High hiring and firing costs; low holding/backorder costs.
- **Best For**: Service-oriented, skilled-labor industries, low-inventory-holding environments.
- **Workforce Rule**: $W_t = \dfrac{D_t}{k}$, where $D_t$ is demand and $k$ is labor-units per product unit.

### 2.1.2 Level Strategy (Production-Leveling)
- **Concept**: Production rate is held **constant** across all periods; fluctuations absorbed entirely through inventory and backorders.
- **Mechanism**: Constant workforce; accumulate inventory in low-demand periods, draw it down in high-demand periods.
- **Cost Implication**: High holding and backorder costs; zero hiring/firing cost.
- **Best For**: Stable, low-variability demand; capital-intensive or highly-skilled workforces.

### 2.1.3 Mixed / Hybrid Strategy
- **Concept**: Combines elements — uses overtime, subcontracting, part-time, and moderate hiring alongside stable production.
- **Mechanism**: A balanced portfolio of levers, each with its own cost structure.
- **Cost Implication**: Moderate across all categories.
- **Best For**: Most real-world scenarios — this is the **default** in modern industry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU questions frequently test the ability to **compute total cost** for each strategy and select the **least-cost option**. Always tabulate all relevant cost components.

## 2.2 Cost Components in APP

| # | Cost Component | Symbol | Applies To | Unit |
|---|----------------|--------|------------|------|
| 1 | Regular-time production | $C_{RT}$ | All strategies | ₹/unit |
| 2 | Overtime production | $C_{OT}$ | Hybrid/Chase | ₹/unit |
| 3 | Hiring cost | $C_H$ | Workforce change | ₹/worker hired |
| 4 | Firing (layoff) cost | $C_F$ | Workforce change | ₹/worker laid off |
| 5 | Inventory holding cost | $C_I$ | Level/Hybrid | ₹/unit/period |
| 6 | Backorder (stockout) cost | $C_B$ | Level/Hybrid | ₹/unit/period |
| 7 | Subcontracting cost | $C_S$ | Hybrid | ₹/unit |
| 8 | Undertime (idle-time) cost | $C_U$ | Level | ₹/labor-hour |
| 9 | Part-time labor cost | $C_{PT}$ | Hybrid | ₹/labor-hour |

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| $\text{Workers required} = \dfrac{D_t}{k \times H}$ | Workforce calculation, where $k$ = labor-hours/unit, $H$ = hours/period |
| $W_t = W_{t-1} + H_t - F_t$ | Workforce balance: current = previous + hired − fired |
| $H_t = \max(0, W_t - W_{t-1})$ | Hiring in period $t$ |
| $F_t = \max(0, W_{t-1} - W_t)$ | Firing in period $t$ |
| $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t + B_{t-1} - B_t$ | Inventory balance (with backorders) |
| $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$ | Inventory balance (no backorders) |
| $P_t = W_t \cdot k$ | Production = workers × productivity |
| $\text{Total Cost} = \sum_t \left[ C_{RT} P_t + C_H H_t + C_F F_t + C_I I_t + C_B B_t + \ldots \right]$ | Aggregate cost function |
| $\text{Inventory Carrying} = \bar{I} \times C_I$ | Average inventory × holding rate |
| $\text{Backorder Penalty} = \bar{B} \times C_B$ | Average backorders × penalty rate |
| $\text{Chase} \Rightarrow I_t = 0, P_t = D_t$ | Definition of pure chase |
| $\text{Level} \Rightarrow P_t = \bar{D} = \dfrac{\sum D_t}{T}$ | Constant production = average demand |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU papers, **always define variables explicitly** before substituting — examiners award marks for the *setup* even before computation.

## 2.4 Transportation Method for APP

The **Transportation Method** is a classical optimization technique for APP where:
- **Rows** = Periods of production (supply)
- **Columns** = Periods of demand (demand)
- **Cell cost** $c_{ij}$ = (production cost in $i$) + (holding cost from $i$ to $j$) + (backorder cost if $j < i$)
- **Capacity** of each row = max producible in period $i$
- **Demand** of each column = forecast demand in period $j$

The optimal solution minimizes $\sum_{i,j} c_{ij} x_{ij}$ subject to supply and demand constraints.

## 2.5 Linear Programming Formulation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Minimize} \quad & Z = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left[ C_{RT} P_t + C_{OT} P_t^{OT} + C_H H_t + C_F F_t + C_I I_t^{+} + C_B I_t^{-} + C_S S_t \right] \\[4pt]
\textbf{Subject to:} \quad & I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t + P_t^{OT} + S_t - D_t + I_{t-1}^{-} - I_t^{-} \\[2pt]
& P_t \le k \cdot W_t^{RT} \cdot H \\[2pt]
& P_t^{OT} \le k \cdot W_t^{OT} \cdot H^{OT} \\[2pt]
& W_t = W_{t-1} + H_t - F_t \\[2pt]
& P_t, P_t^{OT}, H_t, F_t, I_t^{+}, I_t^{-}, S_t \ge 0
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — KTU-Style APP Cost Comparison

### Problem Statement

A firm produces a single product family. Demand over the next **4 months** is:

| Month | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|-------|----|----|----|----|
| Demand (units) | 500 | 700 | 800 | 600 |

**Initial Conditions:** Beginning inventory = 100 units; Beginning workforce = 10 workers.

**Parameters:**
- Productivity: $k = 50$ units/worker/month
- Regular-time cost: $C_{RT} = ₹20$/unit
- Holding cost: $C_I = ₹5$/unit/month
- Backorder cost: $C_B = ₹10$/unit/month
- Hiring cost: $C_H = ₹300$/worker
- Firing cost: $C_F = ₹400$/worker
- Initial inventory, $I_0 = 100$ units

**Required:** Compare the total cost of the **Chase**, **Level**, and a **Mixed** strategy.

---

### 3.1.1 Strategy 1 — Chase Strategy

> **Logic:** Produce exactly to demand each period; inventory always zero; adjust workforce.

Since $I_0 = 100$ units, we can absorb it by producing less in Month 1.

**Worker requirement per period:**

$$
W_t = \left\lceil \frac{D_t - I_{t-1}}{k} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{D_t - I_{t-1}}{50} \right\rceil
$$

**Month 1:** $D_1 = 500$, $I_0 = 100$ → need 400 units → $W_1 = 400/50 = 8$ workers  
**Month 2:** $D_2 = 700$, $I_1 = 0$ → $W_2 = 700/50 = 14$ workers  
**Month 3:** $D_3 = 800$, $I_2 = 0$ → $W_3 = 800/50 = 16$ workers  
**Month 4:** $D_4 = 600$, $I_3 = 0$ → $W_4 = 600/50 = 12$ workers

| Month | Demand | Production | Workers | Hire | Fire |
|-------|--------|------------|---------|------|------|
| 1 | 500 | 400 | 8 | — | 2 (from 10→8) |
| 2 | 700 | 700 | 14 | 6 | — |
| 3 | 800 | 800 | 16 | 2 | — |
| 4 | 600 | 600 | 12 | — | 4 |
| **Total** | | | | **8** | **6** |

**Total Chase Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Production Cost} &= (400 + 700 + 800 + 600) \times 20 = 2500 \times 20 = ₹50{,}000 \\
\text{Hiring Cost} &= 8 \times 300 = ₹2{,}400 \\
\text{Firing Cost} &= 6 \times 400 = ₹2{,}400 \\
\text{Holding Cost} &= 0 \\
\text{Backorder Cost} &= 0 \\
\hline
\textbf{Total Chase Cost} &= ₹54{,}800
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.1.2 Strategy 2 — Level Strategy

> **Logic:** Constant workforce producing a constant rate; inventory absorbs fluctuations.

**Total demand** = $500 + 700 + 800 + 600 = 2600$ units over 4 months.  
**Net production required** = $2600 - 100 = 2500$ units (subtracting beginning inventory).  
**Constant monthly production** $P = 2500/4 = 625$ units/month.  
**Constant workforce** $W = 625/50 = 12.5 \rightarrow 13$ workers (round up; or use fractional).

Using 13 workers → $P = 13 \times 50 = 650$ units/month.  
Over 4 months, total production = $650 \times 4 = 2600$ units → ending inventory = $I_0 + \text{prod} - \text{demand} = 100 + 2600 - 2600 = 100$ units.

**Period-by-period inventory:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= I_0 + P - D_1 = 100 + 650 - 500 = 250 \\
I_2 &= 250 + 650 - 700 = 200 \\
I_3 &= 200 + 650 - 800 = 50 \\
I_4 &= 50 + 650 - 600 = 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hiring:** From 10 to 13 workers → 3 hires in Month 1 (and zero thereafter).  
**Firing:** 0.

**Total Level Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Production Cost} &= 2600 \times 20 = ₹52{,}000 \\
\text{Hiring Cost} &= 3 \times 300 = ₹900 \\
\text{Holding Cost} &= (250 + 200 + 50 + 100) \times 5 = 600 \times 5 = ₹3{,}000 \\
\text{Backorder Cost} &= 0 \\
\hline
\textbf{Total Level Cost} &= ₹55{,}900
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.1.3 Strategy 3 — Mixed Strategy (Sample Hybrid)

> **Logic:** Use overtime in peak months to avoid hiring spikes; keep workforce moderate.

Let's try: **Base workforce = 12 workers** (12 × 50 = 600 units regular time), with overtime of up to 100 units/month at $C_{OT} = ₹30$/unit (50% premium on $C_{RT}$).

| Month | Demand | Regular Prod | Overtime | Hire/Fire | Ending Inv. |
|-------|--------|--------------|----------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | 500 | 600 | 0 | H:2 (10→12) | 200 |
| 2 | 700 | 600 | 100 | 0 | 200 |
| 3 | 800 | 600 | 100 | 0 | 100 |
| 4 | 600 | 600 | 0 | 0 | 100 |
| **Total** | | 2400 | 200 | 2 | |

**Total Mixed Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Regular Production} &= 2400 \times 20 = ₹48{,}000 \\
\text{Overtime Production} &= 200 \times 30 = ₹6{,}000 \\
\text{Hiring Cost} &= 2 \times 300 = ₹600 \\
\text{Holding Cost} &= (200 + 200 + 100 + 100) \times 5 = 600 \times 5 = ₹3{,}000 \\
\hline
\textbf{Total Mixed Cost} &= ₹57{,}600
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.1.4 Strategy Comparison

| Strategy | Production | Hiring | Firing | Holding | Backorder | **Total** |
|----------|-----------|--------|--------|---------|-----------|-----------|
| **Chase** | 50,000 | 2,400 | 2,400 | 0 | 0 | **₹54,800** |
| **Level** | 52,000 | 900 | 0 | 3,000 | 0 | **₹55,900** |
| **Mixed** | 54,000 | 600 | 0 | 3,000 | 0 | **₹57,600** |

> **Recommendation:** **Chase strategy** is cheapest in this scenario (low holding cost relative to labor adjustment cost). In a different parameter set (high holding cost, low hiring cost), Level might win.

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Framework

```
            ┌──────────────────────────────┐
            │   Compute Demand Variability │
            │     (CV = σ/μ of demand)     │
            └──────────────┬───────────────┘
                           │
            ┌──────────────▼───────────────┐
            │   Is Holding Cost (C_I) Low? │
            └──────────────┬───────────────┘
                  YES       │        NO
            ┌──────────────▼─┐  ┌──────▼───────────────┐
            │  CHASE Strategy │  │ Is Hire/Fire Cost Low?│
            │  (low C_I path) │  └──────┬───────────────┘
            └─────────────────┘    YES   │    NO
                                  ┌─────▼────┐ ┌────▼────┐
                                  │  CHASE   │ │  LEVEL  │
                                  └──────────┘ └─────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Mixed/Hybrid** strategy almost always lies between these pure extremes and is selected when a portfolio of cost trade-offs is most efficient.

## 3.3 Key Numerical Ratios for Quick KTU Heuristics

| Indicator | Formula | Interpretation |
|-----------|---------|----------------|
| **Demand CV** | $\sigma_D / \mu_D$ | $> 0.5$ → high variability → favor chase/level trade-off |
| **Cost Ratio 1** | $C_H + C_F \over C_I$ | High → favor Level; Low → favor Chase |
| **Cost Ratio 2** | $C_B \over C_I$ | High → favor Chase (avoid backorders) |
| **Productivity** | $k$ | Constant; bridges production ↔ workforce |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — APP Decision Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Long-Range Strategic Plan] --> B[Aggregate Production Plan]
    B --> C[Master Production Schedule MPS]
    C --> D[Short-Range Shop Floor Schedule]
    B --> E[Capacity Decisions]
    B --> F[Workforce Decisions]
    B --> G[Inventory Decisions]
    B --> H[Subcontracting Decisions]
    E --> E1[Overtime]
    E --> E2[Undertime]
    F --> F1[Hire]
    F --> F2[Layoff]
    G --> G1[Build Inventory]
    G --> G2[Allow Backorders]
    H --> H1[Outsource Production]
    H --> H2[Part-Time Labor]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — APP Strategy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start([Forecasts: Demand over T periods]) --> Compute[Compute total cost for each strategy]
    Compute --> Chase{Chase Strategy}
    Compute --> Level{Level Strategy}
    Compute --> Mixed{Mixed Hybrid Strategy}
    Chase --> CostA[Cost A: high H F low H B]
    Level --> CostB[Cost B: low H F high H B]
    Mixed --> CostC[Cost C: balanced portfolio]
    CostA --> Select[Select minimum cost strategy]
    CostB --> Select
    CostC --> Select
    Select --> Output([Approved Aggregate Plan])
```

```mermaid
    P --> P2[Overtime]
    W --> W1[Hiring Cost]
    W --> W2[Firing Cost]

```


# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Aggregate Production Planning. Mention its planning horizon.**

**Model Answer:**
Aggregate Production Planning (APP) is the medium-range planning process (typically **3 to 18 months**) that determines the **overall production quantities, workforce levels, inventory levels, backorder levels, overtime usage, and subcontracting volumes** required to satisfy forecasted aggregate demand at the **minimum total cost**. It operates on **aggregated units** (e.g., product families or labor-hours) rather than individual SKUs, and serves as the bridge between long-range strategic planning and the short-range Master Production Schedule.

> **Marks Distribution (3):** Definition (1.5) + Horizon (0.5) + Role (1)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any four cost components considered in Aggregate Production Planning.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Regular-time production cost** — wages for in-house production at normal hours
2. **Overtime production cost** — premium wages beyond regular hours
3. **Hiring cost** — recruitment, training, onboarding of new workers
4. **Firing (layoff) cost** — severance, administrative, morale costs
5. *Inventory holding cost* and *backorder cost* are the other two commonly considered.

> **Marks Distribution (3):** Listing 4 components (2) + Brief description of each (1)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

A manufacturing firm faces the following demand forecast for the next **6 months**:

| Month | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|-------|----|----|----|----|----|----|
| Demand (units) | 1000 | 1200 | 800 | 1100 | 1500 | 900 |

**Initial Conditions:**
- Beginning inventory $I_0 = 200$ units
- Beginning workforce $W_0 = 20$ workers
- Productivity $k = 50$ units/worker/month
- Regular-time cost $C_{RT} = ₹25$/unit
- Overtime cost $C_{OT} = ₹37.50$/unit (50% premium)
- Holding cost $C_I = ₹4$/unit/month
- Backorder cost $C_B = ₹8$/unit/month
- Hiring cost $C_H = ₹500$/worker
- Firing cost $C_F = ₹700$/worker

**(a) [7 Marks]** Develop the **Chase Strategy** and compute its total cost.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Develop the **Level Strategy** and compute its total cost. Which strategy is more economical? Justify.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — Chase Strategy

**Step 1: Compute workers required each period** (assuming starting from $I_0 = 200$)

$$
W_t = \left\lceil \frac{D_t - I_{t-1}}{k} \right\rceil
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_1 &= (1000 - 200)/50 = 16 \text{ workers} \\
W_2 &= 1200/50 = 24 \text{ workers} \\
W_3 &= 800/50 = 16 \text{ workers} \\
W_4 &= 1100/50 = 22 \text{ workers} \\
W_5 &= 1500/50 = 30 \text{ workers} \\
W_6 &= 900/50 = 18 \text{ workers}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Hiring/Firing Schedule**

| Period | From | To | Hire | Fire |
|--------|------|-----|------|------|
| 1 | 20 | 16 | 0 | 4 |
| 2 | 16 | 24 | 8 | 0 |
| 3 | 24 | 16 | 0 | 8 |
| 4 | 16 | 22 | 6 | 0 |
| 5 | 22 | 30 | 8 | 0 |
| 6 | 30 | 18 | 0 | 12 |
| **Total** | | | **22** | **24** |

**Step 3: Cost Computation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Production Cost} &= (800 + 1200 + 800 + 1100 + 1500 + 900) \times 25 = 6300 \times 25 = ₹1{,}57{,}500 \\
\text{Hiring Cost} &= 22 \times 500 = ₹11{,}000 \\
\text{Firing Cost} &= 24 \times 700 = ₹16{,}800 \\
\text{Holding Cost} &= 0 \\
\text{Backorder Cost} &= 0 \\
\hline
\textbf{Total Chase Cost} &= ₹1{,}85{,}300
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Awarded 7 marks** for: 1 (workforce calculation) + 1 (hiring/firing table) + 2 (production cost) + 1 (hiring cost) + 1 (firing cost) + 1 (final total).

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — Level Strategy

**Step 1: Compute constant production rate**

$$
P = \frac{\sum D_t - I_0}{T} = \frac{6500 - 200}{6} = \frac{6300}{6} = 1050 \text{ units/month}
$$

**Step 2: Compute constant workforce**

$$
W = P/k = 1050/50 = 21 \text{ workers}
$$

**Step 3: Hiring in Period 1** (from 20 → 21): 1 hire, then 0 thereafter.

**Step 4: Period-by-period inventory**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= 200 + 1050 - 1000 = 250 \\
I_2 &= 250 + 1050 - 1200 = 100 \\
I_3 &= 100 + 1050 - 800 = 350 \\
I_4 &= 350 + 1050 - 1100 = 300 \\
I_5 &= 300 + 1050 - 1500 = -150 \quad (\text{backorder of 150 units}) \\
I_6 &= -150 + 1050 - 900 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** Period 5 shows a backorder. This violates pure level — typically in KTU we assume backorders are permitted.

**Step 5: Cost Computation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Production Cost} &= 6 \times 1050 \times 25 = ₹1{,}57{,}500 \\
\text{Hiring Cost} &= 1 \times 500 = ₹500 \\
\text{Firing Cost} &= 0 \\
\text{Holding Cost} &= (250 + 100 + 350 + 300 + 0 + 0) \times 4 = 1000 \times 4 = ₹4{,}000 \\
\text{Backorder Cost} &= 150 \times 8 = ₹1{,}200 \\
\hline
\textbf{Total Level Cost} &= ₹1{,}63{,}200
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison:**

| Strategy | Total Cost (₹) |
|----------|----------------|
| Chase | 1,85,300 |
| **Level** | **1,63,200** ← Cheaper |

> **Justification:** Level strategy is cheaper because the **hiring/firing costs in Chase (₹27,800)** outweigh the combined **holding + backorder costs in Level (₹5,200)**.

> **Awarded 7 marks** for: 1 (constant rate) + 1 (workforce) + 2 (inventory table) + 1 (production cost) + 1 (holding + backorder) + 1 (comparison + justification).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Alternate Choice]

A firm produces two product families, **A** and **B**, with forecasted demand over **4 quarters** as follows:

| Quarter | Family A | Family B |
|---------|----------|----------|
| 1 | 800 | 500 |
| 2 | 1000 | 600 |
| 3 | 1200 | 700 |
| 4 | 900 | 550 |

**Parameters:**
- Labor required: $k_A = 2$ hours/unit-A, $k_B = 1$ hour/unit-B
- Regular hours per worker: 160/month, 480/quarter
- Regular wage: ₹50/hour; Overtime wage: ₹75/hour
- Hiring cost: ₹2000/worker; Firing cost: ₹3000/worker
- Holding cost: ₹3/unit-equivalent/quarter
- Backorder cost: ₹10/unit-equivalent/quarter
- Initial workforce: 10 workers; Initial inventory: 0

**(a) [7 Marks]** Convert the demand into **aggregate labor-hour equivalent** and develop a **Level Strategy**. Compute total cost.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Develop a **Chase Strategy** using aggregate labor-hour conversion. Compute total cost. Compare with (a).

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Level Strategy (Aggregate Labor-Hours)

**Step 1: Convert demand to aggregate labor-hours**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_1 &= 800 \times 2 + 500 \times 1 = 1600 + 500 = 2100 \text{ hrs} \\
Q_2 &= 1000 \times 2 + 600 \times 1 = 2000 + 600 = 2600 \text{ hrs} \\
Q_3 &= 1200 \times 2 + 700 \times 1 = 2400 + 700 = 3100 \text{ hrs} \\
Q_4 &= 900 \times 2 + 550 \times 1 = 1800 + 550 = 2350 \text{ hrs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute average quarterly labor-hour demand**

$$
\bar{Q} = \frac{2100 + 2600 + 3100 + 2350}{4} = \frac{10150}{4} = 2537.5 \text{ hrs}
$$

**Step 3: Compute constant workforce** (assuming 480 hours/quarter per worker)

$$
W = \left\lceil \frac{2537.5}{480} \right\rceil = \lceil 5.29 \rceil = 6 \text{ workers}
$$

**Step 4: Constant production in labor-hours**

$$
P = 6 \times 480 = 2880 \text{ hrs/quarter}
$$

**Step 5: Period-by-period inventory** (in labor-hour equivalents)

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= 0 + 2880 - 2100 = 780 \\
I_2 &= 780 + 2880 - 2600 = 1060 \\
I_3 &= 1060 + 2880 - 3100 = 840 \\
I_4 &= 840 + 2880 - 2350 = 1370
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Cost Computation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Production Cost} &= 4 \times 2880 \times 50 = ₹5{,}76{,}000 \\
\text{Hiring Cost} &= (6 - 10) \text{ negative? — no, fire 4 from 10 to 6} \\
\text{Firing Cost} &= 4 \times 3000 = ₹12{,}000 \\
\text{Holding Cost} &= (780 + 1060 + 840 + 1370) \times 3 = 4050 \times 3 = ₹12{,}150 \\
\text{Backorder Cost} &= 0 \\
\hline
\textbf{Total Level Cost} &= ₹6{,}00{,}150
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Awarded 7 marks** for: 1 (conversion) + 1 (average) + 1 (workforce) + 1 (inventory table) + 2 (cost) + 1 (total).

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — Chase Strategy

**Step 1: Workers required per quarter** (using $W_t = Q_t / 480$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_1 &= 2100/480 = 4.375 \rightarrow 5 \text{ workers} \\
W_2 &= 2600/480 = 5.42 \rightarrow 6 \text{ workers} \\
W_3 &= 3100/480 = 6.46 \rightarrow 7 \text{ workers} \\
W_4 &= 2350/480 = 4.90 \rightarrow 5 \text{ workers}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Hiring/Firing**

| Period | From | To | Hire | Fire |
|--------|------|-----|------|------|
| 1 | 10 | 5 | 0 | 5 |
| 2 | 5 | 6 | 1 | 0 |
| 3 | 6 | 7 | 1 | 0 |
| 4 | 7 | 5 | 0 | 2 |
| **Total** | | | **2** | **7** |

**Step 3: Cost Computation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Production Cost} &= (2100 + 2600 + 3100 + 2350) \times 50 = 10150 \times 50 = ₹5{,}07{,}500 \\
\text{Hiring Cost} &= 2 \times 2000 = ₹4{,}000 \\
\text{Firing Cost} &= 7 \times 3000 = ₹21{,}000 \\
\text{Holding Cost} &= 0 \\
\text{Backorder Cost} &= 0 \\
\hline
\textbf{Total Chase Cost} &= ₹5{,}32{,}500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison:**

| Strategy | Total Cost (₹) |
|----------|----------------|
| **Chase** | **5,32,500** ← Cheaper |
| Level | 6,00,150 |

> **Justification:** In this case, holding cost is low (₹3) relative to firing cost (₹3000), making Chase the better choice.

> **Awarded 7 marks** for: 1 (workers/period) + 1 (H/F table) + 2 (production cost) + 1 (H + F cost) + 1 (total) + 1 (comparison).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where KTU students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract initial inventory** when computing production — this understates cost. Always write $P_t = D_t - I_{t-1}$ explicitly.
> 2. **Rounding workforce carelessly** — use ceiling or floor consistently; do not mix within the same problem.
> 3. **Omitting the hiring/firing transition table** — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for it explicitly. Always tabulate.
> 4. **Mixing up holding cost across periods** — the inventory at end-of-period $t$ carries cost *only for period $t$*; do not double-count.
> 5. **Not stating the strategy being used** — write "Under Chase Strategy" or "Under Level Strategy" at the start of each sub-part. A lost mark here is very common.
> 6. **Backorder sign errors** — when $I_t$ becomes negative, treat it as backorder $B_t = -I_t$, not as a "negative inventory cost." This is a top-3 reason for full-mark denial in KTU.
> 7. **Skipping the comparison/justification** — even if the math is correct, the final 1 mark requires an explicit *recommendation with reasoning*.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Aggregate Production Planning**

- ☐ **APP horizon = 3 to 18 months** (medium range); uses **aggregate units** (product family, labor-hours, or machine-hours).
- ☐ APP is the **bridge** between Strategic Plan and Master Production Schedule.
- ☐ **Three core strategies**: Chase (P = D), Level (P = constant = $\bar{D}$), Mixed/Hybrid.
- ☐ **Workforce balance**: $W_t = W_{t-1} + H_t - F_t$.
- ☐ **Inventory balance**: $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$ (or with backorders: $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t - B_t$).
- ☐ **Chase → high H/F cost, low holding**; **Level → low H/F cost, high holding**.
- ☐ **Mixed/Hybrid** is the most realistic and is the *default* industrial choice.
- ☐ Cost components: $C_{RT}, C_{OT}, C_H, C_F, C_I, C_B, C_S$ — always tabulate all applicable ones.
- ☐ **Transportation method** is a valid optimization tool — rows = production periods, columns = demand periods, cell cost = production + holding/backorder.
- ☐ **Linear programming** formulation is the rigorous alternative — objective minimizes total cost subject to balance and capacity constraints.
- ☐ **Productivity $k$** is the conversion factor between workers and output — never forget it.
- ☐ When comparing strategies, **always end with a recommendation** justified by cost trade-offs.
- ☐ **Sign convention for backorders**: a negative $I_t$ means backorder; charge $C_B \cdot |I_t|$ in that period.
- ☐ The optimal APP minimizes **total cost**, not just one component — be holistic.
- ☐ KTU expects **clear tables, labeled variables, and a closing recommendation** — present like a professional consultant, not just a math solver.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
