topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET601 - INDUSTRIAL AND SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Material Requirement Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)** is a computer-based, logic-driven production planning and inventory control system that translates the **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** into a time-phased schedule of component-level procurement and production activities. It determines *what* items are needed, *how many* are required, and *when* they must be ordered to support the production plan.

Mathematically stated, MRP is governed by the fundamental identity:

$$
\text{Net Requirements (NR)} = \text{Gross Requirements (GR)} - \text{Scheduled Receipts (SR)} - \text{On-Hand Inventory (OH)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **lead time offset** is the operational heart of MRP. The planned order release is shifted *backwards* in time by the item's lead time, so the planned order **receipt** aligns perfectly with the net requirement date.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Kitchen Pantry Planner"

Imagine you are hosting a 7-day dinner party. You write down the **menu for each day** (this is your *Master Production Schedule*). For each dish, you need ingredients like flour, sugar, eggs (this is your *Bill of Materials*). 

Instead of buying a new bottle of milk every morning, MRP works like a smart pantry planner:
- It checks your fridge (the *Inventory Status File*).
- It calculates what you'll need on Day 5.
- It tells you: *"Order 2 litres of milk on Day 3, because the grocery store takes 2 days to deliver"* — that 2 days is the **lead time**.

**MRP does exactly this for a factory**: it converts the "menu" (MPS) into a precise grocery list with delivery dates aligned to the shop floor's consumption schedule.

## 1.3 Historical Context & Evolution

MRP evolved through three distinct generations:

| Generation | Name | Scope | Key Addition |
|------------|------|-------|--------------|
| MRP I (1970s) | Material Requirements Planning | Inventory & scheduling logic | BOM explosion, time-phasing |
| MRP II (1980s) | Manufacturing Resource Planning | Shop floor + finance integration | Capacity planning, costing |
| ERP (1990s–present) | Enterprise Resource Planning | Whole-organisation integration | SCM, CRM, HR modules |

## 1.4 Core Objectives of MRP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three primary objectives of MRP (Kanet & Wright framework) are:
> 1. **Inventory Minimisation** — ensure materials arrive *Just-In-Time*, not *Just-in-Case*.
> 2. **Priority Enforcement** — maintain the delivery schedule of finished goods.
> 3. **Load Balancing** — plan shop floor workload using finite capacity checks.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Primary Inputs to MRP

The MRP engine is essentially a *data-fusion processor*. It takes three primary inputs and runs them through a logic engine to produce actionable outputs.

### Input 1: Master Production Schedule (MPS)
- A **time-phased** statement of *what* finished products must be produced, *in what quantities*, and *by when*.
- Operates on the **planning horizon** (typically 3–18 months).
- Driven by demand forecasts and confirmed customer orders.
- **Unit of measure**: pieces, kg, hours.

### Input 2: Bill of Materials (BOM)
- A **structured, hierarchical product definition** listing every component, sub-assembly, and raw material needed to make one unit of the parent item.
- Visualised as an **indented BOM** or **product structure tree** (see SECTION 4).
- Each level represents a stage of manufacture.

### Input 3: Inventory Status File (ISF)
- The **real-time snapshot** of all inventory positions, including:
  - On-hand quantity
  - Allocated (reserved) stock
  - Scheduled receipts (open POs / WIP)
  - Safety stock levels
  - Lead times (lot size, scrap %, etc.)

> [!NOTE]
> **The ISF is the "source of truth" for the MRP system**. Errors in ISF propagate as errors in the planned order schedule.

## 2.2 The Three Primary Outputs of MRP

| Output | Purpose | Audience |
|--------|---------|----------|
| **Planned Order Releases** | Authorise procurement / production | Buyer / Shop Foreman |
| **Order Rescheduling Notices** | Modify existing open orders | Buyer / Production Planner |
| **Exception Reports** | Highlight out-of-bound situations (past-due, scrap, planning failure) | Master Scheduler |

## 2.3 MRP Processing Logic — Step-by-Step

For **each item at each level** of the BOM, MRP runs the following algorithmic loop:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The MRP Calculation Engine Logic:**
> 1. Read **Gross Requirements (GR)** for the period.
> 2. Subtract **Scheduled Receipts (SR)** arriving in that period.
> 3. Compare with **Projected On-Hand (POH)** inventory.
> 4. If POH ≥ GR, no action. Carry forward POH = POH − GR.
> 5. If POH < GR, generate a **Net Requirement (NR)**.
> 6. Apply **lot-sizing rule** to convert NR into a **Planned Order Receipt (PORc)**.
> 7. **Offset backwards** by lead time → **Planned Order Release (POR)**.
> 8. **Explode** the POR to determine Gross Requirements for all child items at the next BOM level.

## 2.4 Lot Sizing Techniques — Theory & Engineering Utility

Lot sizing is the **decision rule** for *how much* to order when a net requirement arises. It is the most-examined topic in KTU Module 2.

| Technique | Core Idea | Formula / Logic | Best Use Case |
|-----------|-----------|-----------------|---------------|
| **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)** | Order exactly the net requirement | $Q = NR$ | High-value items, low carrying cost |
| **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | Minimise total inventory cost | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | Constant, independent demand |
| **Economic Production Quantity (EPQ)** | EOQ with finite production rate | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}\cdot\dfrac{p}{p-u}}$ | Produced internally, not purchased |
| **Period Order Quantity (POQ)** | Use EOQ, expressed in periods | $\text{EOQ} \div \text{avg. weekly demand}$ | Stable demand, lumpy replenishment |
| **Part Period Balancing (PPB)** | Add periods until EPB cost = setup cost | $\text{EPB} = \sum D_i \cdot i \cdot H \approx S$ | Where carrying cost dominates |
| **Wagner-Whitin Algorithm** | Dynamic programming optimum | DP recursion | Most accurate, complex computationally |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** that may appear in a KTU 2024 PCMET601 examination. Memorise units carefully.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $NR = GR - SR - POH_{\text{prev}}$ | Net Requirements | units |
| 2 | $POH_t = POH_{t-1} + PORc_t + SR_t - GR_t$ | Projected On-Hand | units |
| 3 | $POR = PORc$ shifted back by LT | Planned Order Release | units |
| 4 | $Q_{EOQ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | Economic Order Quantity | units/order |
| 5 | $Q_{EPQ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}\cdot\dfrac{p}{p-u}}$ | Economic Production Quantity | units/lot |
| 6 | $N_{EOQ} = \dfrac{Q_{EOQ}}{D}$ | Reorder point (units) | units |
| 7 | $\text{ROP} = d \cdot L + SS$ | Reorder Point | units |
| 8 | $TC_{EOQ} = \sqrt{2DSH}$ | Minimum Total Cost | currency/year |
| 9 | $H = I \cdot C$ | Holding cost | currency/unit/year |
| 10 | $EPB_k = \sum_{j=1}^{k} D_j \cdot (j-1) \cdot H$ | Part Period Cost for k periods | currency |

**Where:** $D$ = annual demand, $S$ = ordering/setup cost, $H$ = holding cost per unit per year, $p$ = production rate, $u$ = usage rate, $L$ = lead time, $SS$ = safety stock, $I$ = holding cost fraction, $C$ = unit cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition for EOQ**: $Q^* > 0$, demand must be approximately constant, and instantaneous replenishment is assumed. If replenishment is gradual → use **EPQ**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

MRP is the **digital nervous system** of discrete manufacturing. Modern variants power:
- **Automotive assembly lines** (Toyota Production System integrated with MRP).
- **Aerospace MRO operations** (Bill-of-Materials with thousands of components per aircraft).
- **Electronics OEMs** (Apple, Samsung) managing global supply chains.
- **Lean + MRP hybrids** — used in Industry 4.0 smart factories where MRP logic runs on MES/SCADA layers.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Complete MRP Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Style):**
> For Item X, the following data is given:
> - Lead Time (LT) = **2 weeks**
> - Safety Stock (SS) = **0 units**
> - Beginning Inventory = **0 units**
> - Lot-sizing rule = **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)**
>
> | Week | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
> |------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | Gross Requirements | 0 | 0 | 50 | 0 | 0 | 50 | 0 | 100 |
> | Scheduled Receipts | 50 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

**Step 1 — Projected On-Hand (POH) at end of Week 1:**

$$
POH_1 = POH_0 + SR_1 - GR_1 = 0 + 50 - 0 = 50 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — Projected On-Hand at end of Week 2:**

$$
POH_2 = POH_1 + SR_2 - GR_2 = 50 + 0 - 0 = 50 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3 — Week 3: POH is insufficient.**

$$
POH_3 = 50 + 0 - 50 = 0 \text{ units}
$$

Since $POH_3 = GR_3$, **no net requirement is generated**.

**Step 4 — Week 4 & 5: No demand, no receipts.**

$$
POH_4 = 0, \quad POH_5 = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Week 6: Demand 50, POH is 0.**

$$
POH_6 = 0 + 0 - 50 = -50
$$

**Net Requirement in Week 6:**

$$
NR_6 = 50 - 0 - 0 = 50 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Lot-for-Lot: Planned Order Receipt = 50 units in Week 6.**

$$
PORc_6 = 50
$$

**Step 7 — Offset by Lead Time (2 weeks):**

$$
POR_4 = PORc_6 = 50 \text{ units} \quad (\text{released in Week } 6-2=4)
$$

**Step 8 — Week 7:** No demand, so POH stays 0.

**Step 9 — Week 8: Demand 100, POH is 0.**

$$
NR_8 = 100
$$

$$
PORc_8 = 100, \quad POR_6 = 100 \text{ units (released in Week } 8-2=6)
$$

### Final MRP Table

| Week | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Requirements | 0 | 0 | 50 | 0 | 0 | 50 | 0 | 100 |
| Scheduled Receipts | 50 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| **Projected On-Hand** | 50 | 50 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| **Net Requirements** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 50 | 0 | 100 |
| **Planned Order Receipts** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 50 | 0 | 100 |
| **Planned Order Releases** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 50 | 0 | 100 | 0 | 0 |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: EOQ Lot Sizing

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Annual demand $D = 5000$ units, ordering cost $S = \text{₹}200$ per order, holding cost $H = \text{₹}5$ per unit per year.

**Step 1 — Substitute into EOQ formula:**

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}
$$

**Step 2 — Plug in numerical values:**

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5000 \times 200}{5}}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify numerator:**

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2{,}000{,}000}{5}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the quotient:**

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{400{,}000}
$$

**Step 5 — Take the square root:**

$$
Q^* \approx 632.46 \text{ units} \approx 632 \text{ units per order}
$$

**Step 6 — Minimum Total Cost (TC):**

$$
TC_{min} = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 5000 \times 200 \times 5} = \sqrt{10{,}000{,}000} = \text{₹}3162.28/\text{year}
$$

**Step 7 — Number of orders per year:**

$$
N = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{5000}{632.46} \approx 7.9 \approx 8 \text{ orders/year}
$$

**Step 8 — Time between orders:**

$$
T = \frac{1}{N} = \frac{1}{8} \text{ year} = 1.5 \text{ months}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Part Period Balancing (PPB)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** Demand is lumpy across 4 weeks. Setup cost $S = \text{₹}200$, $H = \text{₹}2$/unit/week. We need to find the optimal lot size that "balances" the part period cost against the setup cost.
>
> | Week | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
> |------|---|---|---|---|
> | Demand (units) | 30 | 50 | 20 | 70 |

**Step 1 — Try covering weeks 1 only:**
$$
EPB_1 = 0 \text{ (no carryover cost)} \quad \rightarrow \text{order 30 units}
$$

**Step 2 — Try covering weeks 1–2:**
$$
EPB_2 = 30 \times 1 \times 2 = \text{₹}60 \quad (\text{carry 30 units for 1 week})
$$
Compare to $S = \text{₹}200$. $EPB_2 < S$, so continue.

**Step 3 — Try covering weeks 1–3:**
$$
EPB_3 = (30 \times 1 \times 2) + (50 \times 2 \times 2) = 60 + 200 = \text{₹}260
$$
Now $EPB_3 = \text{₹}260 > S = \text{₹}200$. **STOP**.

**Decision:** Order covers weeks 1 and 2 (total $30 + 50 = 80$ units). The third week starts a new lot.

**Step 4 — Next lot covers week 3:**
$$
\text{Order 20 units for week 3.}
$$

**Step 5 — Try extending lot to cover weeks 3–4:**
$$
EPB = 20 \times 1 \times 2 = \text{₹}40 < S
$$
Extend further? No more weeks. **Order 20 + 70 = 90 units for weeks 3–4.**

> [!NOTE]
> The **PPB algorithm** is a heuristic — it does not guarantee the global optimum but is computationally simple and yields solutions within 5% of Wagner-Whitin in most cases.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Lot-Sizing Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix helps KTU students select the right lot-sizing technique for a given scenario.

| If your problem has... | Then use... | Because... |
|------------------------|-------------|------------|
| Stable, level demand, purchased items | **EOQ** | Minimises total of ordering + holding cost |
| Stable demand, items produced in-house | **EPQ** | Accounts for production build-up rate |
| High value, lumpy demand | **L4L** | Avoids holding expensive stock |
| Setup cost much greater than holding cost | **L4L** | Setups cheap, holding expensive |
| Holding cost much greater than setup cost | **FOQ / POQ** | Need to amortise setup across long runs |
| Heuristic needed for large BOMs | **PPB** | Fast, near-optimal for moderate variability |
| Need for absolute minimum cost, research context | **Wagner-Whitin** | DP-based proven optimum |
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MRP System Block Diagram — Inputs, Processor, Outputs

The following Mermaid block diagram shows how the three primary MRP inputs are processed to produce the three primary outputs. All node IDs are alphanumeric-prefixed to satisfy the Mermaid compilation safeguard.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[MPS Master Production Schedule] --> coreA[MRP Logic Engine]
    inputB[BOM Bill of Materials] --> coreA
    inputC[ISF Inventory Status File] --> coreA
    coreA --> outputA[POR Planned Order Releases]
    coreA --> outputB[ORN Order Rescheduling Notices]
    coreA --> outputC[EXR Exception Reports]
    outputA --> buyerA[Buyer Action Purchase Orders]
    outputA --> floorA[Shop Floor Production Orders]
    outputB --> buyerA
    outputB --> floorA
    outputC --> schedA[Master Scheduler Review]
```

## 4.2 MRP Processing Logic Flowchart — Net Requirements Loop

The following diagram shows the algorithmic decision sequence executed for every item at every time bucket.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Start: New Time Bucket] --> stepB[Read Gross Requirements GR]
    stepB --> stepC[Add Scheduled Receipts SR to POH]
    stepC --> stepD{POH greater than or equal GR}
    stepD -->|Yes| stepE[POH = POH minus GR, no action]
    stepD -->|No| stepF[Compute Net Requirement NR]
    stepF --> stepG[Apply Lot Sizing Rule]
    stepG --> stepH[Generate Planned Order Receipt PORc]
    stepH --> stepI[Offset by Lead Time LT]
    stepI --> stepJ[Generate Planned Order Release POR]
    stepJ --> stepK[BOM Explosion: GR for child items]
    stepE --> stepL[Move to next time bucket]
    stepK --> stepL
    stepL --> stepB
```

## 4.3 BOM Explosion — Product Structure Tree

The following diagram shows the hierarchical explosion of a finished product (Level 0) into its sub-assemblies and components. This is the structure MRP uses to propagate planned orders down the bill of materials.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    lv0A[FINISHED PRODUCT ASSEMBLY Level 0]
    lv0A --> lv1A[SUB ASSEMBLY A Level 1]
    lv0A --> lv1B[SUB ASSEMBLY B Level 1]
    lv0A --> lv1C[PACKAGING KIT Level 1]
    lv1A --> lv2A[MOTOR UNIT Level 2]
    lv1A --> lv2B[CHASSIS Level 2]
    lv1B --> lv2C[CONTROL BOARD Level 2]
    lv1B --> lv2D[DISPLAY MODULE Level 2]
    lv2A --> lv3A[SHAFT Level 3]
    lv2A --> lv3B[BEARINGS Level 3]
    lv2A --> lv3C[WINDING COIL Level 3]
    lv2C --> lv3D[MICROCHIP Level 3]
    lv2C --> lv3E[CAPACITOR Level 3]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **MRP Explosion Rule:** The Gross Requirements at *Level n+1* equal the Planned Order Release of the parent at *Level n* multiplied by the **usage quantity** in the BOM.

Mathematically:

$$
GR_{\text{child, level } n+1} = POR_{\text{parent, level } n} \times Q_{\text{usage}}
$$

## 4.4 MRP, MRP II, and ERP Evolution Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    eraA[MRP I 1970s Inventory and Scheduling] --> eraB[MRP II 1980s Manufacturing Resource Planning]
    eraB --> eraC[ERP 1990s Enterprise Resource Planning]
    eraC --> eraD[Cloud ERP 2020s SaaS based Industry 4.0]
    eraA -.adds.-> addA[BOM Explosion]
    eraB -.adds.-> addB[Capacity Planning]
    eraC -.adds.-> addC[Finance and HR Integration]
    eraD -.adds.-> addD[AI driven Demand Sensing]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU students must know the *key distinguishing feature* of each generation.** MRP I handles materials; MRP II adds shop floor capacity; ERP unifies the entire enterprise; modern Cloud ERP adds AI/ML for demand sensing and prescriptive analytics.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q1.** Define **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)**. List its **three primary inputs** and **three primary outputs**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

MRP is a computer-based, logic-driven production planning system that translates the Master Production Schedule into a time-phased schedule of component-level orders.

> **Three primary inputs:**
> 1. Master Production Schedule (MPS)
> 2. Bill of Materials (BOM)
> 3. Inventory Status File (ISF)

> **Three primary outputs:**
> 1. Planned Order Releases
> 2. Order Rescheduling Notices
> 3. Exception Reports

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for inputs + 1 mark for outputs.)*

---

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q2.** Differentiate between **MRP I** and **MRP II**. State any **two advantages** of MRP II over MRP I.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | MRP I | MRP II |
|---------|-------|--------|
| Scope | Materials only | Manufacturing resources (machines, labour) |
| Capacity check | Infinite capacity assumed | Finite capacity planning |
| Financial integration | None | Includes cost accounting |
| Feedback loop | One-way | Closed-loop, feedback-driven |

> **Two advantages of MRP II:**
> 1. Integrates production planning with **shop floor capacity** and financial control.
> 2. Provides **closed-loop feedback** for replanning when actuals deviate from plan.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** For the item below, construct a complete **MRP table** using the **Lot-for-Lot** lot-sizing rule. Lead time = 2 weeks. Opening inventory = 30 units.

| Week | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Requirements | 20 | 30 | 0 | 40 | 0 | 0 | 60 | 0 |
| Scheduled Receipts | 0 | 0 | 50 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Wagner-Whitin algorithm** for lot sizing. Why is it considered the optimal method, and what limits its industrial use?

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Compute Projected On-Hand week-by-week:**

$$
POH_1 = 30 + 0 - 20 = 10
$$

$$
POH_2 = 10 + 0 - 30 = -20
$$

**Net Requirement in Week 2:**

$$
NR_2 = 20
$$

**Step 2 — Scheduled receipt of 50 in Week 3:**

$$
POH_3 = 0 + 50 - 0 = 50
$$

$$
POH_4 = 50 + 0 - 40 = 10
$$

**Step 3 — Weeks 5 & 6: no demand.**

$$
POH_5 = 10, \quad POH_6 = 10
$$

**Step 4 — Week 7: demand 60, POH 10 → shortage.**

$$
NR_7 = 60 - 10 = 50
$$

**Step 5 — Week 8: no demand.**

$$
POH_8 = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Apply L4L → Planned Order Release is offset by 2 weeks:**

| Week | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Requirements | 20 | 30 | 0 | 40 | 0 | 0 | 60 | 0 |
| Scheduled Receipts | 0 | 0 | 50 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| **Projected On-Hand** | 10 | 0 | 50 | 10 | 10 | 10 | 0 | 0 |
| **Net Requirements** | 0 | 20 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 50 | 0 |
| **Planned Order Receipts** | 0 | 20 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 50 | 0 |
| **Planned Order Releases** | 20 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 50 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

*(7 marks: 1 mark setup, 4 marks for POH/NR, 2 marks for PORc/POR.)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Wagner-Whitin Algorithm** is a **dynamic programming** method that determines the minimum-cost lot sizing decision for every period in the planning horizon.

**Recursive formulation:**

$$
Z(j) = \min_{1 \le i \le j} \left\{ Z(i-1) + S + H \cdot \sum_{k=i}^{j-1} (k-i+1) \cdot D_k \right\}
$$

Where:
- $Z(j)$ = minimum cost to cover demand through period $j$.
- $S$ = setup cost, $H$ = holding cost per unit per period, $D_k$ = demand in period $k$.

**Optimality:** It is the only algorithm with a **proven optimality guarantee** for the dynamic lot-sizing problem.

**Limitations:**
1. Computationally expensive — $O(n^2)$ complexity, infeasible for n > 50 periods.
2. Requires **deterministic, known future demand** — fails under uncertainty.
3. Does not handle capacity constraints.
4. Industrial MRP systems use it only for high-value, low-volume items (e.g., aerospace spares).

*(7 marks: 2 marks for formulation, 2 marks for optimality, 3 marks for limitations.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A company uses an item with annual demand $D = 12000$ units, ordering cost $S = \text{₹}300$ per order, and holding cost $H = \text{₹}6$ per unit per year. Compute:
1. Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)
2. Number of orders per year
3. Minimum total cost
4. Reorder point if lead time = 4 weeks (assume 52 weeks/year)

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Part Period Balancing (PPB)** technique with a suitable numerical example.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Economic Order Quantity:**

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12000 \times 300}{6}}
$$

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{7{,}200{,}000}{6}} = \sqrt{1{,}200{,}000} \approx 1095.45 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of orders:**

$$
N = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{12000}{1095.45} \approx 10.95 \approx 11 \text{ orders/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Minimum total cost:**

$$
TC_{min} = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 12000 \times 300 \times 6} = \sqrt{43{,}200{,}000} = \text{₹}6572.67/\text{year}
$$

**Step 4 — Reorder point:**

Daily demand $d = D / 365 = 12000/365 \approx 32.88$ units/day.

$$
ROP = d \times L = 32.88 \times 28 = 920.55 \approx 921 \text{ units}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks EOQ, 1 mark N, 1 mark TC, 3 marks ROP.)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**PPB Concept:**
The PPB heuristic adds periods to a lot until the **cumulative part-period cost** (carrying cost × period carried) **just equals or exceeds** the **setup cost** of placing a new order.

**Numerical Example:**

> $S = \text{₹}100$, $H = \text{₹}1$/unit/week
>
> | Week | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
> |------|---|---|---|---|
> | Demand | 40 | 60 | 30 | 70 |

**Step 1 — Try week 1 only:** $EPB_1 = 0$. Order 40 units. *(cumulative cost ₹0)*

**Step 2 — Try weeks 1–2:** $EPB = 40 \times 1 \times 1 = \text{₹}40 < S$. Extend. Order 100 units.

**Step 3 — Try weeks 1–3:** $EPB = 40 \times 1 + 60 \times 2 = 40 + 120 = \text{₹}160 > S = \text{₹}100$. **STOP**.

**Decision:** Order covers weeks 1 and 2 → order $40 + 60 = 100$ units.

**Step 4 — Reset for week 3.** Try extending to week 4: $EPB = 30 \times 1 = \text{₹}30 < S$. No more periods. Order $30 + 70 = 100$ units for weeks 3–4.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for concept, 3 marks for numerical, 2 marks for interpretation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the lead-time offset** when computing Planned Order Release → loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Scheduled Receipts with On-Hand inventory** — these are *different* columns in MRP tables.
> 3. **Mixing up POH calculation** by forgetting to add Scheduled Receipts at the right period.
> 4. **Forgetting to apply lot sizing BEFORE offsetting** — net requirement first, then lot size, then offset.
> 5. **In PPB**: students often add one extra period even after the EPB exceeds the setup cost. STOP exactly at the first crossing.
> 6. **In EOQ**: students often forget that the formula assumes *constant, independent* demand — do not use it for dependent demand items (use MRP-L4L instead).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 PCMET601 — Module 2 / MRP**

- ✅ MRP = **logic-driven**, time-phased, computer-based production planning system.
- ✅ **Three inputs**: MPS (what & when), BOM (how it's made), Inventory Status File (what we have).
- ✅ **Three outputs**: Planned Order Releases, Order Rescheduling Notices, Exception Reports.
- ✅ Core identity: $NR = GR - SR - POH$ and $POH_t = POH_{t-1} + PORc_t + SR_t - GR_t$.
- ✅ **Lead-time offset** is applied to PORc to get POR — offset moves the order *backwards* in time.
- ✅ **L4L** = order exact net requirement (no safety stock, no over-ordering).
- ✅ **EOQ** = $\sqrt{2DS/H}$, valid for *constant independent demand*.
- ✅ **EPQ** = $\sqrt{2DS/H \cdot p/(p-u)}$, used when production is *gradual* (not instantaneous).
- ✅ **POQ** = EOQ expressed in number of periods, useful for stable, lumpy demand.
- ✅ **PPB** = cumulative carrying cost equals setup cost heuristic — fast, near-optimal.
- ✅ **Wagner-Whitin** = DP-based, proven optimum, but $O(n^2)$ — used only for high-value items.
- ✅ **BOM Explosion** propagates planned orders from Level 0 down to raw materials; $GR_{child} = POR_{parent} \times Q_{usage}$.
- ✅ **MRP I → MRP II → ERP** evolution adds capacity planning → finance/HR integration → enterprise-wide unification.
- ✅ **Reorder Point** = $d \cdot L + SS$ — must be computed using *average* daily demand, not annual.
- ✅ For KTU numericals, **always show the POH column step-by-step** — it is the most-scored part of MRP problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Weight Topics for KTU 2024 ESE (Module 2):**
> 1. MRP table construction (≥ 8 marks weightage).
> 2. Lot-sizing rule selection + numerical (≥ 6 marks).
> 3. Differences between MRP I / II / ERP (≥ 4 marks conceptual).
> 4. PPB or Wagner-Whitin (≥ 4 marks).
> 5. BOM explosion and lead-time offset questions (≥ 3 marks).
> 
> **Total expected marks from MRP in KTU 2024 ESE: 25–30 marks across Part A and Part B.**
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inventory Management: EOQ Models, Discount Models, P System, Q System, and Reorder Level
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET601 - INDUSTRIAL AND SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inventory Management: EOQ Models, Discount Models, P System, Q System, and Reorder Level

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inventory Management** is the branch of industrial and systems engineering concerned with planning, ordering, storing, tracking, and controlling an organization's inventory to ensure the right quantity of materials is available at the right time, while minimizing the total cost of ownership.

In a manufacturing or service system, **inventory** refers to the stock of raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), finished goods, MRO (Maintenance, Repair, and Operating) supplies, and transit goods held by an organization. The discipline applies quantitative models to determine:

- **How much** to order (Order Quantity, Q)
- **When** to order (Reorder Point, ROP)
- **How frequently** to review stock (Continuous vs. Periodic)
- **How much safety stock** to maintain against uncertainty

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Grocery Store

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Refrigerator of a Hostel Canteen"**
>
> Imagine a hostel canteen owner who must decide how many litres of milk to buy every week. If he buys too little, students go thirsty (stockout cost). If he buys too much, the milk spoils and his freezer overflows (carrying cost). Every time he places an order from the dairy, he pays a delivery fee (ordering cost). His **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** is the perfect batch size that balances these opposing forces — neither running out nor drowning in milk. This is the heart of inventory management: **trade-off optimisation under uncertainty**.

### 1.3 Key Vocabulary Used Throughout This Module

| Term | Meaning (Plain English) |
|---|---|
| **Demand (D)** | Number of units required per planning horizon (usually annual). |
| **Ordering Cost (S or Co)** | Fixed cost incurred every time an order is placed (paperwork, transport, inspection setup). |
| **Holding Cost (H or Cc)** | Cost of carrying one unit in stock for one year (storage, insurance, depreciation, capital tied up). |
| **Shortage / Stockout Cost (Cs)** | Penalty when demand cannot be met (lost sales, backorders, lost goodwill). |
| **Lead Time (L)** | Time elapsed between placing an order and receiving it. |
| **Reorder Level (ROL)** | Inventory position at which a fresh order must be triggered. |
| **Safety Stock (SS)** | Buffer inventory held to absorb demand and lead-time variability. |
| **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | The order size that minimises the sum of ordering and holding costs. |
| **P-System** | Continuous-review system: order fixed quantity whenever stock reaches ROL. |
| **Q-System** | Periodic-review system: order variable quantity at fixed time intervals. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Heuristic:** Always state the assumptions of the EOQ model *before* writing its formula. Examiners allocate dedicated marks for the assumption block (constant demand, instantaneous replenishment, no stockouts, independent ordering & holding costs).

### 1.4 The Three Costs Inventory Management Must Balance

1. **Ordering Cost (Co)** — *decreases* as order quantity increases (fewer orders per year).
2. **Holding Cost (Cc)** — *increases* linearly with order quantity (more stock sitting in warehouse).
3. **Shortage Cost (Cs)** — *decreases* with larger safety stock.

The EOQ is the point where the *sum* of ordering and holding costs is at its minimum.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Classical EOQ Model (Wilson, 1934) — Assumptions

- Demand rate (D) is **constant** and **known**.
- Lead time (L) is **constant** and **known**.
- Order is received **instantaneously** (infinite replenishment rate).
- **No stockouts** are permitted (or shortages are negligible).
- Ordering cost (S) is **fixed per order**, independent of Q.
- Holding cost (H) is **linear** in average inventory.
- The item is treated **independently** of other items (no joint ordering constraint).

### 2.2 Building the Total Cost Function

Let Q be the order quantity (units per order). Then:

- Number of orders per year = $D / Q$
- Ordering cost per year = $(D/Q) \cdot S$
- Average inventory = $Q / 2$ (because stock oscillates linearly between Q and 0)
- Holding cost per year = $(Q/2) \cdot H$

Therefore the **Total Relevant Cost (TRC)** is:

$$
TRC(Q) = \underbrace{\frac{DS}{Q}}_{\text{Ordering Cost}} \;+\; \underbrace{\frac{HQ}{2}}_{\text{Holding Cost}}
$$

Purchasing cost ($D \cdot P$, where P is unit price) is constant w.r.t. Q and is excluded when comparing order quantities.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD CHEAT SHEET — Memorise this table before any KTU exam.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Economic Order Quantity (EOQ / Q*) | $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS / H}$ | units |
| 2 | Total Relevant Cost at Q* | $TC^* = \sqrt{2DSH}$ | currency/yr |
| 3 | Optimal Number of orders / yr | $N^* = D / Q^*$ | orders/yr |
| 4 | Cycle Time (Time Between Orders) | $T^* = Q^* / D$ | years |
| 5 | Reorder Point (deterministic) | $ROP = d \cdot L$ | units |
| 6 | Reorder Point (with safety stock) | $ROP = d \cdot L + SS$ | units |
| 7 | Safety Stock — variable demand | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | units |
| 8 | Safety Stock — variable lead time | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_L \cdot d$ | units |
| 9 | Both variable (independent) | $SS = z \cdot \sqrt{L\sigma_d^2 + d^2\sigma_L^2}$ | units |
| 10 | P-System (Continuous) Order-up-to | $S = d(R+L) + SS$ | units |
| 11 | Q-System (Periodic) Order Quantity | $q = D_{R+L} + SS - IP$ | units |
| 12 | EOQ with finite replenishment | $Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{p-d}}$ | p = production rate |
| 13 | EOQ with backorders allowed | $Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{C_c + C_s}{C_s}}$ | Cs = shortage cost |
| 14 | All-units discount TC | $TC = \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{HQ}{2} + DP$ | P = unit price in slab |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conventions used:** `d` = average daily demand, `R` = review period, `L` = lead time, `σ` = standard deviation, `z` = standard-normal service-level factor (e.g., z = 1.65 for 95 %, z = 2.33 for 99 %), `IP` = inventory position at review.

### 2.4 Why EOQ Minimisation is Guaranteed (Unimodality)

The total cost function $TRC(Q) = DS/Q + HQ/2$ is **convex** for $Q > 0$:

- First term $DS/Q$ is convex and decreasing.
- Second term $HQ/2$ is linear.
- Sum is strictly convex, so any stationary point is the **unique global minimum**.

This mathematical guarantee is why the EOQ model is widely used in industry despite its simplifying assumptions.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where These Models Are Used

- **Manufacturing:** Raw-material lot sizing for MRP/ERP systems.
- **Retail:** Replenishment at supermarkets (chips, beverages).
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Vaccine cold-chain planning.
- **Spare parts:** Aircraft, automotive, heavy engineering (high carrying cost, critical stockouts).
- **E-commerce:** Amazon / Flipkart warehouse replenishment, where P-system dominates due to fixed truck schedules.

### 2.6 Comparison: P-System vs. Q-System (Continuous vs. Periodic Review)

> [!TIP]
> **This is a guaranteed 7-mark question in KTU Module 2. Know the trade-offs cold.**

| Feature | P-System (Continuous Review, Q-Model) | Q-System (Periodic Review, P-Model) |
|---|---|---|
| When is stock reviewed? | Continuously (every transaction) | At fixed intervals (R days) |
| Order quantity | Fixed = Q* | Variable (= up-to-level − on-hand) |
| Trigger to order | Inventory hits ROP | Every review instant |
| Safety stock required | Lower (no review-period risk) | Higher (must cover review + lead time) |
| Information system | Needs real-time data | Simpler periodic count suffices |
| Best suited for | High-value items (A-class in ABC analysis) | Group ordering, vendor-managed inventory |
| Typical industry | Spare parts, jewellery, cars | Grocery, FMCG, milk runs |

(Note: Note that some textbooks swap the letters P/Q — KTU follows the convention above.)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Wilson EOQ Formula

We minimise $TRC(Q) = DS/Q + HQ/2$ with respect to $Q > 0$.

**Step 1 — Compute the first derivative.**
Differentiating both terms:

$$
\frac{d}{dQ}\!\left(\frac{DS}{Q}\right) = -\,\frac{DS}{Q^{2}}, \qquad \frac{d}{dQ}\!\left(\frac{HQ}{2}\right) = \frac{H}{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{d\,TRC}{dQ} \;=\; -\,\frac{DS}{Q^{2}} \;+\; \frac{H}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the first-order optimality condition.**
Set the derivative equal to zero for a stationary point:

$$
-\,\frac{DS}{Q^{2}} \;+\; \frac{H}{2} \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 3 — Solve algebraically for Q.**

$$
\frac{H}{2} \;=\; \frac{DS}{Q^{2}}
$$

$$
Q^{2} \;=\; \frac{2DS}{H}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q^{*} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify it is a minimum using the second derivative.**

$$
\frac{d^{2}\,TRC}{dQ^{2}} \;=\; \frac{2DS}{Q^{3}} \;>\; 0 \quad \text{for all } Q>0
$$

Positive second derivative confirms the EOQ is a **global minimum**.

**Step 5 — Substitute Q* back into TRC.**

$$
TRC(Q^{*}) \;=\; \frac{DS}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} \;+\; \frac{H}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}} \;+\; \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,TC^{*} \;=\; \sqrt{2DSH}\,}
$$

---

### 3.2 Numerical Solved Example — Basic EOQ

> **Problem [KTU Model Question]:**
> A manufacturing unit consumes **10,000 units/year** of a raw material. Ordering cost is **₹ 200 per order** and holding cost is **₹ 4 per unit per year**. Determine (a) EOQ, (b) optimal number of orders/year, (c) time between orders, (d) minimum total cost. Lead time is 6 days and the firm works 250 days/year.

**Given:**
$D = 10{,}000$ units, $S = 200$ ₹/order, $H = 4$ ₹/unit-yr, $L = 6$ days, working days = 250.

**Step (a) — Compute EOQ.**

$$
Q^{*} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 200}{4}} \;=\; \sqrt{1{,}000{,}000} \;=\; \boxed{1{,}000 \text{ units}}
$$

**Step (b) — Optimal number of orders per year.**

$$
N^{*} \;=\; \frac{D}{Q^{*}} \;=\; \frac{10{,}000}{1{,}000} \;=\; \boxed{10 \text{ orders/yr}}
$$

**Step (c) — Time between two successive orders.**

$$
T^{*} \;=\; \frac{Q^{*}}{D} \;=\; \frac{1{,}000}{10{,}000} \;=\; 0.1 \text{ yr } \;=\; 0.1 \times 250 \;=\; \boxed{25 \text{ working days}}
$$

**Step (d) — Minimum total relevant cost.**

$$
TC^{*} \;=\; \sqrt{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 200 \times 4} \;=\; \sqrt{16{,}000{,}000} \;=\; \boxed{4{,}000 \text{ ₹/yr}}
$$

**Step (e) — Reorder Point (working days basis).**
Daily demand $d = 10{,}000 / 250 = 40$ units/day.

$$
ROP \;=\; d \cdot L \;=\; 40 \times 6 \;=\; \boxed{240 \text{ units}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Whenever the inventory level falls to **240 units**, place an order for **1,000 units**. Stock arrives after 6 days, by which time consumption is exactly 240 units, leaving zero stockout.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Solved Example — All-Units Quantity Discount Model

> **Problem [KTU Model Question — 14 marks]:**
> Annual demand $D = 5{,}000$ units, ordering cost $S = ₹ 40$/order, holding cost rate $i = 20\%$ of unit price/year. The supplier offers:
>
> | Price break | Range (units) | Unit price (₹) |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 | 0 – 499 | 10.00 |
> | 2 | 500 – 999 | 9.80 |
> | 3 | 1,000 and above | 9.60 |

**Step 1 — Compute EOQ at each price level.**
Holding cost at each price: $H_1 = 0.20 \times 10.00 = 2.00$, $H_2 = 0.20 \times 9.80 = 1.96$, $H_3 = 0.20 \times 9.60 = 1.92$.

$$
Q^{*}_{1} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5{,}000 \times 40}{2.00}} \;=\; \sqrt{200{,}000} \;\approx\; 447 \text{ units}
$$

$$
Q^{*}_{2} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5{,}000 \times 40}{1.96}} \;\approx\; \sqrt{204{,}081} \;\approx\; 452 \text{ units}
$$

$$
Q^{*}_{3} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5{,}000 \times 40}{1.92}} \;\approx\; \sqrt{208{,}333} \;\approx\; 456 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — Check feasibility of each EOQ.**
- $Q^{*}_1 = 447$ → not feasible (needs ≥ 500 for that price).
- $Q^{*}_2 = 452$ → not feasible (needs ≥ 1,000 for that price).
- $Q^{*}_3 = 456$ → feasible for price 3.

Hence, **$Q^{*}_3 = 456$ is a valid candidate**.

**Step 3 — Adjust non-feasible EOQs upward to their minimum qualifying quantity.**

- For price slab 2 → adjust to $Q = 500$ units (lowest quantity for ₹ 9.80).
- For price slab 1 → since EOQ is feasible if we stay in slab 1, we can also compare its TC at 447. But price 1 is strictly higher; we only need to compare the *cheapest candidate from each price tier* and the unadjusted EOQ where feasible.

**Step 4 — Compute Total Cost (including purchase cost) for each candidate.**
Use $TC = (D/Q)S + (Q/2)H + DP$.

**Candidate A — Q = 456 at ₹ 9.60:**

$$
TC_A \;=\; \frac{5{,}000}{456}\times 40 \;+\; \frac{456}{2}\times 1.92 \;+\; 5{,}000 \times 9.60
$$

$$
TC_A \;=\; 438.60 \;+\; 437.76 \;+\; 48{,}000 \;=\; \boxed{₹\;48{,}876.36}
$$

**Candidate B — Q = 500 at ₹ 9.80:**

$$
TC_B \;=\; \frac{5{,}000}{500}\times 40 \;+\; \frac{500}{2}\times 1.96 \;+\; 5{,}000 \times 9.80
$$

$$
TC_B \;=\; 400 \;+\; 490 \;+\; 49{,}000 \;=\; \boxed{₹\;49{,}890.00}
$$

**Candidate C — Q = 1,000 at ₹ 9.60 (highest break):**

$$
TC_C \;=\; \frac{5{,}000}{1{,}000}\times 40 \;+\; \frac{1{,}000}{2}\times 1.92 \;+\; 5{,}000 \times 9.60
$$

$$
TC_C \;=\; 200 \;+\; 960 \;+\; 48{,}000 \;=\; \boxed{₹\;49{,}160.00}
$$

**Step 5 — Select the minimum.**
$\min(TC_A,\; TC_B,\; TC_C) = TC_A = ₹ 48{,}876.36$ at **Q\* = 456 units** and **₹ 9.60/unit**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule (KTU-Standard):** "Order Q* units at the price level where the EOQ is feasible, but always compare against the adjusted quantities at *every* price break."

### 3.4 Numerical Solved Example — Safety Stock & ROL with Variable Demand

> **Problem [KTU Model Question]:**
> Average demand = 100 units/day, standard deviation of daily demand $\sigma_d = 25$ units. Lead time = 10 days. The firm wants a 95 % service level (z = 1.65). Find safety stock and reorder point.

**Step 1 — Standard deviation of demand over lead time.**

$$
\sigma_{L} \;=\; \sigma_d \sqrt{L} \;=\; 25 \sqrt{10} \;\approx\; 25 \times 3.162 \;\approx\; 79.06 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — Safety stock.**

$$
SS \;=\; z \cdot \sigma_{L} \;=\; 1.65 \times 79.06 \;\approx\; \boxed{130.4 \text{ units}}
$$

**Step 3 — Reorder Point.**

$$
ROP \;=\; d \cdot L + SS \;=\; 100 \times 10 + 130.4 \;=\; \boxed{1{,}130.4 \approx 1{,}131 \text{ units}}
$$

**Interpretation:** When stock falls to ~1,131 units, raise a fresh purchase order; the safety buffer covers demand surges during the 10-day lead time.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Model Selection Decision Tree

| If your situation is… | Use this model |
|---|---|
| Constant demand, fixed price, no stockouts | **Basic EOQ** |
| Constant demand, quantity discounts given | **EOQ with all-units / incremental discount** |
| Constant demand, finite production rate p > d | **Production Order Quantity (POQ)** |
| Constant demand, backorders allowed | **EOQ with planned shortages** |
| Variable demand, variable lead time, fixed Q | **Q-System (Continuous review)** |
| Variable demand, periodic count, fixed R | **P-System (Periodic review)** |
| Multiple items from same supplier | **Joint replenishment / Multi-item EOQ** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Saw-Tooth Inventory Profile (Single EOQ Cycle)

The classic EOQ behaviour over time is a "saw-tooth" pattern. Stock arrives at level $Q$, depletes at rate $d$ to zero exactly as the next order arrives.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Time 0: Order arrives, Stock = Q*"] --> B["Stock depletes linearly at rate d"]
    B --> C["Stock hits 0 just as next order arrives"]
    C --> D["New order placed; cycle repeats"]
    D --> A
```

**Schematic (text-rendered):**

```
Inventory Level
   Q* |\
      | \
      |  \   <-- average inventory = Q*/2
      |   \
      |    \
      |     \
    0 |______\______ Time
        T*      2T*
       (cycle length)
```

### 4.2 Q-System (Continuous Review) Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: item withdrawn or received]) --> UpdateIP[Update Inventory Position IP]
    UpdateIP --> Check{IP <= ROP?}
    Check -- No --> Start
    Check -- Yes --> PlaceOrder[Place order of size Q*]
    PlaceOrder --> WaitLead[Wait for Lead Time L]
    WaitLead --> Receive[Receive Q* units, IP increases by Q*]
    Receive --> Start
```

### 4.3 P-System (Periodic Review) Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Begin([Every R days]) --> CountStock[Physical count of on-hand stock]
    CountStock --> ComputeIP[Compute Inventory Position IP]
    ComputeIP --> CalcOrder["Calculate order q = S - IP where S = order-up-to level"]
    CalcOrder --> PlaceOrder[Place order of size q]
    PlaceOrder --> WaitLead[Wait for Lead Time L]
    WaitLead --> Receive[Receive q units]
    Receive --> Begin
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Discount-Model Decision Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[/Demand D, Order S, Holding rate i, Price breaks/] --> Step1[Compute H for each price slab]
    Step1 --> Step2[Compute EOQ at each price level]
    Step2 --> Step3{Feasible EOQ at this price?}
    Step3 -- Yes --> CandidateA[Add EOQ to candidate list]
    Step3 -- No --> Step4[Adjust Q to minimum qualifying quantity]
    Step4 --> CandidateB[Add adjusted Q to candidate list]
    CandidateA --> Step5[Compute TC including purchase cost]
    CandidateB --> Step5
    Step5 --> Step6[Choose minimum TC]
    Step6 --> Output[/Optimal Q* and Price P*/]
```

### 4.5 Decision Matrix — When to Use P vs. Q System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Real-time data available?}
    Q1 -- No --> PSYS[P-System: Periodic Review every R days]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Item value HIGH and demand volatile?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> QCONT[Q-System: Continuous Review, fixed Q*]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Vendor delivers in fixed truck schedule?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> PSYS
    Q3 -- No --> QCONT
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** Define the term *Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)*. List any four assumptions of the basic EOQ model.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
EOQ is the order quantity that minimises the sum of ordering cost and inventory holding cost per year. Mathematically:
$Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$, where $D$ = annual demand, $S$ = ordering cost/order, $H$ = holding cost/unit/year.
Four assumptions:
1. Demand rate is constant and known.
2. Lead time is constant and known.
3. Replenishment is instantaneous.
4. No stockouts are permitted.
*(Any four valid assumptions accepted.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]** Distinguish between P-System and Q-System of inventory control. Which one requires higher safety stock and why?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | P-System (Continuous) | Q-System (Periodic) |
|---|---|---|
| Review | Continuous | Periodic (every R days) |
| Order quantity | Fixed = Q* | Variable = up-to-level − on-hand |
| Safety stock | Lower | Higher (must cover demand during R + L) |

The **P-System requires lower safety stock** because the order is placed the instant inventory reaches ROP; only lead-time variability must be buffered. In the Q-System, additional demand variability over the entire review period R must also be absorbed, inflating SS.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Choice (Internal Selection)

> **Module 2 Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following.

---

#### **Question A (14 marks)** — *(Continuous-Review + Discount model combination)*

**(a) [7 marks]** A factory uses **8,000 units/year** of a component. Ordering cost = **₹ 150/order**, holding cost = **₹ 6/unit/year**. Lead time is **15 days**, and the firm operates **300 days/year**. Determine:
(i) EOQ,
(ii) Number of orders per year,
(iii) Reorder level.

**(b) [7 marks]** Suppose the supplier offers a quantity discount:
- 0–399 units → ₹ 20/unit
- 400–799 units → ₹ 19/unit
- ≥ 800 units → ₹ 18/unit
Holding-cost rate remains 25 % of unit price. Find the **optimal order quantity and total cost**.

---

**Model Solution A(a):**

Given: $D = 8{,}000$, $S = 150$, $H = 6$, $L = 15$ days, working days = 300, so $d = 8000/300 \approx 26.67$ units/day.

**(i) EOQ:**

$$
Q^{*} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8{,}000 \times 150}{6}} \;=\; \sqrt{400{,}000} \;=\; \boxed{632.46 \approx 632 \text{ units}}
$$

**(ii) Number of orders/year:**

$$
N^{*} \;=\; \frac{8{,}000}{632.46} \;\approx\; \boxed{12.65 \text{ orders/yr}}
$$

**(iii) Reorder level:**

$$
ROP \;=\; d \times L \;=\; 26.67 \times 15 \;\approx\; \boxed{400 \text{ units}}
$$

---

**Model Solution A(b):**

Holding costs: $H_1 = 0.25 \times 20 = 5.00$, $H_2 = 0.25 \times 19 = 4.75$, $H_3 = 0.25 \times 18 = 4.50$.

**EOQ at each price:**

$$
Q^{*}_1 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8{,}000 \times 150}{5.00}} = \sqrt{480{,}000} \approx 693 \text{ units (not feasible for slab 1)}
$$

$$
Q^{*}_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8{,}000 \times 150}{4.75}} = \sqrt{505{,}263} \approx 711 \text{ units (feasible for slab 2)}
$$

$$
Q^{*}_3 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8{,}000 \times 150}{4.50}} = \sqrt{533{,}333} \approx 730 \text{ units (feasible for slab 3)}
$$

**Adjust non-feasible:**
- Slab 1 → adjust to minimum feasible Q where price = ₹ 20? Since slab 1 is highest price, only feasible candidates are: $Q = 711$ at ₹ 19, $Q = 730$ at ₹ 18, $Q = 800$ at ₹ 18 (boundary).

**Total Cost at each candidate:**
$TC = (D/Q)S + (Q/2)H + DP$

**Candidate 1 — Q = 711 at ₹ 19:**

$$
TC_1 = \frac{8{,}000}{711}\times 150 + \frac{711}{2}\times 4.75 + 8{,}000 \times 19 = 1687.76 + 1688.63 + 152{,}000 = ₹\;155{,}376.39
$$

**Candidate 2 — Q = 730 at ₹ 18:**

$$
TC_2 = \frac{8{,}000}{730}\times 150 + \frac{730}{2}\times 4.50 + 8{,}000 \times 18 = 1643.84 + 1642.50 + 144{,}000 = ₹\;147{,}286.34
$$

**Candidate 3 — Q = 800 at ₹ 18 (slab boundary):**

$$
TC_3 = \frac{8{,}000}{800}\times 150 + \frac{800}{2}\times 4.50 + 8{,}000 \times 18 = 1500 + 1800 + 144{,}000 = ₹\;147{,}300
$$

**Decision:** $\min(TC_1, TC_2, TC_3) = TC_2$ → **Order $Q^* = 730$ units** at **₹ 18/unit**, giving **Total Cost ≈ ₹ 1,47,286**.

---

#### **Question B (14 marks)** — *(P-System / Q-System + Safety Stock)*

**(a) [7 marks]** For an item used in an assembly plant: average demand = **120 units/day**, standard deviation of daily demand $\sigma_d = 30$ units, lead time = **8 days**, review period = **7 days**, desired service level = **97.5 %** (z = 2.05). Compute:
(i) Safety stock,
(ii) Order-up-to level S (P-System),
(iii) Reorder Point and EOQ assuming S = ₹ 80/order, H = ₹ 5/unit-year, D = 120 × 300 = 36,000 units/yr.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare the P-system and Q-system for this item and explain why the Q-system needs a larger safety stock. Show the ROP-vs-T relationship numerically.

---

**Model Solution B(a):**

**(i) Safety stock for P-System (must cover review + lead time variability):**

$$
\sigma_{R+L} = \sigma_d \sqrt{R+L} = 30 \sqrt{15} = 30 \times 3.873 = 116.19
$$

$$
SS = z \cdot \sigma_{R+L} = 2.05 \times 116.19 \approx \boxed{238.2 \text{ units}}
$$

**(ii) Order-up-to level S (P-System):**

$$
S = d(R+L) + SS = 120 \times 15 + 238.2 = 1800 + 238.2 = \boxed{2038.2 \approx 2039 \text{ units}}
$$

**(iii) Reorder Point and EOQ for Q-System:**

Demand during lead time only:

$$
\sigma_L = 30\sqrt{8} = 30 \times 2.828 = 84.85
$$

$$
SS_{Q\text{-sys}} = 2.05 \times 84.85 \approx 173.9 \text{ units}
$$

$$
ROP = d \cdot L + SS_{Q\text{-sys}} = 120 \times 8 + 173.9 = 960 + 173.9 = \boxed{1133.9 \approx 1134 \text{ units}}
$$

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 36{,}000 \times 80}{5}} = \sqrt{1{,}152{,}000} \approx \boxed{1073 \text{ units}}
$$

---

**Model Solution B(b):**

In the **Q-system (continuous review)**, the only demand uncertainty to be absorbed is during the **lead time L = 8 days**. Hence $SS_Q = z \sigma_d \sqrt{L} = 174$ units.

In the **P-system (periodic review)**, demand can fluctuate during **both the review period R = 7 days and the lead time L = 8 days**, i.e., over a horizon of **R + L = 15 days**. Hence $SS_P = z \sigma_d \sqrt{R+L} = 238$ units — about **37 % larger** than the Q-system SS.

Therefore, the P-system requires a larger safety stock because it must protect against stockouts that could arise from a surge in demand anywhere within the combined R + L window, whereas the Q-system triggers immediately when stock touches ROP and only needs to cover the L window.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Module 2 — Inventory)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert annual demand into daily demand** before computing ROL. Always write $d = D / \text{working days}$ explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing P-system with Q-system** in the safety-stock formula. P-system uses $\sqrt{R+L}$; Q-system uses only $\sqrt{L}$. This alone costs 3 marks.
> 3. **In discount models**, students often stop after computing EOQ and ignore the feasibility check against price-break quantities. Always list *every* candidate and compute TC including **purchase cost**.
> 4. **Units mismatch** — using ₹ in EOQ numerator but $/yr in holding cost. State units next to every symbol.
> 5. **Confusing average inventory** $Q/2$ with maximum $Q$. Maximum is $Q$; average is $Q/2$. Holding cost uses *average*, not maximum.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** before writing the EOQ formula — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark per missing assumption (out of the 7-mark sub-part).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> **The 30-Sec Revision Punch-List**

- [x] **EOQ formula**: $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — Wilson (1934), derivable by setting $dTC/dQ = 0$.
- [x] **Minimum TC**: $TC^* = \sqrt{2DSH}$ — handy for sanity checks.
- [x] **Cycle time**: $T = Q^*/D$ (years) — convert to days using working days.
- [x] **Deterministic ROP**: $ROP = d \cdot L$.
- [x] **Stochastic ROP**: $ROP = d \cdot L + z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ (Q-system) **OR** $d(R+L) + z\sigma_d\sqrt{R+L}$ (P-system).
- [x] **Discount Model Procedure**: (i) Compute EOQ at each price. (ii) Adjust infeasible EOQs up to minimum qualifying quantity. (iii) Compute TC at every candidate. (iv) Pick minimum.
- [x] **P-System = Continuous Review, Fixed Order Q**; **Q-System = Periodic Review, Variable Order q**.
- [x] **Safety stock ordering**: $SS_P > SS_Q$ because P must cover $\sqrt{R+L}$ while Q only $\sqrt{L}$.
- [x] **Assumptions to memorise**: constant demand, fixed lead time, instantaneous replenishment, no stockouts, fixed ordering cost, linear holding cost, independent items.
- [x] **Holding-cost conversion**: $H = i \cdot P \cdot C$ where $i$ = carrying rate, $P$ = unit price, $C$ = cost per unit per year (usually equals 1 in textbook problems).
- [x] **Real-world tie-in**: ABC analysis, Just-in-Time (JIT), Material Requirements Planning (MRP), Economic Production Quantity (EPQ).
- [x] **Always include the purchase cost** when comparing alternatives with different unit prices — a common mistake is comparing only the relevant (ordering + holding) cost.

> **One-Line Takeaway:**
> *Inventory optimisation is the equilibrium between the cost of ordering (favours large Q) and the cost of carrying (favours small Q) — the EOQ is the bridge; discounts and uncertainty reshape that bridge's span.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selective Inventory Control Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET601 - INDUSTRIAL AND SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Selective Inventory Control Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Selective Inventory Control (SIC)** is a systematic classification and management strategy in which inventory items are grouped into distinct categories based on specific criteria — such as annual consumption value, criticality, usage rate, procurement difficulty, unit price, or demand pattern — and different levels of managerial attention, control effort, safety stock, and review frequency are applied to each category.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept:** "Not all items in a warehouse deserve the same attention. Selective control means we apply the *80/20 rule* — focus rigorous control on the vital few and relaxed control on the trivial many."

The philosophy rests on the principle of **management by exception**, where resources (time, money, manpower) are concentrated on items that contribute most significantly to inventory investment or operational risk.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Wardrobe" Intuition

Imagine a college student's wardrobe:

- **Expensive silk shirts & leather jacket** → Ironed carefully, dry-cleaned, stored in covers, insured. *(Treated like 'A' items.)*
- **Regular cotton shirts & jeans** → Machine-washed, folded neatly, used daily. *(Treated like 'B' items.)*
- **Old t-shirts & shorts** → Worn casually, washed roughly, hardly tracked. *(Treated like 'C' items.)*

The student intuitively practices **selective control** — high effort on a few valuable pieces, low effort on many inexpensive ones. Industrial SIC works exactly the same way at an organizational scale.

---

## 1.3 Why Selective Inventory Control?

| Reason | Explanation |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Cost Reduction** | Carrying cost is reduced because low-value items are not over-managed. |
| **Resource Optimization** | Managers' time and effort are directed at the critical few items. |
| **Service Level Improvement** | Vital/expensive items are always available, reducing stockouts. |
| **Better Working Capital** | Capital is not locked up unnecessarily in non-critical items. |
| **Risk Mitigation** | Critical spares are identified and controlled to prevent downtime. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlight:** Selective Inventory Control is classified as a *qualitative* inventory model — unlike EOQ, EPQ, or ROP models, it does **not** require cost data; it relies on classification logic. This makes it highly popular in KTU 3-mark short-answer questions.

---

## 1.4 Major Selective Inventory Control Techniques

The standard set covered under KTU PCMET601 Module 2 includes:

1. **ABC Analysis** (Always Better Control) — based on **Annual Consumption Value (ACV)**
2. **VED Analysis** (Vital, Essential, Desirable) — based on **Criticality of the item**
3. **FSN Analysis** (Fast, Slow, Non-moving) — based on **Usage / Issue Rate**
4. **SDE Analysis** (Scarce, Difficult, Easy) — based on **Procurement Difficulty**
5. **HML Analysis** (High, Medium, Low) — based on **Unit Price**
6. **GOLF Analysis** (Government, Ordinary, Local, Foreign) — based on **Source of Supply**
7. **SOS Analysis** (Seasonal, Off-Seasonal) — based on **Demand Pattern**
8. **XYZ Analysis** — based on **Inventory Holding Value**

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** *"A Very Funny Student Handles General Subjects Optimally & Seasonally"* → **A**BC, **V**ED, **F**SN, **S**DE, **H**ML, **G**OLF, **S**OS.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Seven (or Eight) Standard Techniques — Detailed Analysis

### 2.1.1 ABC Analysis (Always Better Control)

**Origin:** Derived from **Vilfredo Pareto's 80/20 Principle** (1906) and popularized by **H. F. Dickie** (1951, General Electric) for inventory.

**Concept:** A small percentage of items typically account for a large percentage of total inventory investment.

- **'A' items:** ~10–20% of total SKUs contributing ~70–80% of annual consumption value.
- **'B' items:** ~20–30% of SKUs contributing ~15–25% of annual consumption value.
- **'C' items:** ~50–70% of SKUs contributing ~5–10% of annual consumption value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Control Policy for KTU Exam:**
> - **A** → Tight control, frequent review (monthly/weekly), accurate records, low safety stock, close follow-up.
> - **B** → Moderate control, quarterly review, reasonable records.
> - **C** → Loose control, annual or biannual review, bulk ordering, simple two-bin system.

---

### 2.1.2 VED Analysis (Vital, Essential, Desirable)

**Used in:** Maintenance, spare parts, and production-critical inventory (especially in manufacturing/process industries).

| Category | Meaning | Control Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **V — Vital** | Production halts if item is unavailable. | Always maintain high safety stock, multiple vendors, express procurement. |
| **E — Essential** | Production is seriously affected; partial loss of output. | Moderate safety stock, regular review, planned orders. |
| **D — Desirable** | Production is not critically affected. | Minimum stock, bulk ordering, long lead time acceptable. |

> [!NOTE]
> VED is **always applied together with ABC** in KTU problems — the result is a 3×3 **ABC-VED Matrix** used to prioritize spare parts.

---

### 2.1.3 FSN Analysis (Fast, Slow, Non-moving)

Based on the **issue / consumption rate** from stores over a defined period (e.g., 12 months).

- **F — Fast Moving:** Issue rate is high. Continuous review.
- **S — Slow Moving:** Issue rate is low. Periodic review.
- **N — Non-moving:** No issue in the last 12 months. Candidates for **disposal, scrapping, or write-off**.

---

### 2.1.4 SDE Analysis (Scarce, Difficult, Easy)

Based on **procurement lead time and availability**.

- **S — Scarce:** Imported, monopolistic supply, very long lead time → carry maximum buffer stock.
- **D — Difficult:** Indigenous but few vendors or irregular supply → moderate buffer.
- **E — Easy:** Many vendors, short lead time, readily available → minimum buffer.

---

### 2.1.5 HML Analysis (High, Medium, Low)

Categorization based on **unit price of the item**.

- **H — High Unit Price:** Strict control, locked bins, individual records.
- **M — Medium Unit Price:** Moderate control, periodic stock-taking.
- **L — Low Unit Price:** Loose control, bulk storage, simple records.

> [!NOTE]
> HML is essentially a *cost-emphasis variant* of ABC, but applied per-unit rather than per-consumption-value.

---

### 2.1.6 GOLF Analysis (Government, Ordinary, Local, Foreign)

Categorization by **source of procurement** — important in public-sector and government-audited industries.

- **G — Government:** Items supplied by government agencies (e.g., ordnance factories).
- **O — Ordinary:** Items available in the open market from multiple vendors.
- **L — Local:** Items procured from local suppliers near the plant.
- **F — Foreign:** Imported items requiring foreign exchange, customs, longer lead times.

---

### 2.1.7 SOS Analysis (Seasonal, Off-Seasonal)

Based on **demand periodicity** — items demanded only in specific seasons (e.g., air-conditioners, woollens, fertilizers, harvest equipment).

- **S — Seasonal:** Demand peaks in a specific period → build inventory well in advance.
- **OS — Off-Seasonal:** Demand is uniform throughout the year.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Technique | Classification Basis | Category Labels | Key Parameter (Formula) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **ABC** | Annual Consumption Value | A, B, C | $\text{ACV} = \text{Annual Demand} \times \text{Unit Price}$ |
| **VED** | Criticality to Production | Vital, Essential, Desirable | No numeric formula — qualitative |
| **FSN** | Issue / Usage Rate | Fast, Slow, Non-moving | $\text{Issue Rate} = \frac{\text{Units Issued}}{\text{Period}}$ |
| **SDE** | Procurement Lead Time | Scarce, Difficult, Easy | $L_T$ (Lead Time) — qualitative threshold |
| **HML** | Unit Price | High, Medium, Low | $P_{\text{unit}}$ — bucketed by price band |
| **GOLF** | Source of Supply | Government, Ordinary, Local, Foreign | No formula — sourcing policy |
| **SOS** | Demand Pattern | Seasonal, Off-Seasonal | No formula — seasonal index |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** ABC uses **Annual Consumption Value (ACV) = Demand × Price**, **NOT** just unit price and **NOT** just demand. Many students lose marks by confusing ABC with HML.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Plants:** ABC–VED matrix determines spare-parts criticality for assembly lines.
- **Hospitals:** ABC–VED–SDE combined for medical supply management (pharma + consumables + implants).
- **Automotive Industry:** SOS analysis governs tyre, battery, and AC inventory planning.
- **Defence / PSUs:** GOLF analysis is mandatory for procurement audit compliance.
- **Retail Chains:** FSN drives SKU rationalization and dead-stock liquidation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 14-mark problems, you are frequently asked to construct an **ABC–VED Matrix** (3 × 3 grid) and recommend the control policy for each cell (e.g., AA, AV, CV, CD, etc.). Mastery of this matrix is non-negotiable.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS, WORKED NUMERICALS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 ABC Analysis — Detailed Numerical Procedure

**Standard ABC Classification Procedure (as expected in KTU 14-mark problems):**

1. List all inventory items.
2. Compute the **Annual Demand (D)** in units for each item.
3. Compute the **Unit Price (P)** for each item.
4. Calculate **Annual Consumption Value = D × P** for each item.
5. Sort all items in **descending order** of ACV.
6. Compute **% of ACV** for each item and **cumulative % ACV**.
7. Apply the cutoff:
   - Cumulative up to ~**75%** → **A items**
   - Cumulative from ~**75% to ~95%** → **B items**
   - Cumulative from ~**95% to 100%** → **C items**
8. Apply differential control policies.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — ABC Classification (Typical KTU 14-mark format)

### **Problem Statement:**

A manufacturing firm maintains 8 inventory items. Annual demand and unit price data are given below. Classify the items into A, B, C categories using ABC analysis and recommend a control policy for each.

| Item Code | Annual Demand (units) | Unit Price (₹) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| P1 | 4000 | 50 |
| P2 | 500 | 500 |
| P3 | 100 | 4000 |
| P4 | 2000 | 75 |
| P5 | 100 | 3000 |
| P6 | 800 | 200 |
| P7 | 6000 | 25 |
| P8 | 300 | 600 |

### **Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Annual Consumption Value (ACV) = Demand × Unit Price**

- P1: $4000 \times 50 = 2{,}00{,}000$
- P2: $500 \times 500 = 2{,}50{,}000$
- P3: $100 \times 4000 = 4{,}00{,}000$
- P4: $2000 \times 75 = 1{,}50{,}000$
- P5: $100 \times 3000 = 3{,}00{,}000$
- P6: $800 \times 200 = 1{,}60{,}000$
- P7: $6000 \times 25 = 1{,}50{,}000$
- P8: $300 \times 600 = 1{,}80{,}000$

**Step 2 — Compute Total ACV and Sort in Descending Order**

Total ACV = $200000 + 250000 + 400000 + 150000 + 300000 + 160000 + 150000 + 180000 = 17{,}90{,}000$

Sorted Descending Order:

| Rank | Item | ACV (₹) | % of Total ACV | Cumulative % |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | P3 | 4,00,000 | 22.35% | 22.35% |
| 2 | P5 | 3,00,000 | 16.76% | 39.11% |
| 3 | P2 | 2,50,000 | 13.97% | 53.08% |
| 4 | P1 | 2,00,000 | 11.17% | 64.25% |
| 5 | P8 | 1,80,000 | 10.06% | 74.31% |
| 6 | P6 | 1,60,000 | 8.94% | 83.25% |
| 7 | P4 | 1,50,000 | 8.38% | 91.63% |
| 8 | P7 | 1,50,000 | 8.38% | 100.00% |

**Step 3 — Apply Cutoff Thresholds (A ≤ 75%, B = 75–95%, C ≥ 95%)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cumulative} &\leq 75\% \rightarrow A \\
75\% < \text{Cumulative} &\leq 95\% \rightarrow B \\
\text{Cumulative} &> 95\% \rightarrow C
\end{aligned}$$

- **A items (Cumulative up to ~75%):** P3, P5, P2, P1, P8 → 5 items (62.5% of SKU count, 74.31% of value).
- **B items (Cumulative 75% to 95%):** P6, P4 → 2 items (25% of SKU count, 17.32% of value).
- **C items (Cumulative > 95%):** P7 → 1 item (12.5% of SKU count, 8.38% of value).

**Step 4 — Recommend Control Policy**

| Category | Items | Control Policy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **A** | P3, P5, P2, P1, P8 | Tight control; continuous review; accurate perpetual records; low safety stock; EOQ ordering; close vendor follow-up; monthly review. |
| **B** | P6, P4 | Moderate control; periodic review (quarterly); reorder point system; moderate safety stock. |
| **C** | P7 | Loose control; biannual review; two-bin system; bulk ordering; high safety stock acceptable. |

---

## 3.3 ABC–VED Combined Matrix (3 × 3 Analytical Grid)

**This is the most frequently asked 14-mark extension in KTU.**

When a firm classifies the same item by **both** consumption value (ABC) **and** criticality (VED), a 3 × 3 matrix results:

| ABC \ VED | **V (Vital)** | **E (Essential)** | **D (Desirable)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **A (High Value)** | **AV — Top Priority:** Strict control, no stockout, dual sourcing | **AE — High Priority:** Tight EOQ control | **AD — Monitor closely, periodic review** |
| **B (Medium Value)** | **BV — High Priority despite moderate value** | **BE — Standard EOQ-ROP control** | **BD — Loose control, quarterly review** |
| **C (Low Value)** | **CV — Maintain buffer stock despite low value** | **CE — Reorder when needed** | **CD — Minimum attention, bulk orders** |

**Key Insight for KTU Exam:**

> The diagonal pairs **CV, CE, CD** are often the most *debatable* — a low-value but vital item (CV) must be kept in stock regardless of cost, contradicting pure ABC logic. This is exactly **why VED is added to ABC** — to prevent discarding of low-value critical spares.

---

## 3.4 FSN Numerical Illustration (3-Mark Format)

**Problem:** In a 12-month review period, 5 items had the following issue quantities: 2400, 1200, 600, 150, 0 units. Classify them into F, S, N.

**Solution Approach:**

- **F (Fast-moving):** Issue rate > 1000 units/year → Item 1 (2400), Item 2 (1200)
- **S (Slow-moving):** Issue rate 100–1000 units/year → Item 3 (600), Item 4 (150)
- **N (Non-moving):** No issue in 12 months → Item 5 (0)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb for FSN thresholds:** F ≥ 1000, S = 100–1000, N < 100 OR no issue in 12 months. (The exact threshold may vary; the *principle* of tiered review is the focus.)

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Techniques at a Glance

| Technique | Question It Answers | Decision Made | Control Intensity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| ABC | *How much money is locked in this item?* | Prioritize by value | A → High, B → Medium, C → Low |
| VED | *What happens if the item is out of stock?* | Prioritize by criticality | V → High, E → Medium, D → Low |
| FSN | *How frequently is the item issued?* | Identify obsolete items | F → High, S → Medium, N → Low |
| SDE | *How hard is it to procure the item?* | Set safety stock level | S → High, D → Medium, E → Low |
| HML | *What is the unit price of the item?* | Decide storage & security | H → High, M → Medium, L → Low |
| GOLF | *Where is the item sourced from?* | Define procurement policy | G → High, F → High, O → Medium, L → Low |
| SOS | *Is demand seasonal or uniform?* | Plan production & stocking | S → High (build ahead), OS → Low |
