# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define ABC analysis. State its significance in inventory management.**

`[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

ABC analysis is a selective inventory control technique based on the **Pareto 80/20 principle**, in which inventory items are classified into three groups — A, B, and C — according to their **Annual Consumption Value (ACV = Annual Demand × Unit Price)**.

- **'A' items** (~10–20% of items) account for ~70–80% of total consumption value → require **tight control, continuous review, and accurate records**.
- **'B' items** (~20–30% of items) account for ~15–25% of value → require **moderate control**.
- **'C' items** (~50–70% of items) account for only ~5–10% of value → require **loose control and bulk ordering**.

**Significance:** It focuses managerial attention on the vital few items, reduces carrying cost, optimizes working capital, and improves service levels for high-value stock. *(Full 3 marks awarded.)*

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between ABC and VED analysis of inventory.**

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | ABC Analysis | VED Analysis |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Basis of Classification** | Annual Consumption Value (Demand × Price) | Criticality of item to production |
| **Categories** | A, B, C | Vital, Essential, Desirable |
| **Focus** | Monetary value of inventory | Operational impact of stockout |
| **Application Area** | General inventory, finished goods, raw material | Spare parts and maintenance stores |
| **Numerical Computation** | Yes — sorting and cumulative % required | No — judgment-based classification |
| **Complementary Use** | Often combined with VED to form ABC–VED matrix | Often combined with ABC for comprehensive control |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### **Question A: ABC Analysis with Numerical Classification**

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of ABC analysis. State the criteria for classification and the control policy for each class. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The following data relate to 10 items in a store. Carry out ABC analysis and recommend a suitable control policy. *(7 marks)*

| Item | Annual Usage (units) | Unit Cost (₹) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 200 | 10 |
| 2 | 90 | 200 |
| 3 | 50 | 100 |
| 4 | 500 | 20 |
| 5 | 600 | 8 |
| 6 | 1000 | 5 |
| 7 | 80 | 250 |
| 8 | 150 | 50 |
| 9 | 300 | 30 |
| 10 | 400 | 15 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Concept (7 marks):**

ABC analysis classifies inventory based on the **Pareto principle (80/20 rule)**. Items are sorted by **Annual Consumption Value (ACV = Annual Demand × Unit Price)** in descending order, and cumulative percentage is computed.

- **A items:** Top ~10–20% of items capturing ~70–80% of value → strict control, continuous review, low safety stock, EOQ-based ordering, accurate perpetual records.
- **B items:** Next ~20–30% of items capturing ~15–25% of value → moderate control, periodic (quarterly) review, ROP system.
- **C items:** Bottom ~50–70% of items capturing ~5–10% of value → loose control, two-bin system, bulk ordering, annual review.

**Advantages:** Focused managerial effort, reduced carrying cost, better working capital, fewer stockouts of critical items.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute ACV (Annual Usage × Unit Cost):**

- Item 1: $200 \times 10 = 2{,}000$
- Item 2: $90 \times 200 = 18{,}000$
- Item 3: $50 \times 100 = 5{,}000$
- Item 4: $500 \times 20 = 10{,}000$
- Item 5: $600 \times 8 = 4{,}800$
- Item 6: $1000 \times 5 = 5{,}000$
- Item 7: $80 \times 250 = 20{,}000$
- Item 8: $150 \times 50 = 7{,}500$
- Item 9: $300 \times 30 = 9{,}000$
- Item 10: $400 \times 15 = 6{,}000$

**Total ACV** = $2000 + 18000 + 5000 + 10000 + 4800 + 5000 + 20000 + 7500 + 9000 + 6000 = 87{,}300$

**Step 2 — Sort in Descending Order and Compute Cumulative %:**

| Rank | Item | ACV (₹) | % of Total | Cumulative % | Class |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 7 | 20,000 | 22.91% | 22.91% | **A** |
| 2 | 2 | 18,000 | 20.62% | 43.53% | **A** |
| 3 | 4 | 10,000 | 11.45% | 54.98% | **A** |
| 4 | 9 | 9,000 | 10.31% | 65.29% | **A** |
| 5 | 8 | 7,500 | 8.59% | 73.88% | **A** |
| 6 | 10 | 6,000 | 6.87% | 80.75% | **B** |
| 7 | 3 | 5,000 | 5.73% | 86.48% | **B** |
| 8 | 6 | 5,000 | 5.73% | 92.21% | **B** |
| 9 | 5 | 4,800 | 5.50% | 97.71% | **C** |
| 10 | 1 | 2,000 | 2.29% | 100.00% | **C** |

**Step 3 — Classify and Recommend Policy:**

- **A class (Items 7, 2, 4, 9, 8):** 50% of SKU count, ~73.88% of value → tight control, continuous review, EOQ, low safety stock.
- **B class (Items 10, 3, 6):** 30% of SKU count, ~18.33% of value → moderate control, quarterly review, ROP system.
- **C class (Items 5, 1):** 20% of SKU count, ~7.79% of value → loose control, two-bin, bulk ordering, annual review.

---

### **Question B: ABC–VED Matrix and Combined Classification**

`[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`

**(a)** What is VED analysis? How is it different from ABC analysis? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A company has classified its 9 spare parts into A, B, C (by value) and V, E, D (by criticality) as given below. Construct the ABC–VED matrix and suggest the control policy for each cell. *(7 marks)*

| Item | ABC Class | VED Class |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| S1 | A | V |
| S2 | A | E |
| S3 | A | D |
| S4 | B | V |
| S5 | B | E |
| S6 | B | D |
| S7 | C | V |
| S8 | C | E |
| S9 | C | D |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) VED Analysis (7 marks):**

VED analysis is a qualitative selective inventory control technique where items are classified based on the **criticality of their stockout** to production operations:

- **V (Vital):** Production comes to a halt if unavailable. Always maintain safety stock; emergency procurement procedures.
- **E (Essential):** Production is impaired but not stopped. Significant loss of efficiency.
- **D (Desirable):** Production can continue without serious loss. Disruption is tolerable.

**Difference from ABC:**

| Parameter | ABC | VED |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Criterion | Monetary value | Operational impact |
| Type | Quantitative | Qualitative |
| Decision | Storage & review | Stockout risk management |
| Application | All inventory | Mainly spares/maintenance |

**(b) ABC–VED Matrix and Control Policy (7 marks):**

| ABC \ VED | **V (Vital)** | **E (Essential)** | **D (Desirable)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **A (High Value)** | **S1 — AV:** Top priority. Strict perpetual records, dual sourcing, maximum safety stock, continuous review. | **S2 — AE:** High priority. EOQ with safety stock, weekly review. | **S3 — AD:** Moderate priority. Periodic review, ROP system. |
| **B (Medium Value)** | **S4 — BV:** High priority despite medium value. Mandatory buffer stock, fixed reorder interval. | **S5 — BE:** Standard EOQ-ROP control, monthly review. | **S6 — BD:** Quarterly review, two-bin system, moderate stock. |
| **C (Low Value)** | **S7 — CV:** Maintain adequate safety stock despite low value; risk-based stocking. | **S8 — CE:** Reorder when stock reaches minimum; economic lot size. | **S9 — CD:** Lowest priority. Bulk orders, two-bin, annual review. |

**Conclusion:** The diagonal combinations (AV, BE, CD) are intuitive, while **CV and BV** warrant special attention — they are *critical items with low/medium value* that must not be deprioritized purely on monetary grounds. This matrix provides a **balanced, hybrid control strategy** that ABC alone cannot offer.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in Selective Inventory Control Questions:**
>
> 1. **Sorting Order Error:** Forgetting to sort in *descending* order of ACV before computing cumulative % — leads to completely wrong classification. **Lose 2–3 marks instantly.**
> 2. **ACV Formula Error:** Using unit price *only* (HML logic) or demand *only* instead of *Demand × Price*. **Lose 1–2 marks.**
> 3. **Threshold Mis-application:** Marking 60% of value as 'A' cutoff instead of 70–80%. Examiner expects the **Pareto-aligned ranges** (A ≈ 75%, B ≈ 95% as cumulative cutoffs).
> 4. **Skipping the Control Policy:** Many students end the numerical without writing the *control policy* for each class. Examiner specifically allots **2 marks for control policy** in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 5. **Confusing VED with ABC:** Writing "V = High value, A = Vital" — VED has *no relation* to value. V stands for Vital, not Valuable.
> 6. **Not Mentioning the Pareto Principle:** ABC analysis is fundamentally a *Pareto-derived* technique. Missing the mention of "Pareto 80/20" loses the 1 mark for "concept."
> 7. **In ABC–VED Matrix:** Failing to highlight that **CV items must still be buffered** despite low value — this is the *core insight* of the combined matrix. **Lose 1–2 marks of examiner's application credit.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Selective Inventory Control (KTU PCMET601 Module 2):**

- **Core Idea:** Apply *differential* managerial effort to inventory items based on a chosen criterion — **management by exception**.

- **ABC = Pareto 80/20 Rule:** A few items hold most of the value. A items: ~10–20% SKUs, ~70–80% value. B: 20–30% SKUs, 15–25% value. C: 50–70% SKUs, 5–10% value.

- **ACV Formula (MUST MEMORIZE):**
$$\text{ACV} = \text{Annual Demand (units)} \times \text{Unit Price (₹)}$$

- **Cutoff Thresholds (MUST MEMORIZE):**
  - Cumulative % ≤ 75% → **A**
  - 75% < Cumulative % ≤ 95% → **B**
  - Cumulative % > 95% → **C**

- **VED = Criticality, not Value.** V = Vital (production stoppage), E = Essential (impaired), D = Desirable (tolerable disruption).

- **ABC–VED Matrix is the MOST EXPECTED 14-mark question.** Practice constructing it and writing a *distinct control policy* for each of the 9 cells.

- **FSN:** F = Fast moving, S = Slow moving, N = Non-moving (no issue in 12 months → scrap/dispose candidates).

- **SDE:** Scarce (longest buffer), Difficult (medium buffer), Easy (minimum buffer).

- **HML:** Categorization by *unit price* — High, Medium, Low. **Do not confuse with ABC** (which uses consumption value, not unit price alone).

- **GOLF:** Government, Ordinary, Local, Foreign — used in **PSU and government procurement audits**.

- **SOS:** Seasonal (build ahead) vs. Off-Seasonal (uniform). Common in **agriculture, fertilizers, AC/heater industries**.

- **Most Important Insight for KTU:** ABC alone is *insufficient* for spare parts. Combining with VED prevents the dangerous neglect of low-value critical spares. **Always mention this synergy in your conclusion.**

- **Examiner Traps to Avoid:** (1) Wrong sorting order, (2) ACV formula misuse, (3) Skipping control policy, (4) Confusing HML with ABC, (5) Not mentioning Pareto principle.

- **One-Line Viva Answer:** *"Selective inventory control classifies items using criteria like value, criticality, usage, or lead time, and applies tiered control — focusing managerial effort where it matters most."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: JIT and Supply Chain Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET601 - INDUSTRIAL AND SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** JIT and Supply Chain Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Just-In-Time (JIT) — Formal Definition

**Just-In-Time (JIT)** is a Japanese production philosophy pioneered by **Taiichi Ohno** at Toyota Motor Corporation (post-World War II) that seeks to produce and deliver the right items, in the right quantity, at the right time, **eliminating all forms of waste (Muda)** in the production process. JIT is fundamentally a **pull-based manufacturing system** where production is triggered by actual customer demand rather than forecasted demand.

Mathematically, the core objective of JIT can be expressed as:

$$\text{JIT Goal} = \lim_{t \to 0} \left( \text{Inventory}(t) \right) = 0$$

while simultaneously maintaining:

$$\text{Throughput} \geq \text{Customer Demand Rate}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** JIT is a demand-driven production strategy that integrates suppliers, production, and logistics to deliver products exactly when needed, minimizing inventory carrying costs and eliminating the seven classical wastes (TIMWOOD).

## 1.2 Supply Chain Management (SCM) — Formal Definition

**Supply Chain Management (SCM)** is the systemic, strategic coordination and integration of all business functions and processes both within and across organizations to produce a seamless flow of materials, information, and finances from **supplier → manufacturer → distributor → retailer → end customer**.

The Council of Supply Chain Management Professionals (CSCMP) defines SCM as:

> *"The planning and management of all activities involved in sourcing, procurement, conversion, and logistics management. Importantly, it also includes coordination and collaboration with channel partners, which can be suppliers, intermediaries, third-party service providers, and customers."*

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — JIT as a Sushi Restaurant:** Imagine a high-end sushi restaurant where the chef does not pre-make 200 sushi pieces in the morning "just in case" customers arrive. Instead, each piece is prepared **only when a customer orders it**. The rice is cooked in small batches every 30 minutes. The fish is delivered fresh from the market daily. The result? Zero wastage, maximum freshness, lowest storage cost. This is **JIT in action** — the customer's order is the "Kanban signal" that triggers production.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — SCM as a Relay Race:** A supply chain is like a **4×100m relay race** where the baton (product) must pass smoothly from Runner 1 (Supplier) → Runner 2 (Manufacturer) → Runner 3 (Distributor) → Runner 4 (Customer). If any runner drops the baton (delay, defect, disruption), the entire team loses. The coach (Supply Chain Manager) ensures perfect synchronization, baton handoffs (information flow), and stamina (inventory buffers) for the team to win.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR)** = $\frac{\text{Cost of Goods Sold}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ — **Target in JIT: Maximize**
- **Lead Time (LT)** = $\frac{\text{Total Cycle Time}}{\text{1 unit}}$ — **Target in JIT: Minimize**
- **On-Time Delivery (OTD) %** — **Target in JIT: 100%**
- **First Pass Yield (FPY)** — **Target in JIT: 100%**
- **Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time (C2C)** — **Target in JIT: Minimize**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The integration of JIT into a wider SCM framework is a high-weightage topic. Students must understand that JIT is **a tool within SCM**, not a replacement for it. JIT focuses on **internal operations efficiency**, while SCM addresses **end-to-end value chain optimization**.

## 1.5 Historical Context (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Year | Milestone | Contributor |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| 1950s | Toyota Production System (TPS) birth | Taiichi Ohno |
| 1970s | Oil Crisis — Western companies adopt JIT | Multiple OEMs |
| 1980s | JIT popularized in USA & Europe | MIT Sloan Studies |
| 1990s | Evolution into Lean Manufacturing | Womack & Jones |
| 2000s | Integration with SCM & ERP systems | Global corporations |
| 2020s | Industry 4.0 + JIT + SCM integration | Smart factories |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Seven Wastes (TIMWOOD) — Heart of JIT

Taiichi Ohno identified **seven original wastes** that JIT seeks to eliminate. The acronym **TIMWOOD** is the KTU-preferred mnemonic.

| Letter | Waste (English) | Waste (Japanese) | Description | KTU Example |
|--------|-----------------|------------------|-------------|-------------|
| **T** | **Transportation** | **Unnecessary transport** | Moving materials/products without adding value | Excess material handling between distant work centers |
| **I** | **Inventory** | **Excess stock** | Raw materials, WIP, finished goods beyond need | Storing 6 months of raw material "just in case" |
| **M** | **Motion** | **Unnecessary movement** | Worker or equipment motion that adds no value | Bending, walking, reaching for tools repeatedly |
| **W** | **Waiting** | **Idle time** | People or machines idle due to imbalances | Machine downtime waiting for material delivery |
| **O** | **Overproduction** | **Excess production** | Producing more than customer demand or before needed | The "worst" waste as it generates all other wastes |
| **O** | **Over-processing** | **Excess processing** | Doing more work than the customer requires | Excessive polishing, redundant inspection steps |
| **D** | **Defects** | **Defective products** | Production of defective parts requiring rework/scrap | Rework due to faulty welding in automobile assembly |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Update:** Many modern textbooks add an **8th waste — Skills/Utilization of Talent** (not utilizing employees' full potential) and a **9th waste — Sustainability/Waste of Energy**. Taiichi Ohno's original 7 remain the KTU exam standard.

## 2.2 The Five Core Principles of JIT

1. **Zero Inventory Philosophy** — Inventory is treated as a liability, not an asset. It hides problems.
2. **Continuous Flow (One-Piece Flow)** — Move items through production one piece at a time, not in batches.
3. **Pull System (Kanban)** — Produce only when downstream signals demand.
4. **Takt Time** — Pace production to match customer demand rate.
5. **Built-in Quality (Jidoka)** — Stop production immediately when a defect is detected.

The **Takt Time** formula is critical for KTU:

$$\text{Takt Time} = \frac{\text{Available Production Time per Day}}{\text{Customer Demand per Day}}$$

**Example:** If a factory operates 480 minutes/day and customers demand 240 units/day:

$$\text{Takt Time} = \frac{480 \text{ min}}{240 \text{ units}} = 2 \text{ min/unit}$$

This means every station must complete its work in 2 minutes to match demand.

## 2.3 The Pillars/Building Blocks of JIT

The JIT system is built on **four interlinked pillars**:

| Pillar | Description | KTU Frequency |
|--------|-------------|---------------|
| **Just-In-Time Production** | Pull-based flow, minimal inventory | High |
| **Jidoka (Autonomation)** | Automatic defect detection, line stop authority | High |
| **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** | Zero breakdowns, zero defects, zero accidents | High |
| **Total Quality Management (TQM)** | Quality at source, continuous improvement (Kaizen) | Very High |

> [!NOTE]
> **Supplier Network (Tier 1, 2, 3):** JIT requires a small, reliable, geographically close supplier base. Toyota traditionally uses **keiretsu** — a tightly-knit group of suppliers in long-term partnerships.

## 2.4 Supply Chain Management — Components

The supply chain has **three primary flows**:

1. **Material/Product Flow** (Forward) — Raw materials → Finished goods → Customer
2. **Information Flow** (Bidirectional) — Demand forecasts, orders, schedules, status updates
3. **Financial Flow** (Bidirectional) — Payments, credit terms, invoicing, consignment

## 2.5 Supply Chain Drivers (KTU High-Yield Framework)

Chopra & Meindl's framework identifies **six key supply chain drivers** divided into **facilities, inventory, transportation, information, sourcing, and pricing**.

| Driver | Strategic Role | Example Decision |
|--------|----------------|------------------|
| **Facilities** | Where to locate production/warehouses | Plant location, capacity |
| **Inventory** | Buffering demand-supply mismatch | Safety stock, EOQ |
| **Transportation** | Moving products between stages | Mode (air/sea/road), fleet size |
| **Information** | Coordinating supply chain activities | ERP, forecasting systems |
| **Sourcing** | Procurement strategy | Make-vs-buy, supplier selection |
| **Pricing** | Influencing demand/supply | Discounts, dynamic pricing |

## 2.6 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** Vertical pipe symbols `|` are NOT used inside tables. LaTeX absolute values use `\vert` or `\mid`.

| # | Formula | LaTeX | Variables | KTU Application |
|---|---------|-------|-----------|-----------------|
| 1 | Takt Time | $T_t = \frac{T_{avail}}{D_{cust}}$ | $T_t$ = Takt time, $T_{avail}$ = available time, $D_{cust}$ = demand | Production line balancing |
| 2 | Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) | $Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$ | $D$ = annual demand, $S$ = ordering cost, $H$ = holding cost/unit/year | Optimal lot size in SCM |
| 3 | Total Inventory Cost (TIC) | $TIC = \frac{D}{Q}S + \frac{Q}{2}H$ | — | Cost optimization |
| 4 | Reorder Point (ROP) | $ROP = d \cdot L + SS$ | $d$ = daily demand, $L$ = lead time (days), $SS$ = safety stock | When to order |
| 5 | Safety Stock | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$ | $z$ = service level z-score, $\sigma_{LT}$ = std dev of lead time demand | Buffer against variability |
| 6 | Inventory Turnover | $ITR = \frac{COGS}{AI}$ | $COGS$ = cost of goods sold, $AI$ = average inventory | Efficiency metric |
| 7 | Cycle Time (Little's Law) | $CT = \frac{WIP}{\text{Throughput Rate}}$ | $WIP$ = work-in-process | Flow analysis |
| 8 | Throughput Time | $T_t = \sum t_{process} + \sum t_{queue} + \sum t_{move}$ | — | Lead time decomposition |
| 9 | Bullwhip Ratio | $BW = \frac{\sigma_{orders}}{\sigma_{demand}}$ | — | Demand variability |
| 10 | Cash-to-Cash Cycle | $C2C = DIO + DSO - DPO$ | $DIO$ = days inventory outstanding, $DSO$ = days sales outstanding, $DPO$ = days payable outstanding | Working capital |
| 11 | Supplier On-Time Delivery | $OTD\% = \frac{OTD_{count}}{T_{deliveries}} \times 100$ | — | Supplier scorecard |
| 12 | Perfect Order Fulfillment | $POF = OTD \times OTR \times OSD \times OSD$ | — | SCM excellence KPI |

## 2.7 The Bullwhip Effect — A Critical SCM Phenomenon

The **Bullwhip Effect** is the phenomenon where **small fluctuations in customer demand at the retail end cause progressively larger fluctuations in orders at the wholesale, distributor, manufacturer, and supplier levels**, like a whip that increases in amplitude as it travels.

**Bullwhip Ratio Formula:**

$$BW = \frac{\text{Std Deviation of Orders}}{\text{Std Deviation of Customer Demand}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A $BW > 1$ indicates the bullwhip effect is present. The goal of SCM is to drive $BW \rightarrow 1$.

**Causes of Bullwhip Effect (KTU-Mandated List):**

1. **Demand Forecast Updating** — Each stage forecasts based on incoming orders, not actual demand.
2. **Order Batching** — Bulk orders placed periodically to reduce ordering costs.
3. **Price Fluctuations** — Discounts, promotions trigger "buy more" behavior.
4. **Rationing Game** — When supply is short, retailers over-order to ensure allocation.
5. **Shortage Gaming** — Inflated orders hoping to receive more than actual needs.

**Mitigation Strategies (KTU High-Yield):**

- **Information Sharing** — POS data shared across the chain (VMI, CPFR)
- **Order Smoothing** — Eliminate order batching via EDI
- **Lead Time Reduction** — Shorter LT = smaller safety stock needed
- **Vendor Managed Inventory (VMI)** — Supplier monitors retailer's stock
- **Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, Replenishment (CPFR)**

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Toyota's JIT revolution reduced inventory from 30 days → 3 days.
- **Electronics:** Apple uses JIT-aggregated SCM with Foxconn for iPhone production.
- **Healthcare:** JIT applied to surgical instrument delivery reduces hospital storage costs.
- **Construction:** Just-in-time delivery of materials reduces on-site clutter and theft.
- **Aerospace:** Boeing & Airbus use lean SCM to coordinate 1,000+ tier-3 suppliers globally.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) Model

The **EOQ model** (also called the Wilson-Harris model) is foundational to SCM inventory theory. It determines the order quantity that **minimizes the total annual inventory cost**.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

**Assumptions:**
- Demand $D$ is constant and known
- Lead time $L$ is constant
- Order is received instantaneously
- No stockouts allowed
- Carrying cost $H$ per unit per year is constant
- Ordering cost $S$ per order is constant

**Cost Structure:**

Total Annual Cost (TAC) = Ordering Cost + Holding Cost

$$\text{Ordering Cost} = \left( \frac{D}{Q} \right) \cdot S$$

Explanation: If annual demand is $D$ units and each order is for $Q$ units, then number of orders per year = $D/Q$. Multiply by cost per order $S$.

$$\text{Holding Cost} = \left( \frac{Q}{2} \right) \cdot H$$

Explanation: Average inventory is $Q/2$ (since inventory depletes linearly from $Q$ to 0). Multiply by holding cost per unit per year $H$.

### 3.1.2 Total Cost Function

$$\text{TAC}(Q) = \left( \frac{D}{Q} \right) S + \left( \frac{Q}{2} \right) H$$

This is the function we want to minimize.

### 3.1.3 Finding the Optimum — First Derivative Method

Take the derivative of TAC with respect to $Q$ and set it to zero:

$$\frac{d(\text{TAC})}{dQ} = \frac{d}{dQ}\left[ \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{QH}{2} \right]$$

Apply differentiation rules:

$$\frac{d(\text{TAC})}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2}$$

Set equal to zero for optimum:

$$-\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$

Solve for $Q$:

$$\frac{DS}{Q^2} = \frac{H}{2}$$

$$Q^2 = \frac{2DS}{H}$$

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$

### 3.1.4 Second Derivative Verification

To confirm minimum (not maximum):

$$\frac{d^2(\text{TAC})}{dQ^2} = \frac{2DS}{Q^3} > 0$$

Since the second derivative is positive, $Q^*$ is indeed a **minimum**.

### 3.1.5 Optimal Total Cost

Substitute $Q^*$ back into TAC:

$$\text{TAC}^* = \frac{DS}{Q^*} + \frac{Q^* H}{2} = \frac{DS}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} + \frac{H \sqrt{2DS/H}}{2}$$

Simplify each term:

$$\text{TAC}^* = \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}} + \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}} = 2 \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}} = \sqrt{2DSH}$$

### 3.1.6 KTU Numerical Example

**Problem:** A factory uses **5,000 units/year** of a component. Ordering cost is **₹200/order**. Holding cost is **₹10/unit/year**. Find EOQ and total cost.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify parameters:
- $D = 5000$
- $S = 200$
- $H = 10$

Step 2 — Apply EOQ formula:

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 5000 \cdot 200}{10}} = \sqrt{\frac{2,000,000}{10}} = \sqrt{200,000}$$

$$Q^* = 447.21 \approx 447 \text{ units}$$

Step 3 — Number of orders per year:

$$N = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{5000}{447} = 11.18 \text{ orders/year}$$

Step 4 — Time between orders (cycle time):

$$T_c = \frac{365}{11.18} = 32.65 \text{ days}$$

Step 5 — Total annual cost:

$$\text{TAC}^* = \sqrt{2 \cdot 5000 \cdot 200 \cdot 10} = \sqrt{20,000,000} = ₹4,472.14$$

## 3.2 Bullwhip Effect — Quantitative Demonstration

### 3.2.1 Forrester's Order-Amplification Model

A four-stage supply chain: Customer → Retailer → Wholesaler → Distributor → Manufacturer.

Let the variance amplification at each stage be denoted by the **Order-Variance Ratio**:

$$\text{OVR} = \frac{\text{Var}(\text{Orders placed by stage})}{\text{Var}(\text{Customer demand seen by stage})}$$

For a simple **moving average forecast + order-up-to policy**, Forrester showed:

$$\text{OVR} \geq 1 + \frac{2L}{p} + \frac{2L^2}{p^2}$$

where $L$ is lead time and $p$ is the moving average window.

### 3.2.2 Numerical Demonstration of Bullwhip

**Step 1:** Suppose customer demand has standard deviation $\sigma_D = 10$ units.

**Step 2:** Retailer places orders with $\sigma_R = 15$ units (1.5× amplification).

**Step 3:** Wholesaler observes $\sigma_W = 25$ units (2.5× amplification).

**Step 4:** Distributor sees $\sigma_{Dist} = 40$ units (4× amplification).

**Step 5:** Manufacturer experiences $\sigma_M = 70$ units (7× amplification).

Bullwhip Ratio at Manufacturer Level:

$$BW_{mfr} = \frac{\sigma_M}{\sigma_D} = \frac{70}{10} = 7$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A 10-unit variation in actual customer demand translates to a **70-unit order variability** at the manufacturer — a **7× amplification**. This forces the manufacturer to either over-produce (waste) or under-produce (stockouts).

### 3.2.3 Mitigation by Information Sharing

With **POS data sharing** between retailer and manufacturer, the manufacturer's order variance approaches retail order variance:

$$BW_{mfr, \text{shared}} = \frac{\sigma_M^{\text{shared}}}{\sigma_D} \approx 1.5 \text{ (down from 7)}$$

This **reduces safety stock by 79%** at the manufacturer level.

## 3.3 Takt Time and Line Balancing — Worked Example

**Problem:** A plant operates **2 shifts × 8 hours = 16 hours/day = 960 minutes/day**. Customer demand is **480 units/day**. Breaks and changeovers consume **60 minutes/day**. Calculate Takt Time and the required number of workstations.

**Step 1 — Calculate Net Available Time:**

$$T_{avail} = 960 - 60 = 900 \text{ min/day}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Takt Time:**

$$T_t = \frac{900}{480} = 1.875 \text{ min/unit}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with Task Times:**

Suppose the assembly has 7 tasks with the following times (minutes):

| Task | Time |
|------|------|
| A | 0.5 |
| B | 0.8 |
| C | 1.2 |
| D | 1.8 |
| E | 1.0 |
| F | 0.6 |
| G | 1.5 |

**Step 4 — Total Task Time:**

$$T_{total} = 0.5 + 0.8 + 1.2 + 1.8 + 1.0 + 0.6 + 1.5 = 7.4 \text{ min}$$

**Step 5 — Theoretical Minimum Workstations:**

$$N_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{T_{total}}{T_t} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{7.4}{1.875} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 3.95 \right\rceil = 4$$

**Step 6 — Assign Tasks to 4 Workstations Using Ranked Positional Weight (RPW):**

> [!NOTE]
> This is a standard KTU numerical problem. Detailed assignment is shown in the table below.

| Workstation | Tasks Assigned | Cumulative Time (min) | Slack (Takt - Cumulative) |
|-------------|----------------|----------------------|---------------------------|
| WS-1 | D (1.8) | 1.8 | 0.075 |
| WS-2 | C (1.2) + G (1.5) | 2.7 (exceeds!) → Assign C only → 1.2 | 0.675 |
| WS-2 (reassign) | C (1.2) + E (1.0) = 2.2 (exceeds) → C only | 1.2 | 0.675 |
| WS-3 | B (0.8) + A (0.5) + F (0.6) = 1.9 | 1.9 | -0.025 (slight violation) |
| WS-4 | G (1.5) | 1.5 | 0.375 |

**Step 7 — Line Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{line} = \frac{T_{total}}{N_{actual} \cdot T_t} = \frac{7.4}{4 \cdot 1.875} = 98.67\%$$

**Step 8 — Balance Delay:**

$$BD = 1 - \eta_{line} = 1.33\%$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — JIT vs. Traditional Manufacturing

| Dimension | Traditional (Push) Manufacturing | JIT (Pull) Manufacturing |
|-----------|----------------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Inventory** | High (buffer for safety) | Near-zero |
| **Lead Time** | Long | Short |
| **Batch Size** | Large (economies of scale) | Small (often 1) |
| **Quality Control** | Inspection at end | At-source (Poka-Yoke) |
| **Suppliers** | Many, competitive bidding | Few, long-term partnership |
| **Changeover** | Long, infrequent | Short, frequent (SMED) |
| **Setup Time** | Hours | Minutes (Single-Minute Exchange of Dies) |
| **Workforce** | Specialized, narrow tasks | Multi-skilled, flexible |
| **Information Flow** | Slow, manual | Real-time, electronic |
| **Wastage** | Accepted as "normal" | Zero-tolerance (Kaizen) |
| **Cost Structure** | Fixed cost dominant | Variable cost dominant |
| **Risk** | Inventory obsolescence | Supply disruption |

## 3.5 Cost-Benefit Tradeoff Derivation in JIT

**Traditional System Cost:**

$$C_{trad} = C_{holding} \cdot \frac{Q_{trad}}{2} + S \cdot \frac{D}{Q_{trad}}$$

**JIT System Cost:**

$$C_{jit} = C_{holding} \cdot \frac{Q_{jit}}{2} + S \cdot \frac{D}{Q_{jit}} + C_{jit\text{-}setup}$$

The JIT transition cost includes:
- **SMED implementation** (Single-Minute Exchange of Dies) — reduce changeover time
- **Supplier development** (training, audit, certification)
- **Workforce training** (multi-skilling)
- **Information system upgrades** (EDI, ERP, Kanban boards)

**Break-Even Condition:**

$$C_{trad}(Q_{trad}) = C_{jit}(Q_{jit}) + C_{transition}$$

Solving iteratively gives the optimal transition point.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION_4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — JIT Pull System with Kanban Flow

The following Mermaid block illustrates the **Kanban-based pull system** from customer demand to raw material supplier.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Customer Order Signal] --> B[Finished Goods Kanban]
    B --> C[Assembly Station AS1]
    C --> D[Sub-Assembly Kanban]
    D --> E[Machining Station MS1]
    E --> F[Component Kanban]
    F --> G[Parts Production PP1]
    G --> H[Supplier Withdrawal Kanban]
    H --> I[Supplier Plant SP1]
    I --> J[Raw Material RM Stock]
    J --> I
    I --> H
    H --> G
    G --> F
    F --> E
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Just-In-Time (JIT) production. List any FOUR wastes eliminated by JIT.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Just-In-Time (JIT) is a Japanese production philosophy developed by Taiichi Ohno at Toyota, which aims to produce and deliver the right items, in the right quantity, at the right time — **eliminating all forms of waste (Muda)** in the production process. It is a pull-based system where production is triggered by actual customer demand rather than forecasted demand.

> **Four Wastes (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):**
> 1. **Transportation** — Unnecessary movement of materials
> 2. **Inventory** — Excess raw material, WIP, finished goods
> 3. **Waiting** — Idle time for workers or machines
> 4. **Defects** — Production of faulty items requiring rework
>
> *(Other acceptable wastes from TIMWOOD: Motion, Overproduction, Over-processing)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Explain the Bullwhip Effect in Supply Chain Management. State any THREE causes.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Explanation (1.5 Marks):** The Bullwhip Effect is a phenomenon in supply chains where small fluctuations in end-customer demand cause progressively larger fluctuations in orders as we move upstream from retailer → wholesaler → distributor → manufacturer. The demand variance **amplifies** at each stage, leading to inventory problems, stockouts, and excess production. Mathematically, the Bullwhip Ratio $BW = \sigma_{orders}/\sigma_{demand} > 1$.

> **Three Causes (1.5 Marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Demand Forecast Updating** — Each tier forecasts based on incoming orders, not actual end-demand, accumulating errors.
> 2. **Order Batching** — Periodic bulk ordering for transportation/discount advantages creates artificial spikes.
> 3. **Price Fluctuations** — Promotional discounts trigger forward-buying and demand surges (e.g., "Buy 2 Get 1 Free").
>
> *(Other valid causes: Rationing Game, Shortage Gaming)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### ⭐ QUESTION A — OPTION 1 (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — Explain the philosophy and core principles of Just-In-Time (JIT) production. Discuss its relationship with the Toyota Production System (TPS).

**Model Answer:**

**1. Philosophy of JIT (3 Marks):**

JIT is built on the **central philosophy that waste is the root cause of all production inefficiencies**, and inventory is a liability that hides problems rather than an asset that provides safety. Taiichi Ohno observed that **traditional mass production systems** (Ford-style) accumulated large inventories between production stages as "buffers" against variability. These buffers, however, **concealed underlying problems** like machine breakdowns, quality defects, long setup times, and unreliable suppliers.

The JIT philosophy flips this paradigm:
- **Inventory is a symptom, not a solution.**
- **Exposing problems enables continuous improvement (Kaizen).**
- **Every action must add value to the customer.**

The Japanese concept of **"Muda" (waste)** vs **"Mura" (unevenness)** vs **"Muri" (overburden)** is central. JIT targets all three.

**2. Core Principles of JIT (3 Marks):**

| # | Principle | Description |
|---|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Pull-based production** | Produce only when downstream signals demand (Kanban) |
| 2 | **Continuous flow** | One-piece flow through minimized workstations |
| 3 | **Takt time adherence** | Pace production to customer demand rate |
| 4 | **Built-in quality (Jidoka)** | Stop production the moment a defect is detected |
| 5 | **Zero inventory** | Minimize raw material, WIP, and finished goods |
| 6 | **Continuous improvement (Kaizen)** | Daily incremental improvements by all employees |

**3. Relationship with TPS (1 Mark):**

The **Toyota Production System (TPS)**, developed by Taiichi Ohno, Eiji Toyoda, and Shigeo Shingo, is the **parent system** from which JIT emerged. TPS has **two main pillars**:
- **Jidoka (Autonomation)** — automation with human intelligence
- **Just-In-Time** — the right parts at the right time

JIT is **one of the two pillars of TPS**. The other elements of TPS (TPM, TQM, 5S, Heijunka, etc.) form the supporting infrastructure that makes JIT sustainable.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — Calculate the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) and number of orders per year for the following scenario, and comment on the inventory turnover ratio.

**Data:**
- Annual demand $D = 12,000$ units
- Ordering cost $S = ₹250$ per order
- Holding cost $H = ₹20$ per unit per year
- Lead time $L = 7$ days
- The company operates 300 days/year

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — EOQ Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12,000 \times 250}{20}}$$

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{6,000,000}{20}} = \sqrt{300,000}$$

$$Q^* = 547.72 \approx 548 \text{ units per order}$$

**Step 2 — Number of Orders per Year (1 Mark):**

$$N = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{12,000}{548} = 21.9 \approx 22 \text{ orders per year}$$

**Step 3 — Time Between Orders (Cycle Time) (1 Mark):**

$$T_c = \frac{300 \text{ days}}{22} = 13.64 \text{ days between orders}$$

**Step 4 — Reorder Point (1 Mark):**

Daily demand: $d = 12,000/300 = 40$ units/day

$$ROP = d \times L = 40 \times 7 = 280 \text{ units}$$

So when inventory drops to 280 units, a new order should be placed.

**Step 5 — Total Annual Inventory Cost (1 Mark):**

$$\text{TAC}^* = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 12,000 \times 250 \times 20}$$

$$\text{TAC}^* = \sqrt{120,000,000} = ₹10,954.45$$

**Step 6 — Inventory Turnover Ratio (1 Mark):**

$$ITR = \frac{D}{Q^*/2} = \frac{12,000}{274} = 43.8 \text{ times per year}$$

**Conclusion:** The ITR of 43.8 is high, indicating excellent inventory efficiency. Under a JIT framework, this lean posture is desirable. However, the company must ensure that the 7-day lead time remains reliable to avoid stockouts.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — OPTION 2 (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — Explain the concept of Supply Chain Management (SCM). Discuss the Bullwhip Effect in detail, including its causes, consequences, and mitigation strategies.

**Model Answer:**

**1. SCM Concept (2 Marks):**

Supply Chain Management is the **end-to-end integration and coordination of all activities, organizations, people, technology, and resources** involved in moving a product from supplier to end customer. A modern supply chain involves three primary flows:

- **Material Flow:** Forward, from raw material supplier → manufacturer → distributor → retailer → customer.
- **Information Flow:** Bidirectional, including demand forecasts, POS data, order status, and shipment tracking.
- **Financial Flow:** Bidirectional, including payments, credit terms, consignment, and invoice settlements.

The goal of SCM is to **deliver maximum customer value at minimum total system cost**, while remaining agile to demand variability and supply disruptions.

**2. Bullwhip Effect — Causes (2 Marks):**

| # | Cause | Mechanism |
|---|-------|-----------|
| 1 | **Demand Forecast Updating** | Each stage uses incoming orders (not real demand) for forecasting, amplifying errors. |
| 2 | **Order Batching** | Periodic orders to reduce ordering/transportation costs create artificial spikes. |
| 3 | **Price Fluctuations** | Discounts, promotions cause forward-buying and demand inflation. |
| 4 | **Rationing Game** | When supply is scarce, retailers over-order to maximize allocated share. |
| 5 | **Shortage Gaming** | Inflated orders hoping to receive more than the actual need. |

**3. Consequences (1.5 Marks):**

- **Excess inventory** at upstream stages → higher holding costs, obsolescence
- **Stockouts** at downstream stages → lost sales, customer dissatisfaction
- **Erratic production schedules** → overtime, idle time, poor capacity utilization
- **Increased transportation costs** from emergency shipments
- **Poor customer service levels** (lower fill rate, longer lead times)

**4. Mitigation Strategies (1.5 Marks):**

- **Information Sharing** — POS data, real-time demand visibility across chain (e.g., Walmart's RetailLink)
- **Vendor Managed Inventory (VMI)** — Supplier monitors retailer's stock and replenishes automatically
- **Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, Replenishment (CPFR)** — Joint planning across chain partners
- **Order Smoothing** — Eliminate batch ordering using EDI and continuous replenishment
- **Lead Time Reduction** — Shorten LT to reduce safety stock needs
- **Stable Pricing** — Eliminate forward-buying incentives through Everyday Low Pricing (EDLP)

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — A four-stage supply chain (Customer → Retailer → Wholesaler → Manufacturer) experiences the Bullwhip Effect. Customer demand has a standard deviation of 8 units/day. The variance amplifies by 1.4× at the retailer, 2.2× at the wholesaler, and 3.5× at the manufacturer. Calculate (i) the standard deviation at each stage, (ii) the Bullwhip Ratio at the manufacturer level, and (iii) recommend two SCM strategies to reduce the amplification.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Standard Deviation at Each Stage (2 Marks):**

| Stage | Std Dev Calculation | Result |
|-------|---------------------|--------|
| Customer | $\sigma_C = 8$ (given) | **8 units/day** |
| Retailer | $\sigma_R = 1.4 \times 8$ | **11.2 units/day** |
| Wholesaler | $\sigma_W = 2.2 \times 8$ | **17.6 units/day** |
| Manufacturer | $\sigma_M = 3.5 \times 8$ | **28 units/day** |

**Step 2 — Bullwhip Ratio at Manufacturer (1 Mark):**

$$BW_{mfr} = \frac{\sigma_M}{\sigma_C} = \frac{28}{8} = 3.5$$

This means the manufacturer experiences **3.5× more variability** than actual customer demand — a clear bullwhip effect.

**Step 3 — Two SCM Mitigation Strategies (4 Marks — 2 each):**

**Strategy 1: Vendor Managed Inventory (VMI)**

Under VMI, the manufacturer is granted access to the retailer's POS data and current stock levels. The manufacturer then **takes responsibility for replenishment decisions**, eliminating the retailer's order-batching behavior and the resulting amplification. This compresses the bullwhip ratio from 3.5× to approximately 1.5×.

**Strategy 2: Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment (CPFR)**

CPFR enables **all four stages to share a single, joint demand forecast** built on collaborative inputs (promotions, events, marketing plans). This eliminates forecast-updating error propagation. Additionally, **Everyday Low Pricing (EDLP)** removes the price-fluctuation cause of bullwhip, further reducing amplification toward unity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Pull" with "Make-to-Stock":** Students often write that JIT is "make-to-stock" — this is WRONG. JIT is **make-to-order** (pull), not push.
> 2. **Missing the difference between SCM and Logistics:** SCM includes logistics, but logistics is only ONE function. SCM also covers sourcing, planning, and information.
> 3. **Bullwhip Ratio miscalculation:** The ratio is **orders/demand**, not the reverse. $\sigma_{orders}/\sigma_{demand} > 1$ = bullwhip.
> 4. **Forgetting the 8th waste:** Some KTU 2024 questions include "Skills/Talent" or "Sustainability" as a waste. Always mention the 7 (TIMWOOD) first, then add the 8th if asked.
> 5. **Not mentioning Takt Time units:** Takt Time is in **seconds/unit** or **minutes/unit**, not just "time". Always specify.
> 6. **EOQ units mismatch:** Demand must be in **same time unit** as holding cost. If $D$ is annual, $H$ is annual.
> 7. **Drawing the Bullwhip Effect graph correctly:** The KTU pattern expects an **inverted trapezoid** or **wedge shape** showing amplification upstream. Many students draw a constant-width rectangle and lose marks.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (for the last 30 minutes of revision):**

### ✅ JIT — Must Remember Points

- **JIT** = Pull-based, demand-driven, waste-eliminating production philosophy (Taiichi Ohno, Toyota).
- **TIMWOOD** = Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects.
- **8th waste** = Skills/Talent underutilization; **9th waste** = Sustainability/Energy waste.
- **Takt Time** $T_t = T_{avail}/D_{cust}$ — in **min/unit** or **sec/unit**.
- **Two pillars of TPS** = Jidoka (Autonomation) + Just-In-Time.
- **Kanban** = Visual signal card that authorizes production or material movement.
- **Jidoka** = Authority to stop the production line when a defect occurs (Andon cord).
- **Poka-Yoke** = Mistake-proofing device to prevent defects at source.
- **SMED** = Single-Minute Exchange of Dies — reduce changeover time to <10 min.
- **5S** = Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain.
- **Heijunka** = Level scheduling (production leveling).
- **Kaizen** = Continuous incremental improvement.
- **TPM** = Total Productive Maintenance (autonomous + planned maintenance).
- **JIT inventory rule of thumb**: When in doubt, **less is better**.

### ✅ SCM — Must Remember Points

- **SCM** = End-to-end management of material, information, and financial flows.
- **Three flows**: Material (forward), Information (bidirectional), Financial (bidirectional).
- **Six drivers** (Chopra & Meindl): Facilities, Inventory, Transportation, Information, Sourcing, Pricing.
- **Bullwhip Effect** = Demand variability amplification upstream; $BW = \sigma_{orders}/\sigma_{demand} > 1$.
- **Five causes of Bullwhip**: Forecast updating, Order batching, Price fluctuations, Rationing game, Shortage gaming.
- **Five mitigations**: Information sharing, VMI, CPFR, Order smoothing, Lead time reduction.
- **VMI** = Vendor Managed Inventory — supplier decides replenishment.
- **CPFR** = Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, Replenishment — joint forecast.
- **EOQ formula** = $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — Wilson-Harris model.
- **Reorder Point** = $d \times L + SS$ — when inventory hits this, place a new order.
- **Little's Law** = $WIP = \text{Throughput} \times \text{Cycle Time}$.
- **Cash-to-Cash Cycle** = $DIO + DSO - DPO$ — shorter is better for working capital.

### ✅ Critical Numerical Topics for KTU ESE

1. EOQ calculation (1–2 sub-parts, ~3–4 marks)
2. Takt Time computation
3. Bullwhip Ratio computation
4. Line balancing (RPW method)
5. Reorder Point with safety stock
6. Total cost comparison (push vs. pull)

### ✅ High-Yield KTU Frequently Asked Theory Topics

1. JIT philosophy and Taiichi Ohno's contribution
2. TIMWOOD wastes with examples
3. Comparison: Push vs. Pull, Traditional vs. JIT
4. Kanban system and number of Kanban cards
5. SCM drivers framework (Chopra & Meindl)
6. Bullwhip Effect — causes, consequences, mitigation
7. Vendor Managed Inventory (VMI)
8. 5S, SMED, Poka-Yoke definitions
9. Strategic vs. operational SCM decisions
10. Green SCM and Sustainability in JIT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** In every JIT/SCM answer, **always use the word "waste elimination"** at least once — this is a trigger phrase examiners look for. For SCM, **always mention "end-to-end visibility"** and **"information sharing"** in long answers. Numerical answers must show **all intermediate steps** — KTU awards **method marks** even if the final answer has arithmetic errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Breakdown, Preventive and Predictive Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET601 - INDUSTRIAL AND SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Breakdown, Preventive and Predictive Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 | Breakdown, Preventive and Predictive Maintenance

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Breakdown Maintenance (BM) — The "Run-to-Failure" Strategy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> **Breakdown Maintenance (BM)** is a corrective maintenance strategy wherein no maintenance action is taken on a piece of equipment until it actually fails or breaks down. The equipment is allowed to operate until functional failure occurs, after which it is repaired or replaced. It is also referred to as **Reactive Maintenance**, **Run-to-Failure Maintenance**, or **Corrective Maintenance (CM)**.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Old Car on a Kerala Highway**
Imagine your father's vintage Maruti 800 that has never seen a service centre. It is driven daily through the Kottayam–Kumili ghat road. There is no periodic oil change, no brake inspection, no timing-belt check. The car runs purely until something snaps — the clutch plate burns, the fan belt snaps, or the radiator hose bursts. The instant it stops, you tow it to the mechanic. That is **Breakdown Maintenance** in its purest form. The equipment is "used up" and then revived only when it dies.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Breakdown Maintenance is **unscheduled** in nature and is appropriate only for non-critical, low-cost, easily-replaceable assets (e.g., fuses, bulbs, disposable filters, low-value hand tools).

**Key Characteristics of Breakdown Maintenance**
- Maintenance is performed **after** a failure has occurred.
- Zero planned downtime — production only halts when failure strikes.
- Spare-parts inventory is unpredictable; emergency procurement is common.
- Suitable when **downtime cost is low** and **replacement cost is low**.

---

### 1.2 Preventive Maintenance (PM) — The "Calendar-Based Service" Strategy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** is a scheduled, time-based (or usage-based) maintenance strategy wherein maintenance activities — such as inspection, lubrication, calibration, tightening, cleaning, and parts replacement — are performed on equipment at **pre-planned intervals** (calendar time, running hours, cycles, or production units) **regardless of the equipment's current condition**. The objective is to reduce the probability of failure or to prevent performance degradation of the equipment.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Two-Wheeler Free Service Coupon Book**
Every Royal Enfield or KTM owner in Kerala receives a **"Free Service Coupon Booklet"** from the dealership. At every 1,000 km, 3,000 km, 5,000 km, and 10,000 km — irrespective of whether the engine shows a problem or not — you are **mandated** to take the bike in. Oil is changed, chain is tensioned, spark plug is checked, air filter is cleaned. You are preventing failure before it occurs. That is **Preventive Maintenance**. The interval is fixed by the manufacturer based on statistical life data, not by the bike's actual condition.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** PM is **scheduled** and is the most widely adopted maintenance philosophy in process industries (oil & gas, petrochemical, power plants, fertilizer plants) and manufacturing plants.

**Two Sub-Categories of Preventive Maintenance**
| Sub-Category | Trigger Basis | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Scheduled Maintenance** | Calendar Time (days/months) | Monthly lubrication of bearings |
| **Predictive Interval PM** | Usage Count (hours/cycles) | Overhaul of CNC spindle every 8,000 hours |

---

### 1.3 Predictive Maintenance (PdM) — The "Doctor with Sensors" Strategy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** is a condition-based maintenance strategy in which maintenance is performed only when **diagnostic measurements** and **trending analysis** of specific physical parameters (vibration, temperature, acoustic emission, oil debris, ultrasonic, current signature) indicate that degradation has reached a pre-defined threshold. It is enabled by **non-destructive testing (NDT)**, **IoT sensors**, and **machine learning** algorithms that forecast the **Remaining Useful Life (RUL)** of components.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Smart Watch ECG Alert**
Your Apple Watch continuously monitors your **heart rate, ECG, SpO₂**, and **body temperature** every second. It does NOT tell you "go to the hospital every 30 days" (that would be Preventive Maintenance). Instead, it warns: *"Your resting heart rate has been abnormal for the last 7 days — please consult a cardiologist."* The watch uses sensor data and trend analysis to **predict** a health event **before** you collapse. That is **Predictive Maintenance**. The machine is monitored continuously, and intervention happens only when the data signals danger.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** PdM is enabled by **Industry 4.0 technologies** — vibration analysis, thermography, acoustic emission, motor current signature analysis (MCSA), ultrasonic testing, and AI-based prognostics. It is the cornerstone of **smart manufacturing** and aligns with the **Industry 4.0 / IIoT** framework.

---

### 1.4 Comparative Summary at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Maintenance Type Mnemonic for Exams:** **"R — S — C"** — **R**eactive (BM) → **S**cheduled (PM) → **C**ondition-based (PdM). Modern factories progress in this exact evolutionary sequence.

| Dimension | Breakdown Maintenance | Preventive Maintenance | Predictive Maintenance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Failure | Calendar/Usage interval | Condition threshold breached |
| **Planning** | Unplanned | Planned | Planned (on-demand) |
| **Cost Profile** | Low preventive spend, high emergency spend | Medium (regular service) | High tech spend, low failure cost |
| **Downtime** | High (unplanned) | Medium (planned shutdown) | Low (intervention only when needed) |
| **Spare Inventory** | High buffer needed | Moderate buffer | Minimal buffer (just-in-time) |
| **Data Required** | None | Manufacturer's manual | Real-time sensor streams |
| **Best Suited For** | Non-critical assets | Critical, expensive assets | High-value, continuous-process assets |
| **Technology Level** | None / Low | Medium (CMMS) | High (IIoT, AI, ML) |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reliability Engineering Foundations for Maintenance

Maintenance is essentially a battle against the **degradation of reliability over time**. To make informed decisions about *when* to maintain, we need quantitative measures of equipment behaviour.

### 2.1.1 Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MTBF** is the average time interval between two successive failures of a **repairable** system. It is the reciprocal of the constant failure rate $\lambda$ for systems exhibiting an **exponential reliability distribution**.

The operational interpretation is:
- A high MTBF means the equipment is highly reliable — failures are rare and well-spaced.
- A low MTBF means the equipment is failing frequently.

For a fleet of $N$ identical machines run for a total operating time $T$ with $n$ failures observed:

$$MTBF = \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}} = \frac{T}{n}$$

### 2.1.2 Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MTTR** is the average time required to restore a failed system to operational status. It includes **active repair time + administrative/inspection/logistics time**.

$$MTTR = \frac{\text{Total Downtime (Repair Time)}}{\text{Number of Failures}} = \frac{T_r}{n}$$

### 2.1.3 Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)

For **non-repairable** components (bearings, bulbs, capacitors, fuses), the equivalent metric is **MTTF**:

$$MTTF = \frac{\text{Total Operating Time of All Units Until First Failure}}{\text{Number of Units}}$$

### 2.1.4 Availability (A)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Availability (A)** is the probability that a system is operational and ready for use at any randomly chosen instant of time. For a repairable system at steady-state:

$$A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

Equivalently, if failure rate is $\lambda$ and repair rate is $\mu$:

$$A = \frac{\mu}{\mu + \lambda} = \frac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + 1/\mu}$$

**Inherent Availability** (only considers MTBF and MTTR, ignoring logistics delays):
$$A_i = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

**Achieved Availability** (includes logistics + administrative delays $M_LT$):
$$A_a = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR + M_{LT}}$$

**Operational Availability** (includes preventive maintenance downtime $M_{PM}$):
$$A_o = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR + M_{LT} + M_{PM}}$$

### 2.1.5 Failure Rate and Reliability Function

For an exponential distribution (constant failure rate, valid in the **useful life region** of the bathtub curve):

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

where:
- $R(t)$ = reliability at time $t$ (probability of surviving up to $t$).
- $\lambda$ = constant failure rate (failures per unit time).
- $1/\lambda = MTBF$ for repairable systems.

---

## 2.2 Cost Economics of Maintenance

### 2.2.1 Total Maintenance Cost Function

For a planned PM interval of $T$ hours, the **total expected cost per unit time** is:

$$C_T(T) = C_{PM} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + C_{BM} \cdot \frac{f(T)}{T}$$

where:
- $C_T(T)$ = total expected maintenance cost per unit time (₹/hour).
- $C_{PM}$ = fixed cost of performing one preventive maintenance action (₹/event).
- $C_{BM}$ = expected cost of a single breakdown event (₹/event), including emergency repair + production loss.
- $f(T)$ = expected number of breakdowns in interval $T$ when PM is performed every $T$ hours.
- $1/T$ = frequency of PM actions per unit time.

**Optimisation Logic:** Increasing $T$ (less frequent PM) reduces PM cost but increases breakdown cost and vice versa. The optimum $T^*$ occurs where the marginal costs balance.

### 2.2.2 Optimum PM Interval (Simplified Bathtub Case)

Assuming the equipment is in its useful life (constant failure rate $\lambda$), the **optimum PM interval** is:

$$T^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 C_{PM}}{\lambda \cdot C_{BM}}}$$

This is a classic square-root formula that is highly exam-relevant.

---

## 2.3 Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OEE** is the gold-standard metric for measuring the **productivity of a manufacturing operation** by combining availability, performance, and quality. It is central to **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)**.

$$OEE = A \times P \times Q$$

where:
- $A$ = Availability = $\frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$
- $P$ = Performance = $\frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}}$
- $Q$ = Quality = $\frac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}}$

> [!NOTE]
> **World-Class OEE Benchmark: 85%** (6 Big Losses framework). Average Indian manufacturing OEE hovers around **60–65%**.

---

## 2.4 Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) — The Strategic Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TPM** is a holistic maintenance philosophy that aims for **zero breakdowns, zero defects, zero accidents** through the active involvement of **all employees** — from operators to top management. It was developed in Japan (Nippon Denso, 1971) and is built on **8 pillars**.

The 8 Pillars of TPM:
1. **Autonomous Maintenance** — operators own daily cleaning, lubrication, inspection.
2. **Planned Maintenance** — scheduled PM based on failure data.
3. **Quality Maintenance** — zero-defect condition of equipment.
4. **Focused Improvement** — small-group kaizen activities.
5. **Early Management** — design new equipment for easy maintenance.
6. **Training** — skill development of operators and technicians.
7. **Safety, Health, Environment (SHE)** — zero accidents.
8. **Office TPM** — extends TPM to administrative functions.

---

## 2.5 Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RCM** is a systematic engineering framework used to determine the most cost-effective maintenance strategy for each asset based on its **failure modes**, **failure consequences**, and **proactive task selection**. It is widely used in aviation (FAA), nuclear, and oil & gas.

**The 7 RCM Questions (SAE JA1011 Standard)**
1. What are the functions and desired performance standards of the asset?
2. In what ways can it fail to fulfil its functions?
3. What causes each functional failure?
4. What happens when each failure occurs (consequence)?
5. In what way does each failure matter (safety, environmental, operational, non-operational)?
6. What can be done to prevent or predict each failure?
7. What should be done if no proactive task is found?

---

## 2.6 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Variables & Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $MTBF = T / n$ | $T$ = total operating time (h), $n$ = number of failures | Reliability of repairable system |
| 2 | $MTTR = T_r / n$ | $T_r$ = total repair time (h) | Repair performance |
| 3 | $A_i = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Dimensionless (fraction or %) | Inherent availability |
| 4 | $A_a = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR + M_{LT}}$ | $M_{LT}$ = logistics/admin delay (h) | Achieved availability |
| 5 | $A_o = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR + M_{LT} + M_{PM}}$ | $M_{PM}$ = PM downtime (h) | Operational availability |
| 6 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate (h$^{-1}$) | Survival probability (exponential) |
| 7 | $T^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 C_{PM}}{\lambda C_{BM}}}$ | $C_{PM}$, $C_{BM}$ in ₹; $\lambda$ in h$^{-1}$ | Optimum PM interval |
| 8 | $C_T(T) = \frac{C_{PM}}{T} + \frac{C_{BM} \cdot f(T)}{T}$ | Cost per unit time (₹/h) | Total maintenance cost |
| 9 | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | All dimensionless | Equipment productivity |
| 10 | $\lambda = 1 / MTBF$ | Constant failure rate | Exponential reliability |
| 11 | $f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta} \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}$ | Weibull PDF (2-parameter) | Time-dependent failure rate |
| 12 | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}$ | $\eta$ = scale, $\beta$ = shape | Weibull reliability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Always retain units. Marks are deducted in KTU scripts for unit-less numerical answers. A formula written as $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ without expressing availability as a **percentage** (e.g., $A = 92.4\%$) may lose a half-mark in valuation.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Inherent Availability Formula

### 3.1.1 Starting Assumption
Consider a single repairable machine operating continuously. Let us observe it over a long horizon $T_{total}$. The fraction of time it was **operational** is, by definition, **availability**.

**Step 1 — Total Time Decomposition**

The horizon is split into two mutually exclusive states: *operating* and *failed/repairing*.

$$
T_{total} = T_{operating} + T_{repair}
$$

**Step 2 — Define Downtime Fraction (Unavailability)**

The fraction of total time spent in repair is:

$$
\bar{D} = \frac{T_{repair}}{T_{total}}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Statistical Steady-State Limit**

By the **renewal-reward theorem** (operational in KTU Module 2), for a long run, the time-average converges to the expectation per cycle:

$$
\bar{D} = \frac{E[T_{repair}]}{E[T_{repair}] + E[T_{operating}]} = \frac{MTTR}{MTTR + MTBF}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Availability**

$$
A_i = 1 - \bar{D} = 1 - \frac{MTTR}{MTTR + MTBF}
$$

$$
A_i = \frac{(MTTR + MTBF) - MTTR}{MTTR + MTBF}
$$

$$
A_i = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

**Result:** This is the KTU-standard availability expression. The same derivation is asked in Part B questions frequently.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Optimum Preventive Maintenance Interval $T^*$

### 3.2.1 Model Setup (Simplified Cost Model)

We assume:
- A scheduled PM is performed every $T$ hours of operation.
- Each PM event costs $C_{PM}$ (fixed, regardless of condition).
- If a breakdown occurs between two PMs, it costs $C_{BM}$ (always more than $C_{PM}$).
- Failure rate $\lambda$ is constant in the useful life region.
- Expected number of failures in interval $T$: $f(T) = \lambda T$ (Poisson assumption).

**Step 1 — Form the Total Cost per Unit Time**

Cost per PM event per unit time:

$$
C_{PM-term} = \frac{C_{PM}}{T}
$$

Cost of breakdowns per unit time:

$$
C_{BM-term} = \frac{C_{BM} \cdot f(T)}{T} = \frac{C_{BM} \cdot \lambda T}{T} = C_{BM} \cdot \lambda
$$

Total cost per unit time:

$$
C_T(T) = \frac{C_{PM}}{T} + C_{BM} \cdot \lambda
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. $T$ and Set to Zero**

$$
\frac{dC_T}{dT} = -\frac{C_{PM}}{T^2} + 0 = 0
$$

Wait — this simplified linear model gives a degenerate result. The standard derivation includes the **risk of failures** growing with $T$ via the conditional probability of survival.

**Step 2 (Corrected) — Using the Hazard Function Approach**

The expected number of failures in the next interval $T$ given survival up to $T$ is:

$$
f(T) = \int_0^T \lambda(t) \, dt = \lambda T
$$

Total cost including probability-of-failure scaling:

$$
C_T(T) = \frac{C_{PM}}{T} + \frac{C_{BM} \cdot \lambda T}{2T} = \frac{C_{PM}}{T} + \frac{C_{BM} \lambda}{2}
$$

Minimising w.r.t. $T$:

$$
\frac{dC_T}{dT} = -\frac{C_{PM}}{T^2} = 0
$$

This is degenerate; the proper formulation uses a more realistic $f(T) = e^{\lambda T} - 1$, which yields:

$$
C_T(T) = \frac{C_{PM}}{T} + \frac{C_{BM} \cdot (e^{\lambda T} - 1)}{T}
$$

Differentiating:

$$
\frac{dC_T}{dT} = -\frac{C_{PM}}{T^2} - \frac{C_{BM}}{T^2}(e^{\lambda T} - 1) + \frac{C_{BM}}{T} \lambda e^{\lambda T} = 0
$$

Multiplying through by $-T^2$:

$$
C_{PM} + C_{BM}(e^{\lambda T} - 1) = C_{BM} \lambda T e^{\lambda T}
$$

For small $\lambda T$, using $e^{\lambda T} \approx 1 + \lambda T + (\lambda T)^2 / 2$:

$$
C_{PM} + C_{BM} \lambda T + \frac{C_{BM} \lambda^2 T^2}{2} = C_{BM} \lambda T (1 + \lambda T)
$$

Expanding the right side:

$$
C_{BM} \lambda T + C_{BM} \lambda^2 T^2
$$

Subtracting common terms:

$$
C_{PM} + \frac{C_{BM} \lambda^2 T^2}{2} = C_{BM} \lambda^2 T^2
$$

$$
C_{PM} = \frac{C_{BM} \lambda^2 T^2}{2}
$$

Solving for $T$:

$$
T^2 = \frac{2 C_{PM}}{C_{BM} \lambda^2}
$$

$$
T^* = \frac{1}{\lambda} \sqrt{\frac{2 C_{PM}}{C_{BM}}}
$$

**Result:** The optimum PM interval depends on the **square root of the cost ratio** $C_{PM} / C_{BM}$ and is **inversely proportional** to the failure rate.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU Style

**Problem:** A centrifugal pump in a petrochemical plant has the following data:
- Operating time in 1 year: 7,500 hours
- Number of failures: 6
- Total repair time: 90 hours
- Total preventive maintenance time: 60 hours
- Logistics/admin delay: 30 hours

**Compute (a) MTBF, (b) MTTR, (c) Inherent Availability, (d) Achieved Availability, (e) Operational Availability.**

### Solution

**(a) MTBF**

$$
MTBF = \frac{T_{operating}}{n_{failures}} = \frac{7500}{6}
$$

$$
MTBF = 1250 \text{ hours}
$$

**(b) MTTR**

$$
MTTR = \frac{T_{repair}}{n_{failures}} = \frac{90}{6}
$$

$$
MTTR = 15 \text{ hours}
$$

**(c) Inherent Availability**

$$
A_i = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{1250}{1250 + 15} = \frac{1250}{1265}
$$

$$
A_i = 0.9881 = 98.81\%
$$

**(d) Achieved Availability** (include logistics delay $M_{LT} = 30$ h)

$$
A_a = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR + M_{LT}} = \frac{1250}{1250 + 15 + 30} = \frac{1250}{1295}
$$

$$
A_a = 0.9653 = 96.53\%
$$

**(e) Operational Availability** (include PM downtime $M_{PM} = 60$ h)

$$
A_o = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR + M_{LT} + M_{PM}} = \frac{1250}{1250 + 15 + 30 + 60} = \frac{1250}{1355}
$$

$$
A_o = 0.9225 = 92.25\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The pump is operationally available **92.25%** of the time. The drop from $A_i = 98.81\%$ to $A_o = 92.25\%$ quantifies the cost of logistics and PM activities — a key KTU valuation point.

---

## 3.4 Weibull Distribution — Derivation of Reliability

The Weibull distribution is the most general reliability model for non-constant failure rates. The **Weibull CDF** (probability of failure by time $t$) is:

$$
F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}
$$

Differentiating gives the **PDF**:

$$
f(t) = \frac{dF}{dt} = \frac{\beta}{\eta} \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}
$$

The **reliability function** is:

$$
R(t) = 1 - F(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}
$$

The **failure rate** is:

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{\beta}{\eta} \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}
$$

| Shape parameter $\beta$ | Failure Rate Behaviour | Real-World Phase |
|---|---|---|
| $\beta < 1$ | Decreasing $\lambda(t)$ | Infant mortality (burn-in) |
| $\beta = 1$ | Constant $\lambda(t) = 1/\eta$ | Useful life (random failures) |
| $\beta > 1$ | Increasing $\lambda(t)$ | Wear-out (end-of-life) |

The **MTTF** (mean time to failure, non-repairable) for Weibull is:

$$
MTTF = \eta \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)
$$

where $\Gamma(\cdot)$ is the gamma function.

For $\beta = 1$: $MTTF = \eta$ (since $\Gamma(2) = 1$).
For $\beta = 2$: $MTTF = \eta \cdot \Gamma(1.5) = \eta \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \approx 0.886 \eta$.

---

## 3.5 The Bathtub Curve

The **bathtub curve** is a qualitative model of how failure rate $\lambda(t)$ varies with the age of a component:

```
Failure Rate (λ)
       |
       |  *
       |  *  *  *                                    *
       |  *  *  *                              *     *
       |  *  *  *                          *   *     *
       |  *  *  *                      *   *   *     *
       |  *  *  *  *  *  *  *  *   *  *   *   *     *
       |________________________________________________
         Infant        Useful Life        Wear-out
       Mortality      (Random Failures)   Phase
       (β<1)            (β=1)            (β>1)
```

| Region | Duration | Failure Cause | Maintenance Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Infant Mortality** | Early life | Manufacturing defects, installation errors | Burn-in testing, debugging |
| **Useful Life** | Bulk of life | Random, environmental, overload | PM + condition monitoring |
| **Wear-Out** | Late life | Fatigue, corrosion, wear | Replacement, refurbishment |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Maintenance Strategy Decision Tree

The following Mermaid flow diagram maps the **decision logic** an industrial engineer follows when classifying an asset and selecting its maintenance strategy. This is a high-yield visualisation for KTU exam answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Start: New Asset Acquired] --> node2{Is Asset Critical?}
    node2 -- Yes, Mission-Critical --> node3{Is Failure Pattern Predictable?}
    node2 -- No, Non-Critical --> node4[Use Breakdown Maintenance]
    node3 -- Yes, Wear-Out Pattern --> node5[Use Preventive Maintenance with Scheduled Overhauls]
    node3 -- No, Random Failures --> node6{Is Real-Time Monitoring Feasible?}
    node6 -- Yes, Sensors Available --> node7[Use Predictive Maintenance with IIoT Sensors]
    node6 -- No, Cost of Sensors Too High --> node8[Use Preventive Maintenance at Fixed Intervals]
    node5 --> node9[Monitor OEE Availability MTBF MTTR]
    node7 --> node9
    node8 --> node9
    node4 --> node9
    node9 --> node10{Performance Targets Met?}
    node10 -- No --> node11[Apply RCM Analysis and TPM Pillar Activities]
    node10 -- Yes --> node12[Standardise and Document]
    node11 --> node9
    node12 --> node13[End of Decision Loop]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Start at the top with "New Asset Acquired" and follow the decision branches. Mission-critical assets (e.g., aircraft engine, nuclear reactor coolant pump) warrant PdM or PM. Non-critical assets (e.g., office fan, low-cost tooling) are best left to BM because PM costs would exceed failure costs.
