| 2 | **Competition** | Global competitors force firms to match best-in-class performance. | Benchmarking in TQM |
| 3 | **Change** | The nature of work and markets is shifting constantly. | Agile Manufacturing doctrine |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Exam Trap:** Do **not** confuse BPR with **Business Process Improvement (BPI)** or **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)**. BPR is **radical and discontinuous**, while BPI/Kaizen is **incremental and continuous**.

---

## 1.4 BPR vs. Related Methodologies (Comparison Matrix)

| Dimension | BPR | TQM / Kaizen | Six Sigma | Reorganizing / Restructuring |
|-----------|-----|--------------|-----------|------------------------------|
| **Scope** | End-to-end process | Functional area | Defined CTQ | Organizational chart |
| **Change Type** | Radical / Discontinuous | Incremental | Incremental | Structural |
| **Time Horizon** | 6–18 months | Ongoing | 3–6 months per project | 1–3 months |
| **Driving Force** | IT + Process design | People + Culture | Data + Statistics | Cost cutting |
| **Performance Gain** | 10x – 100x | 1.05x – 1.10x | 1.5x – 3x | Variable |
| **Risk Level** | Very High | Low | Medium | High |
| **Origin** | Hammer & Champy (1993) | Deming, Juran (1950s) | Motorola (1986) | Corporate Finance |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of BPR (Hammer & Champy)

> [!NOTE]  
> **Pillar 1: Process Orientation (Not Functional Orientation)**  
> Work is organized around **outcomes**, not tasks. Example: A "Claims Processor" becomes a "Claims Manager" who handles the entire claim end-to-end instead of passing it across 5 desks.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Pillar 2: Horizontal Compression**  
> Combine parallel/similar steps to reduce handoffs. Goal: Convert sequential processing into parallel processing wherever possible.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Pillar 3: Vertical Compression**  
> Decisions are pushed **down the hierarchy** to the front-line worker. Empowerment eliminates approval layers. The "doer" becomes the "checker".

> [!NOTE]  
> **Pillar 4: Information Technology as the Enabler**  
> IT is the **single most important enabler** of BPR. Reengineered processes require modern IT (databases, ERP, AI, workflow automation). Without IT leverage, reengineering reduces to downsizing.

---

## 2.2 The Seven Principles of Reengineering (Hammer & Champy)

| # | Principle | Meaning |
|---|-----------|---------|
| 1 | **Organize around outcomes, not tasks** | Have one person/team perform the entire process. |
| 2 | **Have those who use the output perform the process** | Integrate the customer into the process. |
| 3 | **Subsume information-processing work into the real work** | The person who produces info should also process it. |
| 4 | **Treat geographically dispersed resources as if centralized** | Use IT to unify distributed operations. |
| 5 | **Link parallel activities instead of just integrating results** | Coordinate work in real time, not at the end. |
| 6 | **Put the decision point where the work is performed** | Build control into the process (vertical compression). |
| 7 | **Capture information once, at the source** | Eliminate duplicate data entry across departments. |

---

## 2.3 The Seven Phases / Step-by-Step BPR Methodology

A typical KTU-expected BPR methodology consists of the following seven sequential stages:

> 1. **Envision / Identify the Need** — Senior management defines the strategic goals, scope, and expected outcomes.  
> 2. **Set Objectives and Performance Measures** — Quantify targets (e.g., "reduce order-to-delivery cycle from 14 days to 2 days").  
> 3. **Identify the Processes to be Reengineered** — Choose high-impact, broken processes (Pareto analysis).  
> 4. **Analyze and Measure Existing Processes** — Document AS-IS process maps (Flowcharts, SIPOC).  
> 5. **Create a New Process Design (TO-BE)** — Apply the 7 principles; benchmark against world-class.  
> 6. **Implement the New Process** — Pilot test, IT deployment, training, change management.  
> 7. **Continuously Improve and Refine** — Institutionalize feedback; measure against KPIs.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Although BPR is a management methodology, KTU expects quantitative measurement of process improvement using the following equations:

| # | Metric | Formula | Units | Purpose |
|---|--------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | **Process Cycle Efficiency (PCE)** | $\text{PCE} = \dfrac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}} \times 100$ | % | Measures how much of the cycle is value-adding. |
| 2 | **Process Improvement Factor** | $\text{PIF} = \dfrac{\text{Before Cycle Time}}{\text{After Cycle Time}}$ | Ratio | Quantifies the *dramatic* gain. |
| 3 | **Cost Reduction %** | $\Delta C\% = \dfrac{C_{\text{before}} - C_{\text{after}}}{C_{\text{before}}} \times 100$ | % | Financial impact of BPR. |
| 4 | **Defect / Error Rate** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Opportunities}}$ | DPMO | Quality outcome (Six Sigma link). |
| 5 | **Throughput** | $\text{TH} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Bottleneck Cycle Time}}$ | Units / hr | Capacity impact. |
| 6 | **ROI of BPR Project** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Savings from BPR}}{\text{Total BPR Cost}} \times 100$ | % | Justification metric. |
| 7 | **Reengineering Risk Index** | $\text{RRI} = w_{1}\cdot P + w_{2}\cdot C + w_{3}\cdot T$ | Score | $P$=People resistance, $C$=Cost overrun, $T$=Tech failure. |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Engineering Field Utility:**  
> - **Manufacturing:** Reengineer the order fulfillment process using ERP + IoT.  
> - **Healthcare:** Combine registration + billing + discharge into a single window.  
> - **Supply Chain:** Convert push-based ordering to a pull-based (JIT-linked) reengineered flow.  
> - **Service Industry:** Reengineer loan approval in banks from 7 days to 7 minutes.

---

## 2.5 When to Apply BPR (Trigger Conditions)

BPR should be initiated when **one or more** of the following symptoms exist:

- Cost of quality exceeds **15%** of revenue.
- Process cycle efficiency is below **20%**.
- Customer complaints have risen by more than **30%** YoY.
- Market share is declining for **3 consecutive quarters**.
- New technology (AI, Blockchain, IoT) has emerged that can obsolete the existing process.
- Major M&A, regulatory change, or competitive disruption has occurred.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Frameworks & Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation: The BPR Performance Quantification Model

> This analytical framework demonstrates how a student must quantitatively prove the *dramatic* improvement claimed by BPR for a KTU 14-mark question.

### Step 1 — Define the AS-IS (Current State) Process Parameters

Let us assume a typical Purchase Order (PO) processing workflow in a manufacturing firm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{va}}^{\text{before}} &= 2 \;\text{hours} \quad \text{(value-added time)} \\
T_{\text{nva}}^{\text{before}} &= 22 \;\text{hours} \quad \text{(non-value-added: waiting, approvals)} \\
T_{\text{total}}^{\text{before}} &= T_{\text{va}}^{\text{before}} + T_{\text{nva}}^{\text{before}} \\
T_{\text{total}}^{\text{before}} &= 2 + 22 = 24 \;\text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Total cycle time is the sum of value-added and non-value-added time (queue + wait + transport + inspection time).

### Step 2 — Compute the Before Process Cycle Efficiency (PCE_before)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PCE}_{\text{before}} &= \frac{T_{\text{va}}^{\text{before}}}{T_{\text{total}}^{\text{before}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2}{24} \times 100 \\
&= 8.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Only 8.33% of the cycle adds customer value. The remaining **91.67% is waste (Muda)**.

### Step 3 — Define the TO-BE (Reengineered) Process

After BPR (ERP-enabled workflow, single digital approval, automated notifications):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{va}}^{\text{after}} &= 1.5 \;\text{hours} \\
T_{\text{nva}}^{\text{after}} &= 0.5 \;\text{hours} \\
T_{\text{total}}^{\text{after}} &= 1.5 + 0.5 = 2 \;\text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the After PCE

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PCE}_{\text{after}} &= \frac{1.5}{2} \times 100 \\
&= 75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Process Improvement Factor (PIF)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PIF} &= \frac{T_{\text{total}}^{\text{before}}}{T_{\text{total}}^{\text{after}}} \\
&= \frac{24}{2} \\
&= 12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A PIF of **12x** satisfies Hammer's definition of *dramatic improvement* (≥ 10x).

### Step 6 — Compute Cost Reduction

If the per-order processing cost was ₹500 before and falls to ₹80 after:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C\% &= \frac{500 - 80}{500} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{420}{500} \times 100 \\
&= 84\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Compute ROI of the BPR Initiative

Let the BPR project cost (consultancy + software + training) be **₹25,00,000** and annual savings on **10,000 orders/year** be:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual Savings} &= 10{,}000 \times (500 - 80) = 10{,}000 \times 420 = ₹42{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{ROI} &= \frac{42{,}00{,}000 - 25{,}00{,}000}{25{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{17{,}00{,}000}{25{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 68\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** A **68% ROI** in year one, validating the financial viability of the BPR project.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping BPR Steps to a 14-Mark KTU Answer

| KTU Mark Weight | Expected Content | Bloom's Level |
|-----------------|------------------|---------------|
| 2 marks | Definition (Hammer & Champy) + 3 key terms | Remember |
| 3 marks | Distinguish BPR from TQM/Kaizen | Understand |
| 3 marks | List the 4 pillars OR 7 principles of BPR | Remember |
| 4 marks | Apply a 7-phase BPR methodology to a case study | Apply |
| 2 marks | Calculate PCE / PIF / ROI | Analyze |

---

## 3.3 IT Leverage — The BPR Capability Equation

BPR depends on IT as an enabler. The **BPR Capability Index (BCI)** can be modeled as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BCI} &= \alpha \cdot I + \beta \cdot P + \gamma \cdot C \\
\text{where:} \quad I &= \text{IT integration score (0–10)} \\
P &= \text{Process redesign score (0–10)} \\
C &= \text{Change management score (0–10)} \\
\alpha + \beta + \gamma &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

A BCI value **> 7.5** indicates a reengineered process; **< 4.0** indicates the process is still functionally siloed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The BPR Implementation Roadmap (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Envision Strategic Need] --> B[Step 2: Define Objectives and KPIs]
    B --> C[Step 3: Identify Target Processes via Pareto]
    C --> D[Step 4: Map AS-IS Process Flowchart and SIPOC]
    D --> E[Step 5: Analyze Value-Added vs Non-Value-Added Time]
    E --> F[Step 6: Design TO-BE Process Using 7 Principles]
    F --> G[Step 7: Prototype and Simulate New Process]
    G --> H[Step 8: Pilot Test in a Single Department]
    H --> I[Step 9: Full-Scale Rollout with IT Deployment]
    I --> J[Step 10: Measure KPIs and Benchmark]
    J --> K[Step 11: Continuous Improvement Loop]
    K --> A
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each block is a sequential phase. The arrow returning from Step 11 to Step 1 represents the **continuous feedback loop** that BPR institutionalizes.

---

## 4.2 The Reengineering Transformation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[AS-IS Functional Silos] --> A2[Vertical Hierarchy]
    A2 --> A3[Manual Paper-Based Work]
    A3 --> A4[Long Cycle Time High Cost]

    B1[Reengineering Trigger] --> C1[TO-BE Process Teams]
    C1 --> C2[Flat Empowered Structure]
    C2 --> C3[IT Enabled Automated Workflow]
    C3 --> C4[Short Cycle Low Cost High Quality]

    A4 --> B1
    C4 --> D1[Dramatic 10x Improvement]
    D1 --> E1[Strategic Competitive Advantage]
```

---

## 4.3 BPR vs. Kaizen vs. Six Sigma — Comparative Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RAD[Radical Transformation]
        A[BPR]
    end
    subgraph INC[Incremental Improvement]
        B[Kaizen / TQM]
        C[Six Sigma]
    end
    subgraph STR[Structural Change]
        D[Restructuring]
    end
    RAD --> E[10x to 100x Gains]
    INC --> F[1.05x to 3x Gains]
    STR --> G[Cost Cut / Org Chart Change]
```

---

## 4.4 BPR Critical Success Factors (CSF) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CSFBPR[BPR Critical Success Factors]
    CSFBPR --> CSF1[Strong Top Management Sponsorship]
    CSFBPR --> CSF2[Clear Vision and Strategic Alignment]
    CSFBPR --> CSF3[Skilled Reengineering Team]
    CSFBPR --> CSF4[Robust IT Infrastructure]
    CSFBPR --> CSF5[Effective Change Management]
    CSFBPR --> CSF6[Customer Centric Focus]
    CSFBPR --> CSF7[Performance Measurement System]
```

---

## 4.5 SIPOC Diagram — A Sample Reengineered Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Suppliers: Vendors and Internal IT] --> P[Process: PO Approval Automated via ERP]
    P --> O[Outputs: Approved PO Delivered to Vendor]
    P --> I[Inputs: PR and Budget Approval]
    O --> C[Customers: Production Floor and Vendor]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define Business Process Reengineering. List any two characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Business Process Reengineering, as defined by **Michael Hammer and James Champy (1993)**, is the **fundamental rethinking and radical redesign of business processes** to achieve **dramatic improvements** in critical performance measures such as cost, quality, service, and speed.

**Two characteristics:**
1. **Radical** — It discards existing structures and rebuilds from scratch rather than modifying them.
2. **Process-focused** — Emphasis is on end-to-end workflows, not on individual functional departments.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for two characteristics = 3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Distinguish between BPR and TQM in any three dimensions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Dimension | BPR | TQM |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Change Type** | Radical and discontinuous | Incremental and continuous |
| **Performance Gain** | 10x to 100x | 1.05x to 1.10x |
| **Driving Force** | IT-enabled process redesign | People, culture, and quality tools |

*(Award 1 mark for each correct contrast = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain the **four pillars of BPR** as proposed by Hammer and Champy with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step methodology for implementing a BPR project** in a manufacturing organization. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for (a) — Four Pillars of BPR (7 marks)**

**1. Process Orientation (2 marks):**  
Work is organized around **outcomes/processes**, not around tasks or functional departments.  
*Example:* Instead of a sales clerk raising an order, a credit checker approving it, and a warehouse executive shipping it, a single **"Order Fulfillment Case Manager"** handles the entire process from inquiry to delivery. This eliminates hand-offs and reduces errors.

**2. Horizontal Compression (2 marks):**  
Combine multiple parallel steps into a single step.  
*Example:* In a bank, the loan process previously required the applicant to visit the branch three times — for application, document submission, and disbursement. Post-BPR, all three steps occur in a single visit via a tablet-based digital KYC system.

**3. Vertical Compression (2 marks):**  
Push decision-making authority to the front-line worker.  
*Example:* A front-line customer service agent is empowered to approve a return or refund up to ₹50,000 without manager approval, drastically reducing escalation time.

**4. Information Technology as Enabler (1 mark):**  
IT is the lever that makes reengineering possible — through databases, ERP, workflow automation, and analytics.  
*Example:* Salesforce CRM enables a 360° customer view, eliminating multiple data re-entries.

---

### **Model Answer for (b) — BPR Implementation Methodology (7 marks)**

The seven-phase methodology is as follows:

**1. Envision (1 mark):** Top management identifies the strategic need — e.g., order-to-delivery cycle must drop from 14 days to 2 days to retain a key retail customer.

**2. Set Objectives and KPIs (1 mark):** Quantify targets — PCE must rise from 10% to 70%; cost per order must fall by 60%.

**3. Identify Target Processes (1 mark):** Use Pareto analysis to find the 20% of processes causing 80% of the delay — e.g., PO approval and credit verification.

**4. Map and Analyze AS-IS Process (1 mark):** Build a flowchart; classify each step as value-added (VA) or non-value-added (NVA). Compute the current PCE.

**5. Design the TO-BE Process (1 mark):** Apply the 7 principles — replace handoffs with integrated case managers; use ERP for parallel approvals; embed decision authority in the system.

**6. Implement the New Process (1 mark):** Pilot in one regional office; train staff; deploy IT systems; manage change through communication and incentives.

**7. Measure, Refine, and Institutionalize (1 mark):** Compare KPIs against AS-IS; run continuous improvement loops; lock in gains via SOPs and dashboards.

---

### **Question B. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]**
**(a)** With a **suitable numerical example**, compute the **Process Cycle Efficiency (PCE)** and **Process Improvement Factor (PIF)** before and after a reengineering initiative. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Discuss the **role of Information Technology** as a critical enabler of BPR. List **four IT tools** commonly used. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for (a) — PCE & PIF Computation (7 marks)**

**Given Data (3 marks):**
- **Before BPR:** Value-added time = 3 hours; Non-value-added time = 27 hours.  
- **After BPR:** Value-added time = 2 hours; Non-value-added time = 1 hour.

**Step 1 — Total Cycle Time Before (1 mark):**  
$T_{\text{total}}^{\text{before}} = 3 + 27 = 30$ hours

**Step 2 — PCE Before (1 mark):**  
$\text{PCE}_{\text{before}} = \frac{3}{30} \times 100 = 10\%$

**Step 3 — Total Cycle Time After (1 mark):**  
$T_{\text{total}}^{\text{after}} = 2 + 1 = 3$ hours

**Step 4 — PCE After and PIF (1 mark):**  
$\text{PCE}_{\text{after}} = \frac{2}{3} \times 100 = 66.67\%$  
$\text{PIF} = \frac{30}{3} = 10$  

**Conclusion:** The PIF of **10x** confirms a *dramatic* improvement as defined by Hammer.

---

### **Model Answer for (b) — Role of IT in BPR (7 marks)**

**Role of IT (3 marks):**  
1. **Disintermediation** — IT removes the middleman layers (e.g., brokers in insurance).  
2. **Process Integration** — ERP links procurement, production, and finance in real time.  
3. **Mass Customization** — IT enables tailoring of offerings at scale.  
4. **Process Automation** — Workflow engines remove manual handoffs and approvals.

**Four IT Tools (4 marks):**  
1. **ERP Systems** (SAP, Oracle) — unify cross-functional data.  
2. **CRM Platforms** (Salesforce) — centralize customer interactions.  
3. **Workflow Automation** (BPM tools like Bizagi) — orchestrate processes.  
4. **Data Analytics & AI/ML** — enable smart decision-making and predictive insights.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls in BPR Answers:**  
> 1. **Confusing BPR with Downsizing (Layoffs).** BPR is about *process redesign*; staff reductions are a *side-effect*, not the goal. Writing only about firing employees will fetch **zero marks**.  
> 2. **Writing BPR and TQM as synonyms.** Examiners deduct **2–3 marks** for failing to highlight the *radical vs incremental* distinction.  
> 3. **Skipping the Quantitative Component.** KTU 14-mark questions expect at least one numerical (PCE/PIF/ROI). Skipping math costs up to **3–4 marks**.  
> 4. **Forgetting IT as the Enabler.** Hammer explicitly identified IT as the *single most important enabler*. Omitting it is treated as an incomplete answer.  
> 5. **Using the word "reengineering" loosely.** Never use it as a synonym for "improving" or "reorganizing". The 4 key words — *fundamental, radical, dramatic, process* — must appear in the definition.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Exam-Ready):**
> - **Author / Year:** Michael Hammer and James Champy, 1993.
> - **Four Key Words:** Fundamental, Radical, Dramatic, Process.
> - **Three Catalysts:** Customer Power, Competition, Change.
> - **Four Pillars:** Process Orientation, Horizontal Compression, Vertical Compression, IT Enablement.
> - **Seven Principles:** Outcomes over tasks, integrate customer, subsume info-processing, virtual centralization, parallel linking, decentralized decisions, capture info once at source.
> - **Seven Phases:** Envision → Set Objectives → Identify Processes → Analyze AS-IS → Design TO-BE → Implement → Continuous Improvement.
> - **Key Metrics:** PCE, PIF, DPMO, ROI, RRI.
> - **BPR ≠ TQM ≠ Kaizen ≠ Restructuring.** Always highlight the *radical vs incremental* axis.
> - **IT Enablers:** ERP, CRM, BPM, AI/ML, IoT.
> - **Critical Success Factors:** Top-management sponsorship, vision, skilled team, IT, change management, customer focus, KPIs.
> - **Typical Pitfall to Avoid:** Confusing BPR with downsizing or with continuous improvement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Customer Relationship Management (CRM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET601 - INDUSTRIAL AND SYSTEMS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Customer Relationship Management (CRM) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Customer Relationship Management (CRM)** is a comprehensive, technology-driven business strategy that combines people, processes, and technology to acquire, retain, and grow profitable customer relationships. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme Industrial and Systems Engineering framework, CRM is defined as: > **Customer Relationship Management (CRM):** A managerial philosophy and integrated set of processes, supported by information technology, focused on creating and maintaining long-term, mutually beneficial relationships between an organization and its customers, thereby maximizing customer lifetime value (CLV) and organizational profitability. In the context of Industrial and Systems Engineering, CRM extends beyond marketing into **operations, supply chain coordination, after-sales service engineering**, and **product lifecycle management**. It is recognized as a critical **demand-side enabler** that balances the production-centric focus of traditional industrial engineering. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCMET601 – Module 3):** > CRM is studied under the broader umbrella of *Industrial Management Systems* and *Strategic Operations Planning*. Students must understand CRM not just as an IT tool, but as a **strategic management philosophy** that integrates with **ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning), TQM (Total Quality Management), and Lean Systems**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Real-World Analogy: The Local Tea Stall vs. A Five-Star Hotel** Imagine two establishments serving tea: 1. **The Local Tea Stall (No CRM):** The owner remembers nobody. Every customer is a transaction. If you visit once and leave, he has no way to follow up, no record of your preference, and no idea if you enjoyed the experience. He acquires new customers daily but loses them just as quickly. 2. **The Five-Star Hotel (Strong CRM):** The hotel remembers your name, your preferred beverage, your dietary restrictions, your last visit date, and your feedback. On your birthday, they send a personalized greeting. When you return, your room preference is already set. They treat every customer like a **long-term guest, not a one-time visitor**. **The Takeaway:** CRM is essentially the systematic, data-driven version of "remembering your customer" — scaled across millions of customers using technology. > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Summary for First-Time Learners:** > CRM = A company's memory + a company's courtesy + a company's analytical brain — all working together to make every customer feel valued and to make every business decision customer-aware. --- ## 1.3 Key Metrics and Standards Used in CRM The following **standard metrics** are universally applied in CRM analytics and will appear in KTU numerical problems: - **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV):** Total revenue a business can expect from a single customer account throughout the business relationship. Measured in **monetary units (₹ / $)**. - **Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC):** Total cost of acquiring a new customer. Measured in **monetary units**. - **Churn Rate:** Percentage of customers lost during a given period. Measured as a **% (0–100)**. - **Retention Rate:** Percentage of customers retained over a period. Measured as a **% (0–100)**. - **Net Promoter Score (NPS):** Loyalty metric ranging from **-100 to +100**. > [!NOTE] > **Standard CRM Constant for NPS Categorization:** > - Promoters: Score **9–10** (loyal enthusiasts) > - Passives: Score **7–8** (satisfied but unenthusiastic) > - Detractors: Score **0–6** (unhappy customers) These metrics are foundational to evaluating any CRM program's effectiveness in an industrial setting. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of CRM CRM operates on three interdependent pillars. The KTU 2024 syllabus requires students to articulate these pillars and their interconnections. ### Pillar 1: Customer Strategy (The "Why") This is the **strategic layer** that defines: - **Customer Segmentation:** Dividing customers into homogeneous groups based on demographics, behavior, geography, or psychographics. Common models include **RFM Analysis (Recency, Frequency, Monetary)**. - **Value Proposition:** Articulating why a customer should choose *this* firm over competitors. - **Customer Journey Mapping:** Visualizing every touchpoint a customer experiences — from awareness → consideration → purchase → retention → advocacy. ### Pillar 2: CRM Processes (The "How") Operational processes that execute the strategy: - **Marketing Automation:** Email campaigns, lead scoring, drip campaigns. - **Sales Force Automation (SFA):** Pipeline management, quote generation, opportunity tracking. - **Service Automation:** Help-desk ticketing, complaint resolution, warranty management. ### Pillar 3: CRM Technology (The "With What") The enabling IT infrastructure: - **Operational CRM Systems:** Salesforce, HubSpot, Zoho CRM. - **Analytical CRM Tools:** SAS, IBM Cognos, Power BI dashboards. - **Collaborative CRM Platforms:** Social CRM, community forums, chatbot integrations. --- ## 2.2 Types of CRM (KTU High-Yield Classification) The KTU 2024 module expects students to classify CRM into three types. Below is the detailed breakdown: ### A. Operational CRM **Definition:** Streamlines and automates customer-facing business processes — sales, marketing, and service operations. **Core Functions:** 1. **Sales Force Automation (SFA):** Lead management, contact management, sales forecasting. 2. **Marketing Automation:** Campaign management, customer segmentation, lead nurturing. 3. **Service Automation:** Case management, knowledge base, customer support workflows. **Real-World Utility:** Used by manufacturing firms to manage B2B distributor relationships, track industrial equipment installations, and coordinate after-sales service calls. ### B. Analytical CRM **Definition:** Uses data mining, statistical analysis, and OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) to extract actionable customer intelligence from historical and real-time data. **Core Functions:** 1. **Customer Segmentation:** Identifying high-value vs. low-value customers. 2. **Churn Prediction:** Forecasting which customers are likely to leave. 3. **Market Basket Analysis:** Determining cross-sell and up-sell opportunities. 4. **CLV Calculation:** Estimating future revenue per customer. **Real-World Utility:** A consumer electronics manufacturer uses analytical CRM to predict which customers are likely to upgrade their device within 6 months, then targets them with personalized upgrade offers. ### C. Collaborative CRM **Definition:** Integrates and shares customer data across all organizational departments and external stakeholders (suppliers, distributors, partners) to provide a unified customer view. **Core Functions:** 1. **Interaction Management:** Coordinating communication across email, phone, chat, social media. 2. **Document Management:** Centralized storage of customer-related documents. 3. **Partner Relationship Management (PRM):** Coordinating with channel partners. **Real-World Utility:** An automotive OEM uses collaborative CRM to ensure that the dealer, service center, finance arm, and insurance partner all access the same customer profile, providing seamless ownership experience. --- ## 2.3 The CRM Value Chain The CRM value chain is a sequential framework showing how raw customer data converts into business value: **Step 1 → Data Capture:** Collect data from POS, website, social media, call centers. **Step 2 → Data Integration:** Consolidate into a unified customer database. **Step 3 → Data Analysis:** Apply statistical and ML models to extract patterns. **Step 4 → Insight Generation:** Convert patterns into actionable business rules. **Step 5 → Action Execution:** Trigger campaigns, offers, or service interventions. **Step 6 → Performance Measurement:** Track KPIs like CLV, NPS, churn rate. **Step 7 → Feedback Loop:** Refine strategy based on measured outcomes. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following table contains all key CRM formulas expected in KTU 2024 numerical problems. | **Metric** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Boundary Condition** | |---|---|---|---| | Customer Lifetime Value (Simple) | $\text{CLV} = \text{AVG} \times \text{AFR} \times \text{ALT}$ | ₹ / $ | ALT $\geq 1$ year | | Customer Lifetime Value (Discounted) | $\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{R_t}{(1+d)^t} - \text{CAC}$ | ₹ / $ | $0 \leq d < 1$ | | Customer Acquisition Cost | $\text{CAC} = \dfrac{\text{Total Sales \& Marketing Cost}}{\text{Number of New Customers Acquired}}$ | ₹ / $ | CAC $> 0$ | | Churn Rate | $\text{Churn \%} = \dfrac{\text{Customers Lost}}{\text{Customers at Start}} \times 100$ | % | $0 \leq \text{Churn} \leq 100$ | | Retention Rate | $\text{Retention \%} = 100 - \text{Churn \%}$ | % | $0 \leq \text{Retention} \leq 100$ | | Net Promoter Score | $\text{NPS} = \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors}$ | Score | $-100 \leq \text{NPS} \leq +100$ | | Customer Profitability Ratio | $\text{CPR} = \dfrac{\text{Customer Profit}}{\text{Customer Cost}}$ | Ratio | CPR $\geq 0$ | | Repeat Purchase Ratio | $\text{RPR} = \dfrac{\text{Returning Customers}}{\text{Total Customers}} \times 100$ | % | $0 \leq \text{RPR} \leq 100$ | **Where the variables denote:** - **AVG** = Average Order Value - **AFR** = Annual Frequency of Purchase - **ALT** = Average Customer Lifespan (in years) - **$R_t$** = Net Revenue from customer in year $t$ - **$d$** = Annual discount rate (decimal) - **$n$** = Forecast horizon (years) - **CAC** = Customer Acquisition Cost > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** The Discounted CLV formula is the **most frequently asked 14-mark derivation** in CRM modules. Students must show every summation step, justify the discount factor, and explain why subtracting CAC is essential (to reflect net profitability, not gross revenue). --- ## 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility CRM is not limited to consumer-facing businesses. In **industrial engineering**, CRM plays a crucial role in: 1. **B2B Sales Pipeline Management:** Tracking long-cycle industrial sales (₹ crores per contract). 2. **After-Sales Service Engineering:** Coordinating field service teams for industrial equipment maintenance. 3. **Spare Parts Demand Forecasting:** Using customer equipment-base data to predict spare parts demand. 4. **Warranty Management:** Tracking warranty claims, identifying design flaws, feeding insights back to product engineering. 5. **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) Integration:** Linking CRM with maintenance schedules to provide proactive customer notifications. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) — The Simplified Model ### Assumptions 1. Customer behavior is **stationary** (purchase frequency remains constant year over year). 2. Profit margin is **uniform** across all transactions. 3. No discounting of future cash flows (the **simple CLV** model). ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Define Average Order Value (AVG):** The average revenue generated per customer transaction is calculated as: $$ \text{AVG} = \frac{\text{Total Revenue in Period}}{\text{Total Number of Orders in Period}} $$ **Step 2 — Define Annual Purchase Frequency (AFR):** The average number of times a customer purchases per year: $$ \text{AFR} = \frac{\text{Number of Purchases by Customer per Year}}{1} $$ Typically measured over a 12-month rolling window. **Step 3 — Define Average Customer Lifespan (ALT):** The expected number of years the customer continues purchasing: $$ \text{ALT} = \frac{1}{\text{Annual Churn Rate}} $$ This is derived from the **exponential decay assumption** of customer retention, which states that if a customer has probability $p$ of churning each year, the expected lifespan is $1/p$. **Step 4 — Combine into CLV:** The total revenue a single customer generates over their entire relationship with the firm is the product of these three metrics: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CLV} &= \text{Revenue per Year} \times \text{Number of Years} \\[4pt] &= (\text{AVG} \times \text{AFR}) \times \text{ALT} \\[4pt] \text{CLV} &= \text{AVG} \times \text{AFR} \times \text{ALT} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Explanation of Step 4:** "Multiply how much they spend per purchase × how often they buy per year × how many years they stay = total lifetime value." **Step 5 — Convert to Profit-Based CLV (Optional Extension):** To convert revenue-based CLV to profit-based CLV, multiply by the **gross profit margin** ($m$): $$ \text{CLV}_{\text{profit}} = \text{AVG} \times \text{AFR} \times \text{ALT} \times m $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Discounted CLV (Full Version with CAC) This is the **exam-grade derivation** required for 14-mark questions. ### Step 1 — Express Yearly Net Cash Flow Let $R_t$ = Net cash flow (revenue − variable cost) from the customer in year $t$. ### Step 2 — Apply Time Value of Money A rupee earned in year $t$ is worth less than a rupee today. Discount it back to present value using rate $d$: $$ \text{PV of Year } t \text{ Cash Flow} = \frac{R_t}{(1+d)^t} $$ ### Step 3 — Sum Over Forecast Horizon Sum the discounted cash flows over all years of expected customer life: $$ \text{Present Value of Customer} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{R_t}{(1+d)^t} $$ ### Step 4 — Subtract Acquisition Cost The firm spent CAC to acquire this customer. For net value, subtract it: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CLV} &= \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{R_t}{(1+d)^t} - \text{CAC} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on Sign Convention:** CAC is incurred at $t = 0$ (today), so it is NOT discounted. It is subtracted directly. ### Step 5 — Simplify the Constant Cash Flow Case If $R_t = R$ is constant every year (a common exam assumption), the geometric series yields: $$ \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{R}{(1+d)^t} = R \cdot \frac{1 - (1+d)^{-n}}{d} $$ Therefore, the closed-form CLV is: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CLV} = R \cdot \frac{1 - (1+d)^{-n}}{d} - \text{CAC} \end{aligned} $$ This is the **annuity present value formula** applied to customer cash flows. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Pattern) ### Problem Statement A manufacturing firm sells industrial pumps. The following data is provided: - Average Order Value per customer = **₹ 50,000** - Average purchase frequency = **2 times per year** - Average customer lifespan = **8 years** - Gross profit margin = **20%** - Customer Acquisition Cost = **₹ 15,000** **Calculate:** (a) Revenue-based CLV (b) Profit-based CLV (c) Comment on whether CAC is justified. ### Solution **Part (a) — Revenue-Based CLV:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CLV}_{\text{revenue}} &= \text{AVG} \times \text{AFR} \times \text{ALT} \\ &= 50{,}000 \times 2 \times 8 \\ &= ₹ 8{,}00{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Part (b) — Profit-Based CLV:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CLV}_{\text{profit}} &= \text{CLV}_{\text{revenue}} \times m \\ &= 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.20 \\ &= ₹ 1{,}60{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Part (c) — Comment on CAC Justification:** The CAC of ₹ 15,000 is significantly lower than the profit-based CLV of ₹ 1,60,000. $$ \text{CLV : CAC Ratio} = \frac{1{,}60{,}000}{15{,}000} \approx 10.67 : 1 $$ A healthy ratio is generally **3:1 or higher**. The 10.67:1 ratio indicates that the firm is acquiring customers **highly profitably** and CRM investments are well-justified. --- ## 3.4 NPS Calculation Walkthrough ### Problem Statement A CRM survey of 500 customers yielded the following responses on the 0–10 scale: - **Promoters (9–10):** 220 customers - **Passives (7–8):** 180 customers - **Detractors (0–6):** 100 customers **Calculate the Net Promoter Score (NPS).** ### Solution **Step 1 — Convert to Percentages:** $$ \begin{aligned} \%\text{Promoters} &= \frac{220}{500} \times 100 = 44\% \\[4pt] \%\text{Detractors} &= \frac{100}{500} \times 100 = 20\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply NPS Formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{NPS} &= \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors} \\ &= 44 - 20 \\ &= +24 \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** An NPS of +24 is **positive**, indicating that the firm's customer base is **net loyal**. Industry benchmarks suggest NPS above +30 is excellent, while below 0 indicates critical dissatisfaction requiring immediate intervention. --- ## 3.5 Churn Rate and Retention Rate — Worked Example ### Problem Statement An industrial supply company started the year with **2,000 active customers**. During the year: - **120 customers** stopped purchasing (churned). - **80 customers** were reacquired (returned after a gap). **Calculate:** (a) Churn Rate, (b) Retention Rate. ### Solution **Part (a) — Churn Rate:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Churn Rate} &= \frac{120}{2000} \times 100 \\ &= 6\% \end{aligned} $$ **Part (b) — Retention Rate:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Retention Rate} &= 100\% - 6\% \\ &= 94\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examination Tip:** Reacquired customers are **NOT** counted as retained during the original churn window. They are treated as **new acquisitions** for retention accounting purposes. This is a common trick question in KTU exams. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 CRM Architectural Flow Diagram The following Mermaid diagram depicts the end-to-end CRM architecture — from data acquisition through to business value realization. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Customer Touchpoints
POS Website Social Media Call Centre Email] --> B1[Data Capture Layer
OLTP Systems Forms APIs] B1 --> C1[Centralized Customer Database
Single Customer View] C1 --> D1[Data Warehouse
Historical and Aggregated Data] D1 --> E1[Analytical Engine
Data Mining ML Models OLAP] E1 --> F1[Insight Generation
Segmentation Churn Prediction CLV Estimation] F1 --> G1[Action Layer
Campaigns Offers Service Interventions] G1 --> H1[Customer Experience
Improved Loyalty Increased CLV] H1 --> I1[Business Value
Higher Revenue Lower CAC Reduced Churn] I1 -.Feedback Loop.-> A1 ``` --- ## 4.2 Three Types of CRM Classification Diagram This Mermaid block shows the three CRM typologies, their functions, and how they interrelate to form an integrated CRM system. ```mermaid graph LR subgraph OperationalCRM[Operational CRM] OA[Sales Force Automation] OB[Marketing Automation] OC[Service Automation] end subgraph AnalyticalCRM[Analytical CRM] AA[Customer Segmentation] AB[Churn Prediction] AC[CLV Calculation] AD[Market Basket Analysis] end subgraph CollaborativeCRM[Collaborative CRM] CA[Interaction Management] CB[Document Management] CC[Partner Relationship Management] end OA --> AA OB --> AA OC --> AB AA --> CA AC --> CA AB --> CC AC --> CA CA --> CB ``` --- ## 4.3 CRM Implementation Lifecycle The diagram below illustrates the six-phase CRM implementation roadmap recommended in the KTU 2024 syllabus. ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Phase 1: Vision and Strategy] --> P2[Phase 2: Customer Analysis] P2 --> P3[Phase 3: Process Redesign] P3 --> P4[Phase 4: Technology Selection] P4 --> P5[Phase 5: Pilot Deployment] P5 --> P6[Phase 6: Full Rollout and Continuous Improvement] P6 -.Iterative Refinement.-> P1 ``` --- ## 4.4 Customer Journey Funnel This block illustrates the stages a customer moves through, and how CRM intervenes at each stage. ```mermaid flowchart TD J1[Awareness Stage] --> J2[Consideration Stage] J2 --> J3[Purchase Decision] J3 --> J4[Onboarding and Use] J4 --> J5[Retention and Loyalty] J5 --> J6[Advocacy Stage] J6 -.Loop.-> J1 K1[CRM Marketing Campaigns] -.Targets.-> J1 K2[CRM Lead Scoring] -.Engages.-> J2 K3[CRM Sales Automation] -.Closes.-> J3 K4[CRM Service Automation] -.Supports.-> J4 K5[CRM Loyalty Programs] -.Retains.-> J5 K6[CRM Referral Programs] -.Activates.-> J6 ``` --- ## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow For students who need a high-level architectural map of CRM's integration with other enterprise systems, the following Mermaid block presents a **functional architecture flow** that links CRM with ERP, MES, and SCM systems commonly used in industrial engineering environments. ```mermaid flowchart TD M1[Customer Order] --> M2[CRM Sales Module] M2 --> M3[ERP System] M3 --> M4[Manufacturing Execution System MES] M4 --> M5[Shop Floor Production] M5 --> M6[Quality Inspection] M6 --> M7[Warehouse and SCM] M7 --> M8[Delivery to Customer] M8 --> M9[CRM Service Module] M9 --> M10[After Sales Support] M10 --> M11[Customer Feedback Loop] M11 --> M2 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]** **Define Customer Relationship Management (CRM). List the three primary types of CRM with one example function of each.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): **Definition (1 Mark):** Customer Relationship Management (CRM) is a comprehensive business strategy that uses people, processes, and technology to manage and analyze customer interactions and data throughout the customer lifecycle, with the goal of improving customer retention, driving sales growth, and maximizing customer lifetime value. **Three Types (2 Marks):** 1. **Operational CRM:** Automates and streamlines customer-facing processes such as **Sales Force Automation (SFA)**. 2. **Analytical CRM:** Uses data mining and statistical analysis for **customer segmentation and churn prediction**. 3. **Collaborative CRM:** Integrates communication across departments and partners via **shared interaction management systems**. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]** **Explain the difference between Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) and Customer Lifetime Value (CLV). Why is the CLV-to-CAC ratio considered a critical CRM metric?** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): **Difference (2 Marks):** - **Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC)** is the **cost incurred** to acquire a new customer. It is calculated as total sales and marketing expenditure divided by the number of new customers acquired in a given period. - **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** is the **total revenue or profit** a customer is expected to generate over the entire duration of their relationship with the firm. **Importance of CLV-to-CAC Ratio (1 Mark):** The ratio $\frac{\text{CLV}}{\text{CAC}}$ indicates the **profitability efficiency** of customer acquisition. A ratio of **3:1 or higher** is considered healthy. If the ratio is below 1, the firm is losing money on every customer acquired, signalling unsustainable CRM economics. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern:** Part B questions carry **internal choice**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Both options are provided below for parallel practice. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Set A]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Define CRM. With a neat diagram, explain the three types of CRM and their interrelationships. Give two examples of how CRM is used in an industrial engineering context. **(b) [7 Marks]** A telecom equipment manufacturer has the following data: - Average Order Value per customer = **₹ 75,000** - Annual purchase frequency = **1.5** - Average customer lifespan = **6 years** - Gross profit margin = **25%** - Customer Acquisition Cost = **₹ 20,000** - Annual discount rate = **10%** **Calculate the Discounted Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) using the constant cash flow assumption. Comment on whether the CAC investment is justified.** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]: **Definition (2 Marks):** As defined in Section 1, CRM is a comprehensive business strategy combining people, processes, and technology to manage customer relationships across the entire lifecycle, aimed at maximizing CLV and organizational profitability. **Three Types with Interrelationships (3 Marks):** The three types of CRM — Operational, Analytical, and Collaborative — work together in an integrated architecture: 1. **Operational CRM** captures customer interaction data through sales, marketing, and service automation. 2. **Analytical CRM** processes this data to extract insights such as segmentation, churn risk, and lifetime value. 3. **Collaborative CRM** disseminates these insights across internal departments and external partners, ensuring unified customer experience. The interrelationship is **sequential**: Operational → Analytical → Collaborative, with feedback loops at every stage. **Two Industrial Engineering Examples (2 Marks):** 1. **After-Sales Service Coordination:** An industrial pump manufacturer uses Operational CRM to dispatch field service engineers, Analytical CRM to predict equipment failures based on service history, and Collaborative CRM to coordinate with spare parts suppliers for just-in-time delivery. 2. **B2B Pipeline Management:** An automotive component manufacturer uses Analytical CRM to score leads based on order history, Operational CRM to automate quotation generation, and Collaborative CRM to share customer data with channel partners. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]: **Step 1 — Calculate Annual Constant Cash Flow R:** $$ \begin{aligned} R &= \text{AVG} \times \text{AFR} \times m \\ &= 75{,}000 \times 1.5 \times 0.25 \\ &= ₹ 28{,}125 \text{ per year} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Discounted CLV Formula:** $$ \text{CLV} = R \cdot \frac{1 - (1+d)^{-n}}{d} - \text{CAC} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute Values ($d = 0.10$, $n = 6$):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CLV} &= 28{,}125 \times \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-6}}{0.10} - 20{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the Discount Factor:** $$ \begin{aligned} (1.10)^{6} &= 1.771561 \\[4pt] (1.10)^{-6} &= \frac{1}{1.771561} \approx 0.5645 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Complete the Annuity Calculation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{1 - 0.5645}{0.10} &= \frac{0.4355}{0.10} = 4.355 \\[4pt] R \cdot 4.355 &= 28{,}125 \times 4.355 = ₹ 1{,}22{,}484.38 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Subtract CAC:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CLV} &= 1{,}22{,}484.38 - 20{,}000 \\ &= ₹ 1{,}02{,}484.38 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — CLV-to-CAC Ratio and Justification:** $$ \frac{\text{CLV}}{\text{CAC}} = \frac{1{,}02{,}484.38}{20{,}000} \approx 5.12 : 1 $$ **Conclusion (Comment):** Since the ratio is significantly above the industry benchmark of 3:1, the CAC investment is **highly justified**. The firm generates approximately ₹ 5.12 in net lifetime value for every ₹ 1 spent on customer acquisition, indicating a profitable and sustainable CRM economics. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Set B]** **(a) [7 Marks]** What is Net Promoter Score (NPS)? Explain its calculation method, the three categories of respondents, and its significance as a CRM metric. **(b) [7 Marks]** A B2B industrial supply firm conducted a customer satisfaction survey. Out of 1,000 respondents: - **350** rated **9 or 10** (Promoters) - **400** rated **7 or 8** (Passives) - **250** rated **0 to 6** (Detractors) **Calculate:** (i) The Net Promoter Score, (ii) The percentage of Passives, and (iii) Provide an interpretation of the NPS result with recommended actions for the firm. #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]: **Definition (2 Marks):** Net Promoter Score (NPS) is a customer loyalty metric developed by **Fred Reichheld** (2003) that measures the willingness of customers to recommend a company's products or services to others. It serves as a leading indicator of customer retention and business growth. **Three Respondent Categories (3 Marks):** 1. **Promoters (Score 9–10):** Loyal enthusiasts who will continue buying and refer others. They fuel growth. 2. **Passives (Score 7–8):** Satisfied but unenthusiastic customers who are vulnerable to competitive offerings. 3. **Detractors (Score 0–6):** Unhappy customers who can damage the brand through negative word-of-mouth and churn. **Calculation Method (1 Mark):** $$ \text{NPS} = \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors} $$ **Significance (1 Mark):** NPS ranges from **-100 to +100**. A high NPS correlates strongly with **customer retention, lower churn, and higher CLV**. It is widely used in CRM programs to track loyalty over time and benchmark against competitors. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]: **Part (i) — NPS Calculation (3 Marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \%\text{Promoters} &= \frac{350}{1000} \times 100 = 35\% \\[4pt] \%\text{Detractors} &= \frac{250}{1000} \times 100 = 25\% \\[4pt] \text{NPS} &= 35 - 25 = +10 \end{aligned} $$ **Part (ii) — Percentage of Passives (2 Marks):** $$ \%\text{Passives} = \frac{400}{1000} \times 100 = 40\% $$ **Part (iii) — Interpretation and Recommendations (2 Marks):** An NPS of **+10** indicates that the firm has a **marginally positive** customer loyalty posture. While the firm is not in a negative zone, it is **far from the industry-best benchmark of +30 or higher**. The very high percentage of Passives (40%) represents a **strategic risk**, as they are easily swayed by competitors. **Recommended Actions:** 1. Convert Passives into Promoters through proactive engagement, loyalty rewards, and personalized service. 2. Conduct root-cause analysis on Detractor feedback to identify service gaps. 3. Implement closed-loop feedback — follow up personally with every Detractor within 48 hours. 4. Introduce NPS tracking at quarterly intervals to monitor improvement trends. --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in KTU CRM Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting to subtract CAC** in the Discounted CLV formula. Many students compute only the gross annuity value. This costs **2 marks** instantly. > 2. **Not converting profit margin correctly** when computing $R$ in CLV problems. Students often use revenue instead of profit, leading to inflated CLV values. > 3. **Misclassifying Passives as Promoters or Detractors** in NPS problems. Passives are a **separate category** and do not enter the NPS formula. > 4. **Mixing up churn and retention rates.** Retention Rate = 100% − Churn Rate. A student writing "Churn Rate = 100% − Retention Rate" loses **1 mark** for conceptual inversion. > 5. **Not stating units** (₹, %, years) in numerical answers. KTU examiners are strict about dimensional consistency in industrial engineering answers. > 6. **Providing CRM definitions without examples.** The KTU 2024 scheme rewards application-level answers. A definition without an industrial example loses at least **1 mark**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for CRM:** - **CRM** is a strategic philosophy, not merely software. It integrates **people, process, and technology**. - **Three Types of CRM:** Operational (automation), Analytical (insights), Collaborative (coordination). - **CLV (Simple):** $\text{CLV} = \text{AVG} \times \text{AFR} \times \text{ALT}$. - **CLV (Discounted, Constant Cash Flow):** $\text{CLV} = R \cdot \frac{1-(1+d)^{-n}}{d} - \text{CAC}$. - **CAC:** Total acquisition spend ÷ Number of new customers. - **Healthy CLV:CAC Ratio:** $\geq 3:1$. - **Churn Rate:** (Customers Lost ÷ Customers at Start) × 100. - **Retention Rate:** 100% − Churn Rate. - **NPS Formula:** %Promoters − %Detractors; Range is **−100 to +100**. - **Promoters:** Score 9–10; **Passives:** 7–8; **Detractors:** 0–6. - **CRM in Industrial Engineering** is applied to **after-sales service, B2B pipeline, warranty management, spare parts forecasting**, and **TPM integration**. - **Six-Phase CRM Lifecycle:** Vision → Customer Analysis → Process Redesign → Technology Selection → Pilot → Rollout. - **Customer Journey Stages:** Awareness → Consideration → Purchase → Onboarding → Retention → Advocacy. - **CRM integrates with ERP, MES, and SCM** to form an end-to-end industrial customer value chain. - **Always convert revenue to profit** by multiplying with gross margin ($m$) before applying CLV for profitability analysis. - **Always state units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners mark strictly on dimensional accuracy.

topics.title: Quality, Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Assurance, and Quality Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET601 - INDUSTRIAL AND SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quality, Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Assurance, and Quality Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Definition of Quality

The term **Quality** has been defined by various quality gurus, each providing a unique perspective that has shaped modern management philosophy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (ISO 9000:2015)**
> **Quality** is the *degree to which a set of inherent characteristics of an object fulfills requirements*. An "object" can be a product, service, process, person, organization, system, or resource.

### Classical Definitions by Quality Gurus

| Guru / Authority | Definition of Quality |
|---|---|
| **Joseph M. Juran** | **Quality is fitness for use** — the product must satisfy customer needs. |
| **W. Edwards Deming** | **Quality is a predictable degree of uniformity and dependability** at low cost and suited to the market. |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | **Quality is conformance to requirements**, not elegance. |
| **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | Quality is a **total field** — a customer-determine matter that reflects total composite of product and service characteristics. |
| **Genichi Taguchi** | **Quality is the loss a product causes to society** after it is shipped. |
| **ISO 8402 (Legacy)** | The totality of characteristics of an entity that bear on its ability to satisfy stated and implied needs. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** KTU frequently asks for definitions of Quality from Juran, Deming, and Crosby. Memorize the *exact keywords* in bold above, as Board examiners award marks specifically for these terms.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Restaurant Experience

Imagine visiting a **restaurant** for dinner:

- You want **good food** (performance, taste, reliability) → **Product Quality**
- You expect **polite service and clean ambience** (aesthetics, responsiveness) → **Service Quality**
- The restaurant **plans** its menu, ingredients, chef training in advance → **Quality Planning**
- The head chef **tastes and inspects** each dish before serving → **Quality Control**
- The owner maintains an **FSSAI license, hygiene SOPs, and audit records** → **Quality Assurance**
- The owner integrates menu design, training, customer feedback, supplier selection into a single philosophy → **Total Quality Management (TQM)**

In this analogy, every visit is a "transaction," and the customer's satisfaction determines whether the quality is acceptable. Likewise, in manufacturing, every product shipped is judged against an expected standard.

---

## 1.3 Dimensions of Quality (David Garvin's Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Garvin's 8 Dimensions of Quality** — Frequently tested as a 7-mark descriptive question.

1. **Performance** — Primary operating characteristics (e.g., speed of a car).
2. **Features** — Secondary characteristics that add value (e.g., power steering).
3. **Reliability** — Probability of failure-free operation over time.
4. **Conformance** — Degree to which a product meets pre-established standards.
5. **Durability** — Length of useful life before replacement.
6. **Serviceability** — Speed, courtesy, and competence of repair.
7. **Aesthetics** — Sensory appeal (look, feel, sound).
8. **Perceived Quality** — Subjective image of quality (brand reputation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 8 Dimensions Matter:** These dimensions allow students and managers to translate the abstract concept of "quality" into **measurable attributes**, enabling objective specification, design, and inspection.

---

## 1.4 Standard Quality Metrics (Industry Standards)

- **Defects per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** — Used in Six Sigma.
- **Parts per Million (PPM) defect rate** — Benchmarking metric.
- **First Pass Yield (FPY)** — Percentage of units passing without rework.
- **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** — Probability of a multi-step process yielding defect-free output.
- **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** — Total cost of prevention, appraisal, and failure.

The most rigorous industry benchmark is **Six Sigma = 3.4 DPMO**, meaning 99.99966% of opportunities are defect-free — a near-perfect process capability.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Quality Planning (Juran's Trilogy — Planning Component)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Planning** is the structured process of designing products and processes that are capable of meeting customer needs *before* production begins.

### Steps of Quality Planning (Juran's Universal Sequence)

1. **Identify the customers** (internal and external).
2. **Determine customer needs** — using market research, voice of customer (VOC).
3. **Translate needs into product specifications** (technical requirements).
4. **Develop product features** that meet the specifications.
5. **Develop process capabilities** — choose machinery, methods, workforce.
6. **Establish quality controls** — measurement systems and feedback loops.

### Why Quality Planning Matters
- It **prevents defects** rather than detecting them — a *proactive* approach.
- Reduces the **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** by eliminating root causes at the design stage.
- Forms the first pillar of **Juran's Trilogy**: *Plan → Control → Improve*.

---

## 2.2 Quality Control (QC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Control (QC)** is the operational set of techniques and activities used to *fulfill* quality requirements. It is **reactive** — focused on inspection, measurement, and correction.

### Key Characteristics of QC
- **Operational** — performed during production.
- **Inspection-based** — uses gauges, sampling, statistical tools.
- **Corrective** — defects found are reworked or scrapped.
- **Departmental** — typically handled by the Quality Department.

### Common QC Tools (The Seven QC Tools — Ishikawa / Karou Ishikawa)
1. **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** (Fishbone)
2. **Check Sheet**
3. **Control Chart**
4. **Histogram**
5. **Pareto Chart**
6. **Scatter Diagram**
7. **Flowchart / Stratification**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** "List and explain the Seven QC Tools." Memorize all seven with examples.

---

## 2.3 Quality Assurance (QA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Assurance (QA)** is a *planned and systematic* set of activities implemented within the **Quality Management System** that can be demonstrated to provide confidence that a product or service will satisfy quality requirements.

### Distinguishing Features of QA
- **System-wide** — covers the entire organization, not just production.
- **Proactive and Preventative** — focuses on the *process* so that defects don't occur.
- **Documented** — relies on SOPs, manuals, ISO documentation.
- **Audit-driven** — uses internal and external audits.
- **Strategic** — managed by top leadership, not just operators.

### QA Activities
- Development of **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)**.
- **Process validation and qualification**.
- **Internal quality audits**.
- **Calibration of measurement instruments**.
- **Training and certification** of personnel.
- **Supplier qualification programs**.

---

## 2.4 Quality Management (QM) and Total Quality Management (TQM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Management (QM)** encompasses all coordinated activities to direct and control an organization with regard to quality — including planning, assurance, control, and improvement.

**Total Quality Management (TQM)** is the *organization-wide* philosophy of continuously improving quality by involving **all members** (from CEO to shop-floor worker) at **all levels** of the organization.

### Core Principles of TQM (Aligned with ISO 9001 and TQM Frameworks)
1. **Customer Focus** — primary driver.
2. **Leadership** — establish unity of purpose.
3. **People Involvement** — every employee is responsible for quality.
4. **Process Approach** — manage activities as processes.
5. **System Approach to Management** — interrelated processes form a system.
6. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — never-ending pursuit of excellence.
7. **Factual Approach to Decision Making** — data-driven analysis.
8. **Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relationships**.

---

## 2.5 Deming's 14 Points (Excerpt — High-Yield for KTU)

1. **Create constancy of purpose** for improvement of product and service.
2. **Adopt the new philosophy** — we are in a new economic age.
3. **Cease dependence on inspection** to achieve quality — build quality *in*.
4. **End the practice of awarding business on price alone**.
5. **Improve constantly** every process for planning, production, and service.
6. **Institute training** on the job.
7. **Institute leadership** — supervise for quality, not just numbers.
8. **Drive out fear** so everyone may work effectively.
9. **Break down barriers** between departments.
10. **Eliminate slogans and exhortations** for the workforce.
11. **Eliminate numerical quotas** for the workforce.
12. **Remove barriers** to pride of workmanship.
13. **Institute a vigorous programme of education and self-improvement**.
14. **Put everybody to work** to accomplish the transformation.

> [!NOTE]
> Point 3 — *Cease dependence on inspection* — is Deming's foundational shift from QC to QA. It marks the philosophical pivot from detection to prevention.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Standard | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| DPMO | $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$ | Defects $\times$ 1 million / (Units $\times$ Opportunities per unit) | defects per million |
| Six Sigma Level | $\sigma = \dfrac{Z_{L} + 1.5}{DPMO \text{ equiv.}}$ | Yield $\approx$ **99.99966%** with 3.4 DPMO | dimensionless |
| First Pass Yield (FPY) | $FPY = \dfrac{\text{Units passed first time}}{\text{Units entered}}$ | Percentage passing without rework | percent |
| Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} FPY_{i}$ | Product of FPYs across $n$ process steps | percent |
| Process Capability ($C_{p}$) | $C_{p} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Capability when mean is centered | dimensionless |
| Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$) | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Accounts for off-center process | dimensionless |
| Cost of Quality (CoQ) | $CoQ = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure | currency |
| Pareto Principle | $80\% \text{ defects} \Rightarrow 20\% \text{ causes}$ | Vital few vs. trivial many | ratio |
| Defect Rate | $p = \dfrac{\text{No. of defective units}}{\text{Total units inspected}}$ | Sample proportion | fraction |
| Z-Value (Standard Normal) | $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Distance of $X$ from mean in $\sigma$ units | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vertical Pipe Rule:** All absolute-value notations have been replaced with $\min(\cdot)$ notation to comply with markdown table integrity. Never write $\vert x \vert$ inside a table cell.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry (Toyota):** TQM + Six Sigma + Kaizen — defect rate per vehicle cut to single digits.
- **Aerospace (Boeing, Airbus):** AS9100 QA standard derived from ISO 9001 — *zero tolerance for non-conformance*.
- **Software Engineering:** CMMI Level 5 maps directly to TQM principles.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** GMP (Good Manufacturing Practice) is a regulatory QA system that ensures every tablet meets identity, strength, purity, and quality standards.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Cost of Quality (CoQ) Decomposition

The **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** is the total cost incurred to ensure product conformance plus the cost incurred due to non-conformance. It has four canonical components, often called the **PAF Model**.

$$
CoQ = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}
$$

Where:

- $C_{P}$ = Prevention Cost
- $C_{A}$ = Appraisal Cost
- $C_{IF}$ = Internal Failure Cost
- $C_{EF}$ = External Failure Cost

### Step-by-Step Identification of Each Component

**Step 1 — Prevention Cost ($C_{P}$):**
Costs incurred to *prevent* defects from occurring.

- Quality planning and inspection planning.
- New product review.
- Supplier capability surveys.
- Training of personnel in quality.

$$
C_{P} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} P_{j}
$$

where $P_{j}$ is the $j$-th prevention activity cost across $m$ activities.

**Step 2 — Appraisal Cost ($C_{A}$):**
Costs incurred to *detect* defects through inspection and testing.

- Incoming material inspection.
- In-process and final inspection.
- Product testing.
- Calibration of instruments.

$$
C_{A} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} A_{k}
$$

**Step 3 — Internal Failure Cost ($C_{IF}$):**
Costs incurred when defects are found *before* shipment to the customer.

- Scrap and rework.
- Re-inspection and re-testing.
- Failure mode analysis.
- Design changes due to defects.

$$
C_{IF} = \sum_{l=1}^{p} IF_{l}
$$

**Step 4 — External Failure Cost ($C_{EF}$):**
Costs incurred when defects are discovered *after* the product reaches the customer.

- Warranty claims.
- Returns and replacements.
- Field service costs.
- Loss of goodwill and reputation.

$$
C_{EF} = \sum_{q=1}^{r} EF_{q}
$$

**Step 5 — Aggregation (Total CoQ):**

$$
\boxed{\;CoQ_{total} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} P_{j} + \sum_{k=1}^{n} A_{k} + \sum_{l=1}^{p} IF_{l} + \sum_{q=1}^{r} EF_{q}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** As prevention and appraisal spending **increases**, internal and external failure costs typically **decrease** at a faster rate, resulting in a *net reduction* in CoQ. This is the economic basis of QA over QC.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Six Sigma DPMO Computation

A manufacturing process produced **$U = 10{,}000$ units** over a production run. Each unit has **$O = 4$** opportunities for defects. A total of **$D = 120$ defects** were counted.

**Step 1 — Identify the given values.**

$$
U = 10{,}000 \quad;\quad O = 4 \quad;\quad D = 120
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total opportunity count.**

$$
\text{Total Opportunities} = U \times O
$$

Substitute:

$$
\text{Total Opportunities} = 10{,}000 \times 4 = 40{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the DPMO formula.**

$$
DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}
$$

Substitute:

$$
DPMO = \dfrac{120 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{10{,}000 \times 4}
$$

$$
DPMO = \dfrac{120{,}000{,}000}{40{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;DPMO = 3{,}000\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**

A DPMO of 3,000 corresponds to a yield of:

$$
Y = 1 - \dfrac{DPMO}{10^{6}} = 1 - \dfrac{3{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.997 = 99.70\%
$$

**Step 5 — Determine Sigma Level (using standard table).**

3,000 DPMO lies between 2,400 and 3,350, corresponding approximately to **$4.4\sigma$** process capability (without the 1.5σ shift) and **~4.0σ with shift**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$) Derivation

A shaft diameter specification is **$25.0 \pm 0.05$ mm**. From a sample of 50 measurements, the process mean is $\bar{X} = 25.03$ mm and standard deviation is $\sigma = 0.015$ mm.

**Step 1 — Identify specification limits.**

$$
USL = 25.05 \text{ mm} \quad;\quad LSL = 24.95 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute upper capability.**

$$
C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{25.05 - 25.03}{3 \times 0.015}
$$

$$
C_{pu} = \dfrac{0.02}{0.045} = 0.444
$$

**Step 3 — Compute lower capability.**

$$
C_{pl} = \dfrac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{25.03 - 24.95}{3 \times 0.015}
$$

$$
C_{pl} = \dfrac{0.08}{0.045} = 1.778
$$

**Step 4 — Take the minimum.**

$$
C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl}) = \min(0.444, 1.778)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;C_{pk} = 0.444\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret.**

A $C_{pk} < 1.0$ indicates that the process is **incapable** of meeting specifications consistently. The process mean is shifted toward the USL. Adjustment is required to re-center the mean at the target value (25.00 mm).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Benchmark:** $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ is generally acceptable; $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ is preferred for critical automotive/aerospace components.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: QC vs QA vs QM (High-Yield Comparison Table)

| Parameter | Quality Control (QC) | Quality Assurance (QA) | Quality Management (QM / TQM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Product inspection | Process control | Entire organization |
| **Approach** | Reactive — detect defects | Proactive — prevent defects | Strategic — continuous improvement |
| **Time Orientation** | Post-production | During process design | Throughout value chain |
| **Scope** | Departmental (Quality Dept.) | Cross-functional | Organization-wide |
| **Tools** | 7 QC tools, control charts | SOPs, audits, ISO standards | TQM, Six Sigma, Kaizen, BPR |
| **Responsibility** | Quality inspectors & engineers | All employees in process | Top management & all employees |
| **Philosophy** | "Inspect quality in" | "Build quality in" | "Quality is everyone's responsibility" |
| **Documentation** | Inspection reports | Quality manuals, procedures | Quality policy, strategic plan |
| **Cost Implication** | Higher (failure costs dominate) | Lower (prevention cheaper) | Optimized total CoQ |
| **Standards** | ISO 9001 clauses 8, 9 | ISO 9001 clauses 7, 8, 9 | ISO 9001:2015 entire framework |
| **Originator** | Taylor (Scientific Mgmt era) | Deming, Juran (1950s) | Feigenbaum (1961) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always conclude a comparison with a one-liner: *"QC detects, QA prevents, QM leads."*

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Pareto Analysis for Defect Reduction

A quality team recorded defects in **5 categories** during a week:

| Defect Type | Frequency |
|---|---|
| Surface Scratch | 42 |
| Dimensional Error | 18 |
| Color Mismatch | 12 |
| Loose Fastener | 6 |
| Packaging Damage | 4 |
| **Total** | **82** |

**Step 1 — Sort in descending order (already done above).**

**Step 2 — Compute cumulative frequency.**

- Surface Scratch: $42 \Rightarrow 51.2\%$
- + Dimensional Error: $60 \Rightarrow 73.2\%$
- + Color Mismatch: $72 \Rightarrow 87.8\%$
- + Loose Fastener: $78 \Rightarrow 95.1\%$
- + Packaging Damage: $82 \Rightarrow 100\%$

**Step 3 — Identify the "Vital Few."**

The first **two categories** account for **73.2%** of all defects. By the **80/20 Pareto principle**, the team should first focus improvement efforts on **Surface Scratches** and **Dimensional Errors**.

$$
\text{Pareto Contribution} = \dfrac{60}{82} \times 100 = 73.17\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** This is the practical application of Vilfredo Pareto's principle in quality management — the **"Vital Few" vs. "Trivial Many."**

---
