topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET601 - INDUSTRIAL AND SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Statistical Quality Control: Process Capability, Causes of Variation in Quality, Control Charts for X̄ and R, and Acceptance Sampling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 4 — Statistical Quality Control (SQC)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Statistical Quality Control (SQC)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** is the application of statistical techniques to measure, monitor, analyse, and control the quality of a manufacturing or service process. It provides an objective, data-driven framework to distinguish between *natural random variation* and *unnatural systematic variation* in a process.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus, SQC is divided into three major branches:

| Branch | Purpose | Key Tool |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Control** | Monitor an ongoing process | Control Charts (X̄ and R) |
| **Process Capability** | Quantify inherent ability of a stable process | Cp, Cpk, Pp, Ppk |
| **Acceptance Sampling** | Inspect a finished lot and decide accept/reject | OC Curve, Sampling Plans |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Heartbeat of a Factory"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a factory as a human body and SQC as the medical ECG machine.
> - The **process** is the heart. It beats (varies) naturally.
> - **Common cause variation** is the normal resting heartbeat — slight fluctuations, but healthy.
> - **Assignable cause variation** is a cardiac arrhythmia — sudden, abnormal spike. A control chart is the ECG monitor, alerting the doctor (engineer) the moment the heartbeat goes abnormal.
> - **Process capability** is the cardiologist's report: "Is this heart strong enough to handle a marathon (specifications)?"
> - **Acceptance sampling** is a blood test on a sample drop — we don't drain the whole patient, we infer health from a small sample.

### 1.3 Key Quality Vocabulary (KTU 2024 Terminology)

- **Quality:** The totality of characteristics of an entity that bear on its ability to satisfy stated or implied needs (ISO 8402).
- **Variable:** A measurable quality characteristic (length, weight, diameter) → used in **X̄ and R charts**.
- **Attribute:** A quality characteristic that is either conforming or non-conforming (go/no-go) → used in **p, np, c, u charts**.
- **Specification Limits (USL / LSL):** The boundaries set by the customer or design engineer.
- **Natural Tolerance Limits:** The process's own spread, typically **±3σ** from the mean.

### 1.4 The Two Faces of Variation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Walter Shewhart's Law of Variation (1924):** *No two things in nature are exactly alike.* Every process has variation. The art of SQC is knowing **when to leave the process alone** and **when to intervene**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Causes of Variation in Quality

All variation in any manufacturing or service process arises from one of two broad categories:

### 2.1.1 Common (Chance / Random) Causes
- **Definition:** Inherent, natural, ever-present variation due to the cumulative effect of many small, unavoidable factors.
- **Nature:** Statistical, predictable, inherent to the process design.
- **Examples:** Slight tool wear, ambient temperature fluctuations, minor variations in raw material hardness, operator fatigue micro-effects.
- **Distribution:** Typically **Normal (Gaussian)** with mean μ and standard deviation σ.
- **Action when present only:** **DO NOT INTERVENE.** The process is "in statistical control."

### 2.1.2 Assignable (Special / Systematic) Causes
- **Definition:** External, identifiable, non-random sources of variation that can be traced to a specific cause.
- **Nature:** Non-random, unpredictable, large magnitude shifts.
- **Examples:** Broken machine tool, untrained operator, defective raw material batch, change in process setting.
- **Action when present:** **INVESTIGATE AND ELIMINATE.** The process is "out of statistical control."

> [!NOTE]
> **The Deming Red Bead Experiment (KTU Favourite!):** Deming demonstrated that 94% of variation in worker output was due to the *system* (common causes), not the *worker* (assignable causes). Hence, blaming operators is a fallacy.

### 2.1.3 Type I and Type II Errors in SQC

| Error | Statistical Term | Quality Meaning | Probability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I Error** | α (significance level) | Concluding the process is **out of control** when it is actually **in control** | **Producer's Risk** |
| **Type II Error** | β | Concluding the process is **in control** when it is actually **out of control** | **Consumer's Risk** |

- For control charts, **3σ limits** are chosen such that **α = 0.0027 (0.27%)** — i.e., only 27 out of 10,000 points will fall outside by chance.

## 2.2 Process Capability

### 2.2.1 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Capability** is the measurable, inherent ability of a *stable* (in-control) process to produce output within specification limits. It is measured **after** statistical control is established, **never before**.

### 2.2.2 Key Indices

**A) Cp — Process Capability Index (Potential Capability)**

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
$$

Where σ is the standard deviation of the process. Cp measures the *spread* of the process relative to the specification width. A higher Cp means a more capable process.

**B) Cpk — Process Capability Index (Actual/Centred Capability)**

$$
C_{pk} = C_{pu} = \min\left( \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right)
$$

Cpk accounts for **process centring**. It is **always ≤ Cp**.

**C) Interpretation of Capability Indices**

| Cp / Cpk Value | Process Capability Rating |
|---|---|
| **< 1.00** | Process is **not capable** (6σ > Specification Width) |
| **1.00 – 1.33** | **Marginal / Poorly capable** |
| **1.33 – 1.67** | **Adequate** |
| **> 1.67** | **Excellent / Highly capable** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Six-Sigma Quality Benchmark:** In Six-Sigma, the target is **Cpk = 2.0**, which corresponds to **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**.

### 2.2.3 Steps to Calculate Process Capability
1. Collect at least **25 to 30 subgroups** of size n.
2. Verify process is **in statistical control** (no points outside control limits, no run rules violated).
3. Calculate the **overall standard deviation** (σ̂).
4. Compute **Cp** and **Cpk**.
5. Plot a **histogram** and overlay specification limits.

## 2.3 Control Charts for X̄ and R

### 2.3.1 The Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Control Chart** is a time-sequenced plot of a sample statistic with a **centre line (CL)**, an **upper control limit (UCL)**, and a **lower control limit (LCL)**. It is the *shewhart control chart* — the most powerful diagnostic tool in SQC.

The **X̄ chart** monitors the **process mean**; the **R chart** monitors the **process variability**. **Both must be in control** before the process can be declared stable.

### 2.3.2 The X̄ Chart (Mean Chart)

The X̄ chart uses the **average of each subgroup** as the plotted point. If the subgroup size is n, then:

$$
UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}
$$
$$
CL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}}
$$
$$
LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}
$$

Where:
- **X̄̄ (X-double-bar)** = grand average of all subgroup means.
- **R̄ (R-bar)** = average of all subgroup ranges.
- **A₂** = control chart constant depending on n.

### 2.3.3 The R Chart (Range Chart)

The R chart uses the **range of each subgroup** (max − min) as the plotted point:

$$
UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}
$$
$$
CL_R = \bar{R}
$$
$$
LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R}
$$

Where **D₃** and **D₄** are constants depending on n.

### 2.3.4 Control Chart Constants (Memorise for KTU Exams!)

| Subgroup Size (n) | **A₂** | **D₃** | **D₄** | **d₂** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **2** | 1.880 | 0 | 3.267 | 1.128 |
| **3** | 1.023 | 0 | 2.574 | 1.693 |
| **4** | 0.729 | 0 | 2.282 | 2.059 |
| **5** | 0.577 | 0 | 2.114 | 2.326 |
| **6** | 0.483 | 0 | 2.004 | 2.534 |
| **7** | 0.419 | 0.076 | 1.924 | 2.704 |
| **8** | 0.373 | 0.136 | 1.864 | 2.847 |
| **9** | 0.337 | 0.184 | 1.816 | 2.970 |
| **10** | 0.308 | 0.223 | 1.777 | 3.078 |

### 2.3.5 Step-by-Step Procedure to Construct X̄ and R Charts

1. **Collect Data:** Take k = 20 to 25 subgroups of size n (typically n = 4 or 5).
2. **Compute** X̄ᵢ and Rᵢ for each subgroup.
3. **Compute** grand average X̄̄ and average range R̄.
4. **Compute UCL, CL, LCL** for both X̄ and R charts using the formulas above.
5. **Plot the points** sequentially against subgroup number.
6. **Interpret:** All points within limits → process in control. Any point outside → out of control → investigate assignable cause.

> [!NOTE]
> **Order of construction matters:** Always construct the **R chart FIRST.** If the variability is out of control, the X̄ chart limits become meaningless.

## 2.4 Acceptance Sampling

### 2.4.1 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceptance Sampling** is a statistical quality control procedure where a **random sample** is drawn from a **lot** (batch) and inspected to decide whether to **accept** or **reject** the entire lot. It does **not** improve quality — it merely **verifies** it.

### 2.4.2 Why Use Acceptance Sampling?
- **Destructive testing** (e.g., light bulb life, bullet proofing) — cannot test 100%.
- **Cost of 100% inspection** is prohibitive.
- **Fatigue / boredom** in 100% inspection leads to errors.
- **Procurement** situations where the producer and consumer are different entities.

### 2.4.3 Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve

> [!NOTE]
> The **OC curve** plots the **probability of acceptance (Pₐ)** of a lot against the **incoming quality (fraction defective p)**. It is the **signature** of any sampling plan.

Key points on the OC curve:
- **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level) — p₁:** The worst tolerable quality with high probability of acceptance. Producer's Risk α corresponds to 1 − Pₐ at AQL.
- **LTPD / RQL (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective) — p₂:** The worst quality that should be rejected with high probability. Consumer's Risk β corresponds to Pₐ at LTPD.
- **p₀.₅ (Indifference Quality):** Quality with 50% probability of acceptance.
- **IQL (Indifference Quality Level):** Same as p₀.₅.

### 2.4.4 Types of Sampling Plans

| Plan Type | Number of Samples | Decision Rule |
|---|---|---|
| **Single Sampling** | One | Take sample of size n. Accept if d ≤ c; else reject. |
| **Double Sampling** | Up to two | First sample n₁; accept if d₁ ≤ c₁, reject if d₁ > c₂, else take second sample n₂ and decide. |
| **Multiple Sampling** | Up to seven | Sequential decisions across multiple stages. |

### 2.4.5 Single Sampling Plan Formulas

For a single sampling plan with sample size **n** and acceptance number **c**:

$$
P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^{d} (1-p)^{n-d}
$$

This is the **cumulative Binomial distribution** (for large N, p small, n/N ≤ 0.1, approximated by Poisson).

### 2.4.6 Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) and Average Total Inspection (ATI)

$$
AOQ = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N}
$$

(Where the accepted lot's defectives are replaced by good ones — Rectifying Inspection.)

$$
ATI = n + (1 - P_a)(N - n)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **AOQL (Average Outgoing Quality Limit)** is the **maximum** point of the AOQ curve and represents the *worst* average quality that will reach the customer after rectifying inspection.

### 2.4.7 Producer's and Consumer's Risk

- **Producer's Risk (α):** Probability of rejecting a *good* lot (p = p₁ = AQL). From the customer's perspective on the OC curve, Pₐ at AQL = 1 − α.
- **Consumer's Risk (β):** Probability of accepting a *bad* lot (p = p₂ = LTPD). Pₐ at LTPD = β.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Process Capability (potential) | $C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Assumes process centred |
| 2 | Process Capability (actual) | $C_{pk} = \min\left( \frac{USL-\mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu-LSL}{3\sigma} \right)$ | $C_{pk} \le C_p$ |
| 3 | X̄ Chart UCL | $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | Centre line: X̄̄ |
| 4 | X̄ Chart LCL | $LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | For n ≤ 6, LCL often ≥ 0 |
| 5 | R Chart UCL | $UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}$ | Use D₄ from table |
| 6 | R Chart LCL | $LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R}$ | For n ≤ 6, D₃ = 0 |
| 7 | Estimate of σ | $\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ | d₂ from constants table |
| 8 | Probability of Acceptance | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$ | Binomial (single sampling) |
| 9 | Average Outgoing Quality | $AOQ = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N}$ | Rectifying inspection |
| 10 | Average Total Inspection | $ATI = n + (1-P_a)(N-n)$ | For double sampling, add n₂ term |
| 11 | Producer's Risk | $\alpha = 1 - P_a \text{ at } p = AQL$ | On OC curve |
| 12 | Consumer's Risk | $\beta = P_a \text{ at } p = LTPD$ | On OC curve |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Control Chart Limits (X̄ Chart)

We derive the 3σ control limits for the X̄ chart from first principles.

**Step 1: Distribution of Subgroup Means**

If the individual measurements X follow a normal distribution $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then the sample mean $\bar{X}$ for a subgroup of size n follows:

$$
\bar{X} \sim N\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)
$$

This is a fundamental result from the Central Limit Theorem and the property of normal distributions.

**Step 2: Standard Error of the Mean**

The standard deviation of $\bar{X}$ (called the standard error) is:

$$
\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

**Step 3: Construct the 3σ Limits (Shewhart's Choice)**

We place the control limits at **3 standard errors** from the centre line:

$$
UCL_{\bar{X}} = \mu + 3 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$
$$
CL_{\bar{X}} = \mu
$$
$$
LCL_{\bar{X}} = \mu - 3 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

**Step 4: Replace μ and σ with Sample Estimates**

In practice, the true process parameters are unknown. We estimate:
- $\mu$ with $\bar{\bar{X}}$ (grand mean of all subgroup means).
- $\sigma$ using $\bar{R}/d_2$ (where $d_2 = E[R]/\sigma$ is a constant from the table).

Substituting:

$$
UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + \frac{3\bar{R}}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}
$$

**Step 5: Define the Constant A₂**

We define the constant:

$$
A_2 = \frac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}
$$

This gives the final working formula:

$$
UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}
$$
$$
LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}
$$

**Step 6: Why A₂ Varies with n (Numerical Check)**

For n = 5, $d_2 = 2.326$:

$$
A_2 = \frac{3}{2.326 \cdot \sqrt{5}} = \frac{3}{2.326 \cdot 2.236} = \frac{3}{5.201} = 0.577
$$

This matches the value in the constants table. ✓

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example: Control Charts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Problem Type:** Given 10 subgroups of size 5, calculate control limits and determine if the process is in control.

**Data Given (Measurements in mm):**

| Subgroup (i) | X₁ | X₂ | X₃ | X₄ | X₅ | X̄ᵢ | Rᵢ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 10 | 12 | 11 | 13 | 14 | 12.0 | 4 |
| 2 | 11 | 12 | 13 | 12 | 12 | 12.0 | 2 |
| 3 | 12 | 14 | 13 | 15 | 11 | 13.0 | 4 |
| 4 | 13 | 12 | 14 | 11 | 15 | 13.0 | 4 |
| 5 | 11 | 10 | 12 | 13 | 14 | 12.0 | 4 |
| 6 | 10 | 11 | 12 | 13 | 14 | 12.0 | 4 |
| 7 | 14 | 15 | 13 | 12 | 11 | 13.0 | 4 |
| 8 | 12 | 13 | 14 | 11 | 10 | 12.0 | 4 |
| 9 | 11 | 12 | 13 | 14 | 15 | 13.0 | 4 |
| 10 | 13 | 14 | 12 | 11 | 10 | 12.0 | 4 |

**Step 1: Calculate Grand Average (X̄̄)**

$$
\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{12+12+13+13+12+12+13+12+13+12}{10} = \frac{124}{10} = 12.4 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Average Range (R̄)**

$$
\bar{R} = \frac{4+2+4+4+4+4+4+4+4+4}{10} = \frac{38}{10} = 3.8 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3: For n = 5, Look up the Constants**

From the constants table (n = 5): $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$.

**Step 4: Compute X̄ Chart Limits**

$$
UCL_{\bar{X}} = 12.4 + (0.577)(3.8) = 12.4 + 2.193 = 14.59 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
CL_{\bar{X}} = 12.4 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
LCL_{\bar{X}} = 12.4 - (0.577)(3.8) = 12.4 - 2.193 = 10.21 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5: Compute R Chart Limits**

$$
UCL_R = (2.114)(3.8) = 8.03 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
CL_R = 3.8 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
LCL_R = (0)(3.8) = 0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6: Check for Out-of-Control Points**

- **R chart:** All $R_i$ values are between 2 and 4. UCL = 8.03, LCL = 0. **All within limits ✓**
- **X̄ chart:** All $\bar{X}_i$ values are between 12.0 and 13.0. UCL = 14.59, LCL = 10.21. **All within limits ✓**

**Conclusion:** The process is **in statistical control.** We can now proceed to compute process capability.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example: Process Capability

**Given:** From the previous example, the process is in control. Estimate σ and compute Cp and Cpk.

**Specification Limits (from design):** USL = 16 mm, LSL = 10 mm.

**Step 1: Estimate σ Using R̄/d₂**

For n = 5, $d_2 = 2.326$:

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{3.8}{2.326} = 1.634 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Cp (Potential Capability)**

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{16 - 10}{6 \times 1.634} = \frac{6}{9.804} = 0.612
$$

**Step 3: Compute Cpk (Actual Capability)**

$$
C_{pu} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{16 - 12.4}{3 \times 1.634} = \frac{3.6}{4.902} = 0.734
$$
$$
C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{12.4 - 10}{3 \times 1.634} = \frac{2.4}{4.902} = 0.490
$$
$$
C_{pk} = \min(0.734,\ 0.490) = 0.490
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation**

- $C_p = 0.612 < 1.0$ → Process is **NOT capable** (process spread > specification width).
- $C_{pk} = 0.490 < C_p$ → Process is also **off-centre** (closer to LSL).
- **Recommendation:** Reduce process variation (improve equipment, training) **AND** shift the mean to the centre of the specification window.

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example: Acceptance Sampling (OC Curve)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Problem Type:** Given a single sampling plan (n, c), compute Pₐ at various p, and identify producer's/consumer's risk.

**Given:** Single sampling plan: **N = 2000, n = 50, c = 1.** Compute Pₐ for p = 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.10.

**Step 1: Use the Binomial Formula**

$$
P_a(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d} = (1-p)^n + n \cdot p \cdot (1-p)^{n-1}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate for p = 0.01**

$$
P_a(0.01) = (0.99)^{50} + (50)(0.01)(0.99)^{49}
$$
$$
(0.99)^{50} \approx 0.6050
$$
$$
(0.99)^{49} \approx 0.6113
$$
$$
P_a(0.01) = 0.6050 + 0.5 \cdot 0.6113 = 0.6050 + 0.3057 = 0.9107
$$

**Step 3: Calculate for p = 0.02**

$$
P_a(0.02) = (0.98)^{50} + (50)(0.02)(0.98)^{49}
$$
$$
(0.98)^{50} \approx 0.3642
$$
$$
(0.98)^{49} \approx 0.3715
$$
$$
P_a(0.02) = 0.3642 + 1.0 \cdot 0.3715 = 0.3642 + 0.3715 = 0.7357
$$

**Step 4: Calculate for p = 0.05**

$$
P_a(0.05) = (0.95)^{50} + (50)(0.05)(0.95)^{49}
$$
$$
(0.95)^{50} \approx 0.0769
$$
$$
(0.95)^{49} \approx 0.0810
$$
$$
P_a(0.05) = 0.0769 + 2.5 \cdot 0.0810 = 0.0769 + 0.2025 = 0.2794
$$

**Step 5: Calculate for p = 0.10**

$$
P_a(0.10) = (0.90)^{50} + (50)(0.10)(0.90)^{49}
$$
$$
(0.90)^{50} \approx 0.00515
$$
$$
(0.90)^{49} \approx 0.00572
$$
$$
P_a(0.10) = 0.00515 + 5.0 \cdot 0.00572 = 0.00515 + 0.0286 = 0.0338
$$

**Step 6: Construct the OC Curve Table**

| p (fraction defective) | Pₐ (probability of acceptance) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 0.00 | 1.0000 |
| 0.01 | 0.9107 |
| 0.02 | 0.7357 |
| 0.05 | 0.2794 |
| 0.10 | 0.0338 |
| 0.15 | 0.0031 |
| 0.20 | 0.0002 |

**Step 7: Interpret the OC Curve**

- If AQL = 0.01, then Producer's Risk α = 1 − 0.9107 = 0.0893 (8.93%).
- If LTPD = 0.10, then Consumer's Risk β = 0.0338 (3.38%).
- **A "good" OC curve** drops steeply from near 1 to near 0 over a narrow range of p.

## 3.5 Analytical Example: AOQ and ATI

**Given:** N = 1000, n = 50, c = 1, p = 0.04. Compute AOQ and ATI.

**Step 1: Find Pₐ at p = 0.04**

$$
P_a(0.04) = (0.96)^{50} + (50)(0.04)(0.96)^{49}
$$
$$
(0.96)^{50} \approx 0.1299
$$
$$
(0.96)^{49} \approx 0.1353
$$
$$
P_a(0.04) = 0.1299 + 2.0 \cdot 0.1353 = 0.1299 + 0.2706 = 0.4005
$$

**Step 2: Compute AOQ**

$$
AOQ = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N} = \frac{0.4005 \times 0.04 \times (1000 - 50)}{1000} = \frac{0.4005 \times 0.04 \times 950}{1000}
$$
$$
AOQ = \frac{15.219}{1000} = 0.01522 \text{ (or 1.52% defective)}
$$

**Step 3: Compute ATI**

$$
ATI = n + (1 - P_a)(N - n) = 50 + (1 - 0.4005)(1000 - 50)
$$
$$
ATI = 50 + (0.5995)(950) = 50 + 569.5 = 619.5 \text{ items inspected on average}
$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

### Question 1: Define Process Capability. State the formula for Cp and Cpk.

**Model Answer:**

**Process Capability:** It is the measurable, inherent ability of a *stable* (in-control) process to produce output that lies within the specification limits. It is computed **only after** the process has been verified to be in statistical control using control charts.

**Formulas:**

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left( \frac{USL-\mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right)
$$

- **Cp** measures the *potential* capability assuming the process is perfectly centred.
- **Cpk** measures the *actual* capability and accounts for off-centring. **Cpk ≤ Cp** always.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### Question 2: Distinguish between Common Causes and Assignable Causes of Variation. Give two examples of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Common Causes | Assignable Causes |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Inherent, natural, random | External, identifiable, systematic |
| **Magnitude** | Small, cumulative | Large, sudden |
| **Predictability** | Statistical, predictable | Non-random, unpredictable |
| **Action** | Leave the process alone | Investigate and eliminate |
| **Examples** | Slight tool wear; minor ambient temperature variation | Broken machine; defective raw material batch |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**A machine produces steel rods with specification limits USL = 25.4 mm and LSL = 24.6 mm. Ten samples of size 5 are taken. The grand mean X̄̄ = 25.05 mm and the average range R̄ = 0.30 mm. Assuming the process is in statistical control:**

**(a) Calculate the control limits for the X̄ chart and the R chart. Comment on whether the process is capable. Use the relevant control chart constants for n = 5. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Compute Cp and Cpk. If the process is not capable, what remedial action would you recommend? (7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a): Control Limits**

For n = 5, from the KTU control chart constants table:
- $A_2 = 0.577$
- $D_3 = 0$
- $D_4 = 2.114$
- $d_2 = 2.326$

**X̄ Chart Limits:**

$$
UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 25.05 + (0.577)(0.30) = 25.05 + 0.1731 = 25.223 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
CL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} = 25.05 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} = 25.05 - 0.1731 = 24.877 \text{ mm}
$$

**R Chart Limits:**

$$
UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R} = (2.114)(0.30) = 0.6342 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
CL_R = \bar{R} = 0.30 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R} = 0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Comment:** The limits define the expected spread of subgroup means and ranges. As long as plotted points lie within these limits, the process is in statistical control.

---

**Part (b): Process Capability**

**Step 1:** Estimate process standard deviation:

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.30}{2.326} = 0.1290 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute Cp:

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{25.4 - 24.6}{6 \times 0.1290} = \frac{0.8}{0.774} = 1.034
$$

**Step 3:** Compute Cpk:

$$
C_{pu} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{25.4 - 25.05}{3 \times 0.1290} = \frac{0.35}{0.387} = 0.905
$$
$$
C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{25.05 - 24.6}{3 \times 0.1290} = \frac{0.45}{0.387} = 1.163
$$
$$
C_{pk} = \min(0.905,\ 1.163) = 0.905
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation and Recommendation**

- $C_p = 1.034$ → Marginal capability.
- $C_{pk} = 0.905 < C_p$ → Process is **off-centre** (closer to USL).
- Since $C_{pk} < 1$, the process is **NOT capable** — it will produce some out-of-spec rods.

**Remedial Actions:**
1. **Recentre the process:** Adjust the machine setting to shift the mean from 25.05 mm to 25.0 mm (the target midpoint of the specification).
2. **Reduce variation:** Improve machine precision, replace worn tools, use better fixtures, train operator.
3. **Investigate the cause of the upward drift** — likely assignable cause (e.g., tool wear, temperature rise).
4. After re-centring, re-evaluate capability to confirm $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**A company receives lots of N = 1500 items. A single sampling plan with n = 60 and c = 2 is used for inspection.**

**(a) Construct the OC curve by computing the probability of acceptance (Pₐ) for p = 0.01, 0.03, 0.05, and 0.10. Identify the AQL and LTPD if the producer's risk α = 0.10 and consumer's risk β = 0.10. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Compute the AOQ at p = 0.04 and the ATI. What is the AOQL? (7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a): OC Curve Construction**

The single sampling plan acceptance formula is:

$$
P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d} = (1-p)^n + np(1-p)^{n-1} + \frac{n(n-1)}{2}p^2(1-p)^{n-2}
$$

With n = 60, c = 2.

**At p = 0.01:**

$$
P_a = (0.99)^{60} + (60)(0.01)(0.99)^{59} + \frac{(60)(59)}{2}(0.01)^2(0.99)^{58}
$$
$$
(0.99)^{60} \approx 0.5472,\ (0.99)^{59} \approx 0.5528,\ (0.99)^{58} \approx 0.5584
$$
$$
P_a = 0.5472 + 0.6 \times 0.5528 + 1770 \times 0.0001 \times 0.5584
$$
$$
P_a = 0.5472 + 0.3317 + 0.0988 = 0.9777
$$

**At p = 0.03:**

$$
(0.97)^{60} \approx 0.1612,\ (0.97)^{59} \approx 0.1662,\ (0.97)^{58} \approx 0.1713
$$
$$
P_a = 0.1612 + 1.8 \times 0.1662 + 1770 \times 0.0009 \times 0.1713
$$
$$
P_a = 0.1612 + 0.2992 + 0.2729 = 0.7333
$$

**At p = 0.05:**

$$
(0.95)^{60} \approx 0.0461,\ (0.95)^{59} \approx 0.0485,\ (0.95)^{58} \approx 0.0511
$$
$$
P_a = 0.0461 + 3.0 \times 0.0485 + 1770 \times 0.0025 \times 0.0511
$$
$$
P_a = 0.0461 + 0.1455 + 0.2261 = 0.4177
$$

**At p = 0.10:**

$$
(0.90)^{60} \approx 0.00180,\ (0.90)^{59} \approx 0.00200,\ (0.90)^{58} \approx 0.00222
$$
$$
P_a = 0.00180 + 6.0 \times 0.00200 + 1770 \times 0.01 \times 0.00222
$$
$$
P_a = 0.00180 + 0.01200 + 0.03929 = 0.0531
$$

**OC Curve Summary Table:**

| p | Pₐ |
|:---:|:---:|
| 0.00 | 1.0000 |
| 0.01 | 0.9777 |
| 0.03 | 0.7333 |
| 0.05 | 0.4177 |
| 0.10 | 0.0531 |
| 0.15 | 0.0046 |
| 0.20 | 0.0003 |

**AQL and LTPD:**
- For **α = 0.10**, we need Pₐ = 0.90. From the table, this corresponds to approximately **p₁ = AQL ≈ 0.02** (interpolated between 0.01 and 0.03).
- For **β = 0.10**, we need Pₐ = 0.10. From the table, this corresponds to approximately **p₂ = LTPD ≈ 0.075** (interpolated between 0.05 and 0.10).

---

**Part (b): AOQ at p = 0.04 and ATI**

**Step 1: Find Pₐ at p = 0.04**

$$
(0.96)^{60} \approx 0.0864,\ (0.96)^{59} \approx 0.0900,\ (0.96)^{58} \approx 0.0937
$$
$$
P_a(0.04) = 0.0864 + 2.4 \times 0.0900 + 1770 \times 0.0016 \times 0.0937
$$
$$
P_a(0.04) = 0.0864 + 0.2160 + 0.2653 = 0.5677
$$

**Step 2: Compute AOQ**

$$
AOQ = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N} = \frac{0.5677 \times 0.04 \times (1500 - 60)}{1500}
$$
$$
AOQ = \frac{0.5677 \times 0.04 \times 1440}{1500} = \frac{32.700}{1500} = 0.0218 \text{ (or 2.18% defective)}
$$

**Step 3: Compute ATI**

$$
ATI = n + (1 - P_a)(N - n) = 60 + (1 - 0.5677)(1500 - 60)
$$
$$
ATI = 60 + 0.4323 \times 1440 = 60 + 622.5 = 682.5 \text{ items}
$$

**Step 4: AOQL**

The AOQL is the **maximum** of the AOQ function over all p. By plotting AOQ vs p, the peak occurs at approximately **p ≈ 0.025 to 0.030** with AOQL ≈ 0.023 (2.3% defective). The exact value requires the AOQ function to be evaluated at multiple p values, but the answer should be reported as the **peak** of the AOQ curve.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Reverse the order:** Always construct the **R chart first**, then the X̄ chart. Reverse order → full marks deducted.
> 2. **Misuse of σ vs σ̂:** Use $\bar{R}/d_2$ for σ̂ in capability calculations, not the sample standard deviation of all 50 measurements.
> 3. **Confusing Producer's and Consumer's Risk:** Producer's Risk = probability of rejecting a *good* lot (α = 1 − Pₐ at AQL). Consumer's Risk = probability of accepting a *bad* lot (β = Pₐ at LTPD).
> 4. **Forgetting the constant d₂:** The Cp formula needs σ, not R̄. Always use $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2$.
> 5. **Binomial arithmetic errors:** Be careful with large powers like (0.99)⁵⁰ — use logarithms or a calculator in the exam.
> 6. **Confusing Cp and Cpk:** Cpk ≤ Cp always. If you compute Cpk > Cp, you have made an error.
> 7. **Off-by-one in c:** Acceptance number c means accept for d = 0, 1, ..., c (i.e., c+1 terms in the summation).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 KTU 2024 Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 (SQC)**

### ✅ Causes of Variation
- **Common causes** = natural, leave alone, no investigation.
- **Assignable causes** = specific, investigate, remove.
- **Type I error (α)** = Producer's Risk; **Type II error (β)** = Consumer's Risk.

### ✅ Process Capability
- Compute **only AFTER** the process is in statistical control.
- $C_p = (USL - LSL)/(6\sigma)$ — potential (centred assumption).
- $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$ — actual (with centring).
- **Cpk ≤ Cp** always.
- Target for world-class: $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ (acceptable), $\geq 1.67$ (excellent), $= 2.0$ (Six-Sigma).

### ✅ Control Charts (X̄ and R)
- **Order of construction:** R chart first, then X̄ chart.
- Use control chart constants table: $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$, $d_2$ for given n.
- **X̄ chart:** Monitors process mean.
- **R chart:** Monitors process variability.
- **3σ limits** = 99.73% probability (α = 0.0027).
- If any point outside UCL/LCL → assignable cause present.

### ✅ Acceptance Sampling
- **Single sampling plan:** Sample n, accept if d ≤ c.
- $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$.
- **OC curve:** Pₐ vs p (steepness indicates discrimination power).
- **AQL (p₁):** Worst tolerable quality, $\alpha = 1 - P_a$ at AQL.
- **LTPD (p₂):** Worst intolerable quality, $\beta = P_a$ at LTPD.
- **AOQ** = average outgoing quality after rectifying inspection.
- **AOQL** = maximum point of AOQ curve.
- **ATI** = average total items inspected per lot.

### ✅ Engineering Utilities
- **Control charts** are used in **real-time process monitoring** on shop floors.
- **Process capability** drives **supplier selection** and **Six-Sigma projects**.
- **Acceptance sampling** is critical in **procurement**, **military standards (MIL-STD-105E)**, and **destructive testing** industries.
- All three tools together form the **backbone of ISO 9001:2015** quality management systems.

### ✅ Memorise This Table (MUST for KTU Exam)

| n | $A_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ | $d_2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 2 | 1.880 | 0 | 3.267 | 1.128 |
| 3 | 1.023 | 0 | 2.574 | 1.693 |
| 4 | 0.729 | 0 | 2.282 | 2.059 |
| **5** | **0.577** | **0** | **2.114** | **2.326** |
| 6 | 0.483 | 0 | 2.004 | 2.534 |
| 7 | 0.419 | 0.076 | 1.924 | 2.704 |

> **Final Note for KTU Students:** Questions from this module are most frequently asked for **14 marks** (full problems with sub-parts) and require **calculation skills + interpretation**. Always show your working, **state the constants used**, and **interpret the results in one or two sentences**. The valuation scheme explicitly awards marks for **interpretation comments** even when calculations are correct.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reliability Engineering: Causes of Failures, Bath Tub Curve, System Reliability, and Life Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET601 - INDUSTRIAL AND SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reliability Engineering: Causes of Failures, Bath Tub Curve, System Reliability, and Life Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Reliability Engineering — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Reliability** is the probability that a system, component, or product will perform its intended function under stated operating conditions (environment, load, temperature, stress) for a specified period of time.

Mathematically, Reliability is expressed as:

$$R(t) = P(T > t)$$

Where:
- $R(t)$ = Reliability at time $t$ (dimensionless, between 0 and 1)
- $T$ = Random variable representing the time-to-failure
- $t$ = Specified mission time

The complement of Reliability is the **Unreliability** or **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) of Failure**:

$$F(t) = 1 - R(t) = P(T \leq t)$$

The instantaneous **Failure Rate** (Hazard Rate) is:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

Where $f(t)$ is the probability density function (PDF) of failure.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Lifespan of a Human" Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Think of reliability like the **lifespan of a human being**. A newborn baby is highly reliable to "survive" the first few years (low early failure rate). A young adult is in their "prime" — failures (deaths) are rare and random. An elderly person experiences a sharply rising failure rate due to wear-out of organs. The **Bath Tub Curve** mirrors this exact life pattern!

Reliability is essentially a measure of **trustworthiness over time**. A product that lasts 10 years without failure under normal conditions is considered more reliable than one that fails in 6 months. In KTU Industrial Engineering, reliability directly impacts:
- **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures)
- **Availability** of manufacturing systems
- **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)**
- **Warranty cost** and customer satisfaction

### 1.3 Causes of Failures

> [!NOTE]
> **Failure** is defined as the termination of the ability of an item to perform its required function.

Failures in engineering systems are broadly classified into the following categories:

| S.No | Category of Failure | Description | Typical Examples |
|------|---------------------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Early Failures (Infant Mortality)** | Defects in design, material, or workmanship | Poor soldering, weak welds, substandard components |
| 2 | **Random (Chance) Failures** | Stochastic, unpredictable during useful life | Sudden electrical shorts, accidental overloads |
| 3 | **Wear-out Failures** | Gradual degradation due to aging, fatigue, corrosion | Bearing wear, fatigue cracks, insulation breakdown |
| 4 | **Human-Induced Failures** | Operator errors, improper maintenance | Incorrect assembly, missed lubrication |
| 5 | **Environmental Failures** | External stresses beyond design limits | Temperature extremes, humidity, vibration, dust |

The **three primary physical causes** of failure in mechanical/industrial systems are:

1. **Overload (Stress > Strength)** — When applied stress exceeds the material's strength.
2. **Wear** — Progressive loss of material due to relative motion.
3. **Fatigue** — Failure under repeated cyclic loading below the ultimate strength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A student must be able to *identify*, *classify*, and *map* failures to the appropriate phase of the Bath Tub Curve.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Bath Tub Curve — Detailed Analysis

The **Bath Tub Curve (BTC)** is the classical graphical model depicting the instantaneous failure rate $\lambda(t)$ as a function of time. Its name comes from its characteristic cross-sectional shape resembling a bathtub.

> [!NOTE]
> The Bath Tub Curve is divided into **three distinct regions** (sometimes a fourth is added). Each region has its own dominant failure mechanism, failure rate behavior, and engineering mitigation strategy.

#### Region I — Infant Mortality (Burn-In) Phase
- **Duration:** Early life of the product (0 to $t_1$).
- **Behavior:** $\lambda(t)$ is **high but decreasing**.
- **Dominant Failures:** Manufacturing defects, material flaws, assembly errors, weak components.
- **Engineering Action:** **Burn-in testing** — operate the product under stress to eliminate weak units before shipment. Components that survive this region are "weeding out" the defective ones.
- **Real-world analogy:** Newborn infants in their first year of life (high mortality rate that decreases over time).

#### Region II — Useful Life (Random Failures)
- **Duration:** Major portion of operational life ($t_1$ to $t_2$).
- **Behavior:** $\lambda(t)$ is **approximately constant** (low and steady).
- **Dominant Failures:** Random, chance, unpredictable events — accidental overload, environmental shocks, human error.
- **Engineering Action:** Preventive maintenance, redundancy design, robust quality control.
- **Real-world analogy:** Healthy adult between ages 20–50, deaths are random accidents or illnesses.

#### Region III — Wear-Out Phase
- **Duration:** End of operational life (after $t_2$).
- **Behavior:** $\lambda(t)$ is **increasing rapidly** (monotonically rising).
- **Dominant Failures:** Wear, fatigue, corrosion, aging, material degradation.
- **Engineering Action:** Replacement scheduling, overhaul, predictive maintenance, design retirement.
- **Real-world analogy:** Elderly person above 70 years, mortality rises sharply.

#### Mathematical Description of the Three Regions

The bath tub failure rate can be expressed as a piecewise model:

$$\lambda(t) = \begin{cases} \lambda_1(t) & \text{decreasing} \quad 0 \leq t \leq t_1 \quad \text{(infant mortality)} \\ \lambda_0 = \text{constant} & t_1 \leq t \leq t_2 \quad \text{(useful life)} \\ \lambda_3(t) & \text{increasing} \quad t \geq t_2 \quad \text{(wear-out)} \end{cases}$$

In the **useful life region**, the bath tub curve collapses into a simple **exponential reliability model** where $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ = constant.

### 2.2 Reliability Functions — Mathematical Relationships

The four fundamental reliability functions are inter-related:

| Function | Symbol | Definition | Formula |
|----------|--------|------------|---------|
| **Reliability** | $R(t)$ | Probability of survival up to time $t$ | $R(t) = P(T > t)$ |
| **Unreliability / CDF** | $F(t)$ | Probability of failure by time $t$ | $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ |
| **Probability Density Function (PDF)** | $f(t)$ | Rate of change of failure probability | $f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ |
| **Failure Rate / Hazard Rate** | $\lambda(t)$ | Conditional probability of failure in next instant | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Equation (Universal Reliability Identity):**
> $$R(t) = \exp\left[-\int_0^t \lambda(u)\, du\right]$$
> This equation is the foundation of all reliability modeling and is **highly emphasized** in KTU board examinations.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **most frequently tested formulas** for Module 4 of PCMET601. Memorize these — they appear in **nearly every KTU exam cycle**.

| S.No | Concept | Formula | Units | Boundary Condition |
|------|---------|---------|-------|-------------------|
| 1 | Reliability | $R(t) = P(T > t)$ | Dimensionless | $0 \leq R(t) \leq 1$ |
| 2 | Unreliability (CDF) | $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ | Dimensionless | $F(0)=0, F(\infty)=1$ |
| 3 | Failure Density (PDF) | $f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ | per unit time | $\int_0^\infty f(t)\,dt = 1$ |
| 4 | Failure Rate / Hazard | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | failures/unit time | Always $\lambda(t) \geq 0$ |
| 5 | Master Reliability Identity | $R(t) = \exp\left[-\displaystyle\int_0^t \lambda(u)\,du\right]$ | Dimensionless | Valid for ALL distributions |
| 6 | Series System | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Dimensionless | All units must survive |
| 7 | Parallel System | $R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | Dimensionless | At least one must survive |
| 8 | Mean Time To Failure | $MTTF = \displaystyle\int_0^\infty t\, f(t)\, dt = \int_0^\infty R(t)\, dt$ | Hours / Cycles | Expected life |
| 9 | For Constant $\lambda$ (Exponential) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Dimensionless | Memoryless property |
| 10 | MTBF (Exponential) | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Hours / Cycles | For repairable systems |
| 11 | Weibull Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\alpha)^\beta}$ | Dimensionless | $\alpha$ = scale, $\beta$ = shape |
| 12 | Weibull Failure Rate | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | failures/unit time | $\beta < 1$: infant mortality, $\beta = 1$: random, $\beta > 1$: wear-out |
| 13 | Characteristic Life | $\eta = \alpha = (1/\lambda)^{1/\beta}$ | Time units | 63.2% failure point |
| 14 | Gamma Function (for Weibull MTTF) | $MTTF = \alpha\,\Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$ | Time | $\Gamma(n) = (n-1)!$ for integer $n$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Bath Tub Curve dictates replacement schedules for aircraft engines. Engines are overhauled before wear-out region begins.
- **Electronics (Burn-in):** Semiconductor chips undergo 168-hour burn-in to weed out infant mortality failures.
- **Automotive:** Vehicle warranties are designed around the wear-out region to avoid high warranty costs.
- **Power Plants:** Redundancy (parallel configuration) of critical pumps ensures plant availability.
- **Software:** Reliability growth models track defect-removal curves analogous to infant mortality.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Master Reliability Identity $R(t) = e^{-\int_0^t \lambda(u)\, du}$

**Starting Point:** The hazard rate (failure rate) is defined as:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the identity $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ (this follows from $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ and $f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt}$):

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{-dR(t)/dt}{R(t)}$$

**Step 2:** Separate the variables (treat $R$ as a function of $t$):

$$\lambda(t)\, dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 3:** Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$:

$$\int_0^t \lambda(u)\, du = -\int_0^t \frac{dR(u)}{R(u)}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the right-hand side. Using the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$ (the product is perfectly reliable at the start):

$$\int_0^t \lambda(u)\, du = -\left[\ln R(t) - \ln R(0)\right] = -\ln R(t) - 0$$

**Step 5:** Exponentiate both sides to isolate $R(t)$:

$$R(t) = e^{-\int_0^t \lambda(u)\, du}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** This is the **Universal Reliability Equation**. It holds for *every* failure distribution (exponential, Weibull, normal, lognormal). For KTU exams, when a question gives you $\lambda(t)$, this formula is your starting point.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Exponential Reliability $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$

**Starting Point:** In the useful life region (Region II of bath tub), the failure rate is **constant**:

$$\lambda(t) = \lambda_0 = \text{constant}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute into the master identity:

$$R(t) = e^{-\int_0^t \lambda_0\, du}$$

**Step 2:** Pull the constant out of the integral:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda_0 \int_0^t du}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the simple integral:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda_0 [u]_0^t} = e^{-\lambda_0 (t - 0)}$$

**Step 4:** Final result:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda_0 t}$$

**Step 5:** MTTF (or MTBF for repairable systems):

$$MTTF = \int_0^\infty R(t)\, dt = \int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda_0 t}\, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda_0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memoryless Property of the Exponential Distribution:** A system that has already survived time $t_0$ has the same future reliability as a brand-new system.
> $$P(T > t_0 + t \mid T > t_0) = P(T > t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$
> This property is unique to the exponential distribution and is heavily tested in KTU exams.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Weibull Reliability $R(t) = e^{-(t/\alpha)^\beta}$

The Weibull distribution is the most general and widely used reliability model in engineering.

**Starting Point:** The Weibull failure rate function is given as:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta - 1}$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = **Scale parameter** (also called characteristic life $\eta$, with units of time)
- $\beta$ = **Shape parameter** (dimensionless)

**Step 1:** Substitute $\lambda(t)$ into the master reliability identity:

$$R(t) = e^{-\int_0^t \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{u}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta - 1} du}$$

**Step 2:** Pull constants outside the integral:

$$R(t) = e^{-\frac{\beta}{\alpha^\beta} \int_0^t u^{\beta - 1}\, du}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the integral using the power rule $\int u^{n}\, du = \dfrac{u^{n+1}}{n+1}$:

$$\int_0^t u^{\beta - 1}\, du = \left[\frac{u^{\beta}}{\beta}\right]_0^t = \frac{t^{\beta}}{\beta}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back:

$$R(t) = e^{-\frac{\beta}{\alpha^\beta} \cdot \frac{t^{\beta}}{\beta}} = e^{-\frac{t^\beta}{\alpha^\beta}}$$

**Step 5:** Compact form:

$$R(t) = e^{-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^\beta}$$

**Step 6:** Derive the corresponding PDF $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$:

$$f(t) = \left(\frac{\beta}{\alpha}\right)\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta - 1} e^{-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^\beta}$$

**Step 7:** Derive the MTTF using the gamma function identity $\int_0^\infty t^n e^{-t^\beta} dt = \dfrac{1}{\beta}\Gamma\!\left(\dfrac{n+1}{\beta}\right)$:

$$MTTF = \alpha \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shape Parameter $\beta$ — Engineering Interpretation (Frequently Tested):**
> - $\beta < 1$ → Decreasing failure rate → **Infant Mortality** (defectives)
> - $\beta = 1$ → Constant failure rate → **Random Failures** (Weibull reduces to exponential with $\lambda = 1/\alpha$)
> - $\beta > 1$ → Increasing failure rate → **Wear-Out** (aging)
> - $\beta = 3.5$ approximately → Bell-shaped (normal distribution analogue)
> The shape parameter $\beta$ is **the most powerful parameter** in reliability engineering because it directly maps to a region of the bath tub curve.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Series System Reliability

Consider $n$ components in **series** — the system fails if *any one* component fails.

**Step 1:** Let $R_i$ be the reliability of the $i$-th component.

**Step 2:** Define events $A_i$ = "$i$-th component survives to time $t$". The system survives if **all** components survive.

**Step 3:** Assuming **statistical independence** of component failures:

$$R_s(t) = P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap \ldots \cap A_n)$$

**Step 4:** For independent events, the joint probability equals the product of marginal probabilities:

$$R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Series system reliability is **always less than the reliability of the weakest component**. The weakest link dominates the system.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Parallel System Reliability

Consider $n$ components in **parallel** — the system works if *at least one* component works.

**Step 1:** The system fails only if **all** components fail.

**Step 2:** Probability of all components failing:

$$P(\text{all fail}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left[1 - R_i(t)\right]$$

**Step 3:** The system succeeds if it is NOT the case that all fail:

$$R_p(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left[1 - R_i(t)\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Parallel (redundant) system reliability is **always greater than the reliability of the strongest component**. This is the basis of all redundancy design.

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Combined Series-Parallel System

**Problem:** A system has two pumps in parallel, each with reliability $R = 0.9$. The parallel combination is in series with a control valve of reliability $R = 0.95$. Find the system reliability.

**Step 1:** Compute the parallel block reliability:

$$R_p = 1 - (1 - 0.9)(1 - 0.9) = 1 - (0.1)(0.1) = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$$

**Step 2:** Compute the overall series reliability:

$$R_s = R_p \times 0.95 = 0.99 \times 0.95 = 0.9405$$

**Answer:** System reliability = **0.9405** (or 94.05%).

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Failure Rate Behavior Comparison

| Distribution | $R(t)$ | $f(t)$ | $\lambda(t)$ | MTTF |
|--------------|--------|--------|--------------|------|
| **Exponential** | $e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ (constant) | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ |
| **Weibull** | $e^{-(t/\alpha)^\beta}$ | $\dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}\! e^{-(t/\alpha)^\beta}$ | $\dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | $\alpha\,\Gamma\!\left(1+\dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$ |
| **Rayleigh** ($\beta=2$) | $e^{-(t/\alpha)^2}$ | $\dfrac{2t}{\alpha^2} e^{-(t/\alpha)^2}$ | $\dfrac{2t}{\alpha^2}$ | $\alpha\sqrt{\pi}/2$ |

### 3.8 Life Testing — Theory and Approach

> [!NOTE]
> **Life Testing** is the experimental procedure used to obtain data on the time-to-failure of a product under specified conditions, in order to estimate reliability parameters ($\alpha$, $\beta$, $\lambda$, MTTF).

#### 3.8.1 Types of Life Testing

| Type | Description | Sample Size | Termination |
|------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Complete (Censored=0)** | All units tested until failure | Small (10–30) | When last unit fails |
| **Censored (Type I)** | Test stopped at pre-fixed time $T$ | Medium | Fixed time $T$ |
| **Censored (Type II)** | Test stopped at pre-fixed number of failures $r$ | Medium | When $r$-th unit fails |
| **Truncated** | Pre-determined maximum runtime cap | Variable | When test budget exhausted |

#### 3.8.2 Weibull Parameter Estimation — Graphical Method

The most common KTU-tested life testing method is the **Weibull probability paper graphical estimation**.

**Step 1:** Arrange failure times $t_i$ in ascending order.

**Step 2:** Assign median rank (plotting position) for each failure:

$$\text{Median Rank } F_i = \frac{i - 0.3}{n + 0.4}$$

Where $i$ is the failure order number and $n$ is the total sample size.

**Step 3:** Transform the Weibull CDF into a linear form. Take double logarithms:

$$F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\alpha)^\beta}$$

$$1 - F(t) = e^{-(t/\alpha)^\beta}$$

$$\ln[1 - F(t)] = -\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^\beta$$

$$\ln\!\left\{-\ln[1 - F(t)]\right\} = \beta \ln t - \beta \ln \alpha$$

**Step 4:** Recognize this as a straight line $Y = \beta X + C$ where:
- $Y = \ln\{-\ln[1 - F(t)]\}$
- $X = \ln t$
- Slope $= \beta$ (shape parameter)
- Intercept $= -\beta \ln \alpha$ (used to find scale parameter $\alpha$)

**Step 5:** Plot $Y$ vs $X$ on Weibull probability paper; the slope of the best-fit line gives $\beta$.

**Step 6:** From intercept, compute $\alpha$:

$$\alpha = e^{-C/\beta}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** If the plotted data points form an **upward-curving** line on Weibull paper, $\beta > 1$ (wear-out region). If **downward-curving**, $\beta < 1$ (infant mortality). If **straight line**, $\beta \approx 1$ (random/exponential). This is the most important diagnostic test in reliability engineering.

```mermaid
graph TD

    nodeE[Constant Low Failure Rate] --> nodeB
    nodeF[High Increasing Failure Rate] --> nodeC
    nodeG[Burn-in Testing, Quality Screening] --> nodeA
    nodeH[Preventive Maintenance, Redundancy] --> nodeB

    nodeJ[Manufacturing Defects, Material Flaws] --> nodeD
    nodeK[Random Accidents, Environmental Shocks] --> nodeE

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** Define the term **Reliability** of a system. State the difference between **Reliability** and **Maintainability**.

**Model Answer:**

Reliability is defined as the **probability that a system, component, or product will perform its intended function under stated operating conditions for a specified period of time**. It is denoted as $R(t) = P(T > t)$.

**Difference between Reliability and Maintainability:**

| Reliability | Maintainability |
|-------------|----------------|
| Probability that the system **will NOT fail** during the mission time. | Probability that a **failed system will be restored** to working condition in a given time. |
| Concerned with **failure prevention** (design, quality). | Concerned with **failure repair** (maintenance, logistics). |
| Measured in terms of MTTF / MTBF. | Measured in terms of MTTR (Mean Time To Repair). |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]** Sketch the **Bath Tub Curve** and label its three regions. State the dominant failure mechanism in each region.

**Model Answer:**

A labelled sketch of the Bath Tub Curve is described below:

- **Region I (Infant Mortality / Burn-in Phase):** $\lambda(t)$ is high but decreasing. Dominant failure mechanism — manufacturing defects, material flaws, workmanship errors.
- **Region II (Useful Life / Random Phase):** $\lambda(t)$ is approximately constant. Dominant failure mechanism — random, chance failures, environmental shocks, human errors.
- **Region III (Wear-out Phase):** $\lambda(t)$ increases sharply. Dominant failure mechanism — wear, fatigue, corrosion, aging of materials.

Engineering actions: Burn-in testing (Region I), preventive maintenance (Region II), and scheduled replacement/overhaul (Region III).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style, 7 Marks]**

> **(a) Explain in detail the three regions of the Bath Tub Curve. For each region, state the engineering actions taken to improve reliability.**

**Model Answer:**

The Bath Tub Curve is the classical graph of the instantaneous failure rate $\lambda(t)$ versus time $t$, exhibiting a shape resembling a bath tub cross-section. It is divided into three regions:

**Region I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in) Phase**
- **Time Range:** $0$ to $t_1$ (typically the first few weeks/months of operation).
- **Failure Rate Behavior:** $\lambda(t)$ is **high at $t=0$ and decreases monotonically** to a minimum at $t_1$.
- **Dominant Failures:** Defects introduced during manufacturing — substandard components, poor soldering, weak welds, material inclusions, inadequate assembly.
- **Probability Distribution:** Weibull with $\beta < 1$.
- **Engineering Action:** **Burn-in testing** — the product is operated under accelerated stress (heat, voltage, vibration) for a fixed period to eliminate weak units before customer delivery. Quality screening at end of production line.
- **Mathematical Model:** $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta - 1}$ with $\beta < 1$.

**Region II — Useful Life (Random Failure) Phase**
- **Time Range:** $t_1$ to $t_2$ (the major portion of operational life).
- **Failure Rate Behavior:** $\lambda(t) = \lambda_0$ — **constant and at its minimum value**.
- **Dominant Failures:** Random, unpredictable, stochastic — accidental overload, environmental shocks, operator errors, sudden electrical breakdown.
- **Probability Distribution:** Exponential (Weibull with $\beta = 1$).
- **Engineering Action:** **Preventive maintenance**, **redundancy design** (parallel components), and robust quality assurance. Use of MTBF data for spares planning.
- **Mathematical Model:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda_0 t}$, MTTF $= 1/\lambda_0$.

**Region III — Wear-Out Phase**
- **Time Range:** Beyond $t_2$ (end of operational life).
- **Failure Rate Behavior:** $\lambda(t)$ **increases rapidly** with time.
- **Dominant Failures:** Gradual degradation — wear, fatigue, corrosion, creep, oxidation, material aging.
- **Probability Distribution:** Weibull with $\beta > 1$ (often $\beta \approx 2$ to $3.5$).
- **Engineering Action:** **Scheduled replacement**, **overhaul**, **predictive maintenance** (vibration analysis, oil analysis), and **design retirement**. Choose replacement interval $T^* < t_2$ to avoid wear-out region.
- **Mathematical Model:** $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta - 1}$ with $\beta > 1$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often forget to mention the **engineering action** for each region. Merely drawing the curve is worth only 2-3 marks; explaining the cause + action for each region is the key to securing all 7 marks.

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style, 7 Marks]**

> **(b) For an exponential distribution with constant failure rate $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour, calculate: (i) Reliability at $t = 500$ hours, (ii) MTTF, and (iii) the probability that a unit which has survived 500 hours will survive an additional 300 hours.**

**Model Solution:**

Given: Constant failure rate $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour, exponential distribution.

**(i) Reliability at $t = 500$ hours:**

$$R(500) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.001 \times 500} = e^{-0.5}$$

$$R(500) = 0.6065 \text{ (or 60.65%)}$$

**(ii) MTTF:**

$$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.001} = 1000 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) Conditional probability of surviving additional 300 hours given 500 hours already survived:**

Using the memoryless property of the exponential distribution:

$$P(T > 500 + 300 \mid T > 500) = P(T > 300) = e^{-\lambda \times 300}$$

$$= e^{-0.001 \times 300} = e^{-0.3} = 0.7408 \text{ (or 74.08%)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In part (iii), many students incorrectly compute $P(T > 800) = e^{-0.8}$ and then divide by $P(T > 500)$. The **correct approach** uses the **memoryless property** directly, giving $P(T > 300)$ without division. Both methods give the **same answer** (since the exponential is memoryless), but the memoryless approach is the elegant one that earns full marks.

---

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style, 7 Marks]**

> **(a) With a neat block diagram, derive the reliability expression for a series system of $n$ components. A system consists of 3 components in series with reliabilities $R_1 = 0.95$, $R_2 = 0.92$, and $R_3 = 0.90$. Find the system reliability.**

**Model Answer:**

A series system fails if **any one** component fails. The system succeeds only if **all** components succeed.

**Derivation:** Let $A_i$ be the event that the $i$-th component survives up to time $t$, with $P(A_i) = R_i$. Assuming statistical independence:

$$R_s(t) = P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap \ldots \cap A_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(A_i) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $R_1 = 0.95$, $R_2 = 0.92$, $R_3 = 0.90$.

$$R_s = 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.90$$

$$R_s = 0.95 \times 0.828 = 0.7866$$

$$\boxed{R_s = 0.7866 \text{ (or 78.66%)}}$$

**Inference:** Even with each component having high reliability (>90%), the **system reliability drops to 78.66%** — illustrating the **weakest-link principle** in series systems.

---

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style, 7 Marks]**

> **(b) Explain the concept of parallel (redundant) system reliability. Two pumps are connected in parallel, each with reliability $R = 0.85$. If the parallel combination is in series with a control unit of reliability $R = 0.95$, compute the overall system reliability.**

**Model Answer:**

A parallel system works if **at least one** of the parallel components works. The system fails **only if all** components fail.

**Derivation:** Probability that all $n$ components fail simultaneously:

$$P(\text{all fail}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} [1 - R_i]$$

System reliability:

$$\boxed{R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} [1 - R_i]}$$

**Step 1: Compute parallel block reliability (two identical pumps, $R = 0.85$):**

$$R_p = 1 - (1 - 0.85)(1 - 0.85) = 1 - (0.15)(0.15) = 1 - 0.0225$$

$$R_p = 0.9775$$

**Step 2: Combine with the series control unit ($R = 0.95$):**

$$R_{sys} = R_p \times 0.95 = 0.9775 \times 0.95$$

$$\boxed{R_{sys} = 0.9286 \text{ (or 92.86%)}}$$

**Comparison:** Without parallel redundancy, the system reliability would be $0.85 \times 0.95 = 0.8075$. Parallel redundancy **improves the system reliability from 80.75% to 92.86%** — a remarkable enhancement.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often incorrectly multiply the two pump reliabilities ($0.85 \times 0.85 = 0.7225$) thinking pumps are in series. The correct approach is the **complement rule**: $1 - (1 - R)^2$. Always draw the block diagram first and identify whether the configuration is **series** or **parallel** before applying the formula.

---

**Q5(a). [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style, 7 Marks]**

> **(a) What is the Weibull distribution? Show that the Weibull failure rate can be constant, increasing, or decreasing depending on the shape parameter $\beta$.**

**Model Answer:**

The Weibull distribution is the most general and flexible reliability model. It is widely used in engineering because its shape parameter $\beta$ allows it to model **all three regions of the Bath Tub Curve**.

**Reliability Function:**

$$R(t) = e^{-(t/\alpha)^\beta}$$

**Failure Rate (Hazard Function):**

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta - 1}$$

Where $\alpha$ = scale parameter (characteristic life) and $\beta$ = shape parameter.

**Case Analysis:**

**Case 1: $\beta < 1$ (Infant Mortality Region)**

For $\beta < 1$, the exponent $(\beta - 1) < 0$, so $\lambda(t) \propto t^{(\beta-1)}$ is a **decreasing** function of $t$. This corresponds to early-life failures due to manufacturing defects. The reliability drops rapidly at first, then stabilizes.

*Example:* $\beta = 0.5$, then $\lambda(t) \propto 1/\sqrt{t}$, which decreases over time.

**Case 2: $\beta = 1$ (Useful Life / Random Failures)**

For $\beta = 1$:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \text{constant}$$

The Weibull reduces to the exponential distribution with $\lambda = 1/\alpha$. This corresponds to random failures in the useful life region.

*Example:* $\beta = 1$, $\alpha = 1000$ hours, then $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour.

**Case 3: $\beta > 1$ (Wear-Out Region)**

For $\beta > 1$, the exponent $(\beta - 1) > 0$, so $\lambda(t) \propto t^{(\beta-1)}$ is an **increasing** function of $t$. This corresponds to wear-out failures.

*Example:* $\beta = 2$, $\lambda(t) \propto t$ (linearly increasing). For $\beta = 3$, $\lambda(t) \propto t^2$ (parabolically increasing).

**Conclusion:** The Weibull distribution is a **unified model** for the entire Bath Tub Curve. By choosing $\beta$ appropriately, a reliability engineer can model any life-cycle phase.

---

**Q5(b). [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style, 7 Marks]**

> **(b) Ten identical bearings are life-tested. The failure times (in hours) are: 120, 250, 380, 460, 540, 610, 700, 820, 1010, 1200. Assuming a Weibull distribution, use the graphical method to estimate the shape parameter $\beta$ and scale parameter $\alpha$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Arrange data in ascending order (already sorted).**

**Step 2: Compute median rank $F_i = (i - 0.3)/(n + 0.4)$ for $n = 10$:**

| $i$ | $t_i$ (hrs) | $F_i = (i-0.3)/10.4$ | $1 - F_i$ | $-\ln(1-F_i)$ | $\ln t_i$ | $\ln[-\ln(1-F_i)]$ |
|----|-------------|----------------------|-----------|---------------|-----------|--------------------|
| 1  | 120         | 0.0673               | 0.9327    | 0.0697        | 4.7875    | $-2.6634$          |
| 2  | 250         | 0.1635               | 0.8365    | 0.1785        | 5.5215    | $-1.7228$          |
| 3  | 380         | 0.2596               | 0.7404    | 0.3006        | 5.9402    | $-1.2010$          |
| 4  | 460         | 0.3558               | 0.6442    | 0.4396        | 6.1312    | $-0.8217$          |
| 5  | 540         | 0.4519               | 0.5481    | 0.6012        | 6.2916    | $-0.5085$          |
| 6  | 610         | 0.5481               | 0.4519    | 0.7940        | 6.4135    | $-0.2306$          |
| 7  | 700         | 0.6442               | 0.3558    | 1.0331        | 6.5511    | $0.0326$           |
| 8  | 820         | 0.7404               | 0.2596    | 1.3471        | 6.7093    | $0.2979$           |
| 9  | 1010        | 0.8365               | 0.1635    | 1.8116        | 6.9177    | $0.5935$           |
| 10 | 1200        | 0.9327               | 0.0673    | 2.6990        | 7.0901    | $0.9928$           |

**Step 3: Plot $\ln[-\ln(1-F_i)]$ (Y) versus $\ln t_i$ (X).**

**Step 4: Determine the best-fit straight line. Using linear regression or selecting two extreme points:**

Slope (shape parameter):

$$\beta = \frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta X}$$

Using points $(4.7875, -2.6634)$ and $(7.0901, 0.9928)$:

$$\beta = \frac{0.9928 - (-2.6634)}{7.0901 - 4.7875} = \frac{3.6562}{2.3026} \approx 1.588$$

**Step 5: Compute the scale parameter $\alpha$.**

Intercept $= -2.6634$ (at $\ln t = 4.7875$):

$$C = Y - \beta X = -2.6634 - 1.588 \times 4.7875 = -2.6634 - 7.6025 = -10.2659$$

$$\alpha = e^{-C/\beta} = e^{10.2659/1.588} = e^{6.4648} \approx 640.0 \text{ hours}$$

**Final Estimates:**

$$\boxed{\beta \approx 1.59, \quad \alpha \approx 640 \text{ hours}}$$

**Inference:** Since $\beta > 1$, the bearings are exhibiting **wear-out behavior**. The reliability engineer should plan replacement or overhaul before the wear-out accelerates. The characteristic life is 640 hours, meaning 63.2% of bearings will fail by this time.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Many students confuse the median rank formula. Use $F_i = (i - 0.3)/(n + 0.4)$ for **median ranks** (Bernard approximation) or $F_i = i/(n+1)$ for **simple plotting positions**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects the **median rank method**.
> 2. Don't forget to take **two logarithms** — one for $t_i$ and one for $-\ln(1 - F_i)$ — before plotting. Skipping the $\ln[-\ln(1-F_i)]$ transformation is a common error that leads to a meaningless curve.
> 3. The Weibull paper itself already incorporates the double-log transformation, but if drawing manually on plain graph paper, you **must** perform the transformation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute KTU Revision**

- **Reliability Definition:** $R(t) = P(T > t)$ — probability of survival up to time $t$.
- **Four Reliability Functions:** $R(t)$, $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$, $f(t) = dF/dt$, $\lambda(t) = f(t)/R(t)$.
- **Master Reliability Identity:** $R(t) = e^{-\int_0^t \lambda(u)\, du}$ — universal and applies to **all** distributions.
- **Bath Tub Curve — Three Regions:**
  - **Region I (Infant Mortality):** Decreasing $\lambda(t)$ — manufacturing defects — burn-in to weed out weak units.
  - **Region II (Useful Life):** Constant $\lambda(t)$ — random failures — preventive maintenance and redundancy.
  - **Region III (Wear-Out):** Increasing $\lambda(t)$ — fatigue, corrosion, aging — scheduled replacement and overhaul.
- **Causes of Failures:** Overload, wear, fatigue (mechanical); corrosion, thermal degradation (chemical); operator error, environmental stress (human/external).
- **Exponential Distribution:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, MTTF $= 1/\lambda$, **memoryless** — survives $t_0$ ⟹ behaves like new.
- **Weibull Distribution:** $R(t) = e^{-(t/\alpha)^\beta}$ — the **most flexible** reliability model.
  - $\alpha$ = scale parameter (characteristic life, when 63.2% fail).
  - $\beta$ = shape parameter — $\beta<1$: infant mortality, $\beta=1$: random, $\beta>1$: wear-out.
  - MTTF $= \alpha \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$.
- **Series System:** $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ — fails if **any** component fails. Weakest link dominates.
- **Parallel (Redundant) System:** $R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ — fails only if **all** components fail. Reliability is **enhanced** beyond the strongest single component.
- **Combined Systems:** Break down into blocks — compute parallel blocks first, then multiply in series.
- **Life Testing:**
  - **Complete (uncensored):** Test until all $n$ units fail.
  - **Censored Type I:** Stop at fixed time $T$.
  - **Censored Type II:** Stop at $r$-th failure.
- **Weibull Parameter Estimation (Graphical):**
  - Use median rank $F_i = (i - 0.3)/(n + 0.4)$.
  - Linearize: $\ln[-\ln(1-F_i)] = \beta \ln t - \beta \ln \alpha$.
  - Slope of the line gives $\beta$; intercept gives $\alpha$.
- **Engineering Mapping of Weibull to BTC:** Always interpret $\beta$ values to identify the corresponding bath tub region before recommending a maintenance action.
- **Availability vs Reliability:** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ — combines reliability with maintainability.
- **Top KTU Mistakes to Avoid:** Forgetting units in MTTF; applying series formula to a parallel block (or vice versa); missing the memoryless property; not interpreting $\beta$ after Weibull estimation.
- **Most Frequently Asked Question Types:** (1) Draw & explain BTC regions; (2) derive $R(t)$ from $\lambda(t)$; (3) compute series/parallel/combined reliability; (4) exponential reliability + memoryless property; (5) Weibull parameter interpretation; (6) graphical life-test estimation.
